topics.title: Review of Bipolar Junction Transistor and Introduction to DC Biasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Review of Bipolar Junction Transistor and Introduction to DC Biasing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) — Formal Definition A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal, two-junction, current-controlled semiconductor device in which the conduction mechanism involves **both majority and minority charge carriers** (electrons and holes). The three terminals are the **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, and **Collector (C)**, and the device is constructed by sandwiching one type of semiconductor region between two regions of the opposite type, giving rise to two fundamental variants: - **NPN Transistor:** A p-type base sandwiched between two n-type regions (n–p–n). - **PNP Transistor:** An n-type base sandwiched between two p-type regions (p–n–p). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The BJT must be reviewed in terms of its *construction, physical operation, current relationships, terminal characteristics, and the three basic configurations (CB, CE, CC)*. A sound understanding of the **Q-point (Quiescent Operating Point)** is non-negotiable for all subsequent amplifier design modules. The BJT operates in one of four regions depending on the biasing of its two PN junctions (EBJ and CBJ): | Region | EBJ Bias | CBJ Bias | Functional Use | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **Active (Forward-Active)** | Forward | Reverse | Linear Amplification | | **Saturation** | Forward | Forward | Switch ON-State | | **Cutoff** | Reverse | Reverse | Switch OFF-State | | **Inverse-Active** | Reverse | Forward | Rarely used (analog mirror) | ## 1.2 The Hydraulic Analogy — Making the BJT Intuitive Imagine a **water pipeline** with a mechanical **valve** controlled by a small hand-wheel. The main water flow between the input pipe (Collector) and the output pipe (Emitter) cannot occur unless the small hand-wheel (Base) is slightly turned. A small twist of the hand-wheel — corresponding to a tiny base current $I_B$ — allows a much larger main flow from Collector to Emitter. This ratio of *output flow* to *control input* is analogous to the current gain $\beta$ of the BJT. - The **Emitter** is the *source* of charge carriers (analogous to the water reservoir). - The **Base** is the *thin control region* (the valve handle). - The **Collector** is the *output collector* that gathers the carriers injected by the emitter. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** The base is intentionally made *physically thin* and *lightly doped* compared to the emitter. This allows almost all carriers injected from the emitter to pass through to the collector, yielding a high $\alpha$ (typically 0.95 to 0.99 for a well-designed BJT). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (BJT) - **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$ - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ - **Electron Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ - **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Silicon:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ at 300 K - **Common-Base Current Gain (α):** Typically between **0.95 and 0.99** - **Common-Emitter Current Gain (β):** Typically between **50 and 300** for small-signal BJTs # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 BJT Construction Logic (Why Two Junctions?) The BJT contains two back-to-back PN junctions formed within a single crystal: 1. **Emitter–Base Junction (EBJ):** Controls the *injection* of carriers into the base. 2. **Collector–Base Junction (CBJ):** Controls the *collection* of these carriers from the base. The intentional asymmetry in doping and geometry is critical: - **Emitter** is **heavily doped** to maximize carrier injection efficiency. - **Base** is **thin and lightly doped** to minimize recombination in the base region. - **Collector** is **moderately doped** with a **large physical area** to dissipate heat and collect carriers efficiently. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Bipolar"?** Because current conduction involves *both* polarities of carriers (electrons and holes), as opposed to unipolar devices like JFETs or MOSFETs. ## 2.2 Current Relationships — The Algebraic Foundation By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** applied to the transistor: $$I_E = I_B + I_C$$ The transistor action is quantified by two dimensionless current ratios: - **Common-Base Current Gain (α):** Ratio of collector current to emitter current. $$\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E} \quad \text{(typically 0.95 to 0.99)}$$ - **Common-Emitter Current Gain (β):** Ratio of collector current to base current. $$\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B} \quad \text{(typically 50 to 300)}$$ ## 2.3 Deriving the Interrelation Between α and β Starting from $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ and using KCL: $$I_C = \alpha I_E = \alpha (I_B + I_C)$$ Solving for the ratio $I_C / I_B$: $$I_C - \alpha I_C = \alpha I_B \implies I_C(1-\alpha) = \alpha I_B$$ $$\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta+1}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **core, high-yield formulas** that the KTU Board examiner expects students to reproduce under examination conditions for Module 1. | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | KCL at the transistor | A or mA | | 2 | $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ | Common-base current gain | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\beta = I_C / I_B$ | Common-emitter current gain | Dimensionless | | 4 | $\beta = \alpha / (1-\alpha)$ | α-to-β conversion | Dimensionless | | 5 | $\alpha = \beta / (\beta+1)$ | β-to-α conversion | Dimensionless | | 6 | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | Collector current from base current | mA | | 7 | $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$ | Emitter current from base current | mA | | 8 | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7 \text{ V (Si)}$ | Emitter-base voltage drop (active) | V | | 9 | $I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C}$ | Saturation collector current | mA | | 10 | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$ | Q-point collector-emitter voltage | V | ## 2.5 The Three Basic BJT Configurations | Configuration | Input | Output | Current Gain | Voltage Gain | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Common-Base (CB)** | Emitter | Collector | $\alpha < 1$ | High | High-frequency, RF | | **Common-Emitter (CE)** | Base | Collector | $\beta$ (high) | High | General amplifier (most common) | | **Common-Collector (CC)** | Base | Emitter | $\beta+1$ | $\approx 1$ | Buffer / Impedance matching | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The **CE configuration** is the workhorse of analog electronics — used in nearly every audio preamplifier, radio receiver IF stage, and logic gate driver. The **CC (emitter follower)** is the standard *buffer* between a high-impedance sensor and a low-impedance load (e.g., op-amp input stage). ## 2.6 Introduction to DC Biasing — Establishing the Q-Point **DC Biasing** is the process of applying external DC voltages to the BJT to establish a specific **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** defined by the coordinates $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ on the DC load line. This is essential because: - The BJT is a **non-linear** device; without a stable DC operating point, the AC signal will be distorted (clipped, cut off, or saturated). - The Q-point determines the **mode of operation** (Class A, Class B, Class AB, etc.). - Temperature stability of the Q-point affects overall circuit reliability. ### The DC Load Line Equation For a simple fixed-bias CE circuit with $V_{CC}$ supply and collector resistor $R_C$: $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$ This is a linear equation between $I_C$ and $V_{CE}$ with two extreme endpoints: - **Cutoff Point** (where $I_C = 0$): $\quad V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} = V_{CC}$ - **Saturation Point** (where $V_{CE} \approx 0$): $\quad I_{C(\text{sat})} = V_{CC} / R_C$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From α and β to the Master Current Equations We begin with the *definitions* and *KCL* as previously introduced, but expand them into a complete algebraic proof. **Step 1 — Apply KCL at the transistor node:** $$I_E = I_B + I_C$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the definition of α:** Since $\alpha = I_C / I_E$, we have $I_C = \alpha I_E$. Therefore: $$I_E = I_B + \alpha I_E$$ **Step 3 — Isolate the base current $I_B$ in terms of $I_E$:** $$I_B = I_E - \alpha I_E = I_E (1 - \alpha)$$ **Step 4 — Express $I_C$ in terms of $I_B$:** From Step 3, $I_E = \dfrac{I_B}{1-\alpha}$. Substituting this into $I_C = \alpha I_E$: $$I_C = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{I_B}{1-\alpha} = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \cdot I_B$$ **Step 5 — Identify the coefficient as β:** $$\beta \equiv \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$$ This is the formal derivation of the canonical BJT current relationship. **Step 6 — Express $I_E$ in terms of $I_B$:** $$I_E = I_B + I_C = I_B + \beta I_B = I_B(1 + \beta)$$ **Final Result — The Master BJT Current Equations:** $$\boxed{I_C = \beta I_B} \quad ; \quad \boxed{I_E = (\beta+1) I_B} \quad ; \quad \boxed{I_C = \alpha I_E}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: The DC Load Line and Q-Point Coordinates Consider the canonical fixed-bias CE amplifier circuit. Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the output loop ($V_{CC} \to R_C \to \text{Transistor} \to \text{Ground}$): $$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0$$ Solving for $V_{CE}$: $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$ This is a straight line in the $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ plane with slope $-1/R_C$. **Finding the Q-Point (Intersection with the Device Characteristic):** The transistor's collector characteristic gives the relationship $I_C = \beta I_B$. Under DC conditions with a fixed base current $I_{BQ}$, the collector current is constant at $I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ}$. The corresponding collector-emitter voltage is: $$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - \beta I_{BQ} R_C$$ These two values $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ uniquely define the Q-point on the load line. **Worked Numerical Example:** > Given: $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $I_{BQ} = 30 \mu\text{A}$, $\beta = 100$. **Step A — Compute the collector current:** $$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = 100 \times 30 \, \mu\text{A} = 3 \text{ mA}$$ **Step B — Compute the collector-emitter voltage:** $$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 12 - (3 \text{ mA})(2.2 \text{ k}\Omega)$$ $$V_{CEQ} = 12 - 6.6 = 5.4 \text{ V}$$ **Step C — Verify the Q-point is in the active region:** For active region: $V_{CEQ} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ (typically 0.2 V) and $I_{BQ} > 0$. Since $5.4 \text{ V} \gg 0.2 \text{ V}$, the transistor is **biased in the active region**. ✓ **Step D — Compute the saturation and cutoff extremes for the load line:** $$I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \dfrac{12}{2.2 \text{ k}\Omega} \approx 5.45 \text{ mA}$$ $$V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} = V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$$ **Step E — Emitter current check:** $$I_{EQ} = I_{BQ} + I_{CQ} = 30 \, \mu\text{A} + 3 \text{ mA} \approx 3.03 \text{ mA} \approx I_{CQ}$$ This confirms the small error introduced by neglecting $I_B$ in high-$\beta$ designs. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — α-β Numerical Equivalence Table This table is critical for KTU problems that test *numerical conversion* between $\alpha$ and $\beta$. | $\alpha$ | $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ | $\alpha = \beta/(\beta+1)$ | Comment | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 0.900 | 9.00 | 0.900 | Low-gain transistor | | 0.950 | 19.0 | 0.950 | | | 0.980 | 49.0 | 0.980 | | | 0.990 | 99.0 | 0.990 | Typical small-signal BJT | | 0.995 | 199 | 0.995 | High-gain transistor | | 0.999 | 999 | 0.999 | Darlington-pair region | > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** As $\alpha \to 1$, $\beta \to \infty$. This is why high-$\alpha$ transistors exhibit *exponentially* increasing $\beta$, a fact exploited in **Darlington pairs** and **super-beta transistors** used in precision analog instrumentation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BJT Physical Symbol and Terminal Identification ```mermaid flowchart LR A["N REGION
EMITTER"] -->|"E"| B["P REGION
BASE - THIN"] B -->|"B arrow base current"| C["N REGION
COLLECTOR"] C -->|"C"| D["OUTPUT TERMINAL"] B -->|"base current IB"| E["INPUT CONTROL"] A -->|"IE direction"| F["CURRENT FLOW"] style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart abstracts the **NPN BJT** structure. The **emitter** (left, n-type) is heavily doped; the **base** (center, p-type) is thin and lightly doped; the **collector** (right, n-type) is large to dissipate thermal energy. The arrows indicate the conventional current direction (opposite to electron flow). ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) Biasing ```mermaid flowchart TD VCC["VCC Supply DC Voltage"] --> RC["Collector Resistor RC"] RC --> COL["Collector Terminal of BJT"] BJT["NPN BJT in Active Region"] --> COL BJT --> BASE["Base Terminal"] BJT --> EMI["Emitter Terminal"] EMI --> GND1["Ground Reference"] BASE --> RB["Base Bias Resistor RB"] RB --> VBIAS["DC Bias Voltage Source VBB"] VBIAS --> GND2["Ground Reference"] BASE -. "IB enters base" .-> BJT COL -. "IC leaves collector" .-> RC EMI -. "IE leaves emitter" .-> GND1 style VCC fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style VBIAS fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style RC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style RB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style BJT fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style BASE fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style COL fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style EMI fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style GND1 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style GND2 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Block-Flow Interpretation:** This represents the **fixed-bias CE configuration**. The DC supply $V_{CC}$ feeds the collector through $R_C$, while a separate DC bias source $V_{BB}$ drives the base through $R_B$. The currents $I_B$, $I_C$, and $I_E$ flow as marked. The *operating point* is the intersection of the line $I_C = \beta I_B$ (set by the base bias) with the DC load line $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$. ## 4.3 Functional Flow: Establishing the Q-Point ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Apply KCL at transistor node"] --> S2["IE = IB + IC"] S2 --> S3["Step 2: Identify current gains alpha and beta"] S3 --> S4["alpha = IC / IE and beta = IC / IB"] S4 --> S5["Step 3: Derive beta = alpha / 1 minus alpha"] S5 --> S6["Step 4: Compute ICQ = beta times IBQ"] S6 --> S7["Step 5: Apply KVL to collector loop"] S7 --> S8["VCEQ = VCC minus ICQ times RC"] S8 --> S9["Step 6: Verify Active Region condition"] S9 --> COND{"VCEQ greater than VCE sat
and IBQ greater than 0?"} COND -->|"Yes"| OK["Q-Point Valid
Active Region Operation"] COND -->|"No - VCE too low"| SAT["Saturation Region
Re-bias needed"] COND -->|"No - IBQ zero"| CUT["Cutoff Region
Re-bias needed"] style S1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S6 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S7 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S8 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S9 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style COND fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style OK fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SAT fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style CUT fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The decision diamond represents the **active-region verification step**. This is the step that KTU Board examiners often use to test whether a student truly understands the *physical meaning* of a Q-point — not merely the algebraic computation. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PBEET304]` > **Define current gain α in a BJT. Show that β = α/(1-α).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **common-base current gain α** is defined as the ratio of collector current to emitter current: $$\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}$$ Applying KCL: $I_E = I_B + I_C \implies I_B = I_E - I_C = I_E(1 - \alpha)$ **[1 Mark]** Then: $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B} = \dfrac{\alpha I_E}{I_E(1-\alpha)} = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ **[1 Mark]** --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PBEET304]` > **What is meant by the Q-point of a transistor? Why is it important to establish a stable Q-point?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Q-point (Quiescent Operating Point)** is the DC operating condition of a transistor defined by the coordinates $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ on the DC load line, with no input signal applied. **[1 Mark]** A stable Q-point ensures the transistor operates in the **active (linear) region** so that the AC signal is amplified faithfully without distortion. **[1 Mark]** Instability in the Q-point due to temperature variation or device parameter spread causes *thermal runaway*, *clipping*, or *cutoff*, degrading amplifier performance. **[1 Mark]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PBEET304]` (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Derive the relationship between α and β of a BJT. For a transistor with α = 0.98, calculate the base current required to produce a collector current of 50 mA. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** For the fixed-bias CE configuration with $V_{CC} = 20 \text{ V}$, $R_B = 500 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $\beta = 100$, determine the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$. Comment on the operating region. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — α-β Relationship and Base Current Calculation **Step 1:** Apply KCL: $I_E = I_B + I_C$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 2:** Using $\alpha = I_C / I_E \implies I_C = \alpha I_E$, substitute: $I_E = I_B + \alpha I_E$ **Step 3:** Solve for $I_B$: $I_B = I_E(1 - \alpha)$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 4:** $\beta = I_C / I_B = \dfrac{\alpha I_E}{I_E(1-\alpha)} = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ **[2 Marks]** **Numerical calculation (given α = 0.98, I_C = 50 mA):** **Step 5:** $\beta = \dfrac{0.98}{1 - 0.98} = \dfrac{0.98}{0.02} = 49$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 6:** $I_B = I_C / \beta = 50 \text{ mA} / 49 \approx 1.02 \text{ mA}$ **[1 Mark]** **Final Answer:** $I_B \approx 1.02 \text{ mA}$ **[1 Mark]** #### Part (b) — Q-Point Calculation for Fixed-Bias CE **Step 1:** Compute the base current using KVL on the input loop: $V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE} \implies I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \dfrac{20 - 0.7}{500 \text{ k}\Omega} = \dfrac{19.3}{500 \text{ k}\Omega}$ $I_B = 38.6 \, \mu\text{A}$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 2:** Compute the collector current: $I_{CQ} = \beta I_B = 100 \times 38.6 \, \mu\text{A} = 3.86 \text{ mA}$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 3:** Compute the collector-emitter voltage using KVL on the output loop: $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 20 - (3.86 \text{ mA})(2 \text{ k}\Omega) = 20 - 7.72 = 12.28 \text{ V}$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 4:** Verify the operating region: - $V_{CEQ} = 12.28 \text{ V} > V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0.2 \text{ V}$ ✓ - $I_{BQ} = 38.6 \, \mu\text{A} > 0$ ✓ - Therefore, the transistor operates in the **active (linear) region**. **[1 Mark]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to subtract $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ when computing $I_B$, leading to an inflated base current and a wrong Q-point. **Marks lost: 2–3 per part.** > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to verify the active-region condition. Even if the algebra is correct, the Q-point comment carries 1 mark. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using $\beta$ where the question gives $\alpha$ (or vice versa) without conversion. Always re-read the problem statement. --- > ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PBEET304]` (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Explain the construction and working of an NPN transistor with neat sketches. Compare CB, CE, and CC configurations. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A silicon BJT has $\beta = 150$. If the base current is $20 \, \mu\text{A}$ and the collector supply is $V_{CC} = 15 \text{ V}$ with $R_C = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, find $I_{CQ}$, $I_{EQ}$, $V_{CEQ}$, and verify whether the transistor is in saturation. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — NPN Construction, Working, and Configuration Comparison **Construction (2 Marks):** An NPN transistor consists of a **p-type base** sandwiched between two **n-type regions** — the **emitter** and **collector**. The emitter is **heavily doped**, the base is **thin and lightly doped**, and the collector is **moderately doped with larger area** for heat dissipation. **Working (3 Marks):** - In the **active region**, the emitter-base junction is **forward-biased** and the collector-base junction is **reverse-biased**. - Electrons from the n-type emitter are injected into the p-type base (majority carrier injection across EBJ). - Because the base is thin and lightly doped, almost all electrons (~99%) diffuse across the base without recombining and are swept into the collector by the reverse-biased CBJ. - The small (~1%) recombination current in the base constitutes the base current $I_B$. **Comparison Table (2 Marks):** | Parameter | CB | CE | CC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Current gain | $\alpha < 1$ | $\beta$ (high) | $\beta + 1$ (highest) | | Voltage gain | High | High | $\approx 1$ | | Input resistance | Very low | Moderate | High | | Output resistance | Very high | High | Low | | Phase inversion | No | Yes (180°) | No | #### Part (b) — Numerical Q-Point and Saturation Verification **Given:** $\beta = 150$, $I_B = 20 \, \mu\text{A}$, $V_{CC} = 15 \text{ V}$, $R_C = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, silicon BJT. **Step 1:** Compute $I_{CQ}$: $$I_{CQ} = \beta I_B = 150 \times 20 \, \mu\text{A} = 3 \text{ mA}$$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 2:** Compute $I_{EQ}$: $$I_{EQ} = I_B + I_{CQ} = 20 \, \mu\text{A} + 3 \text{ mA} = 3.02 \text{ mA}$$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 3:** Compute $V_{CEQ}$: $$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 15 - (3 \text{ mA})(1.5 \text{ k}\Omega) = 15 - 4.5 = 10.5 \text{ V}$$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 4:** Verify whether the transistor is in saturation: - **Saturation condition:** $V_{CEQ} \leq V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2 \text{ V}$ for silicon. - **Our result:** $V_{CEQ} = 10.5 \text{ V} \gg 0.2 \text{ V}$. **[1 Mark]** - **Conclusion:** The transistor is **NOT in saturation**; it is operating comfortably in the **active region** with substantial voltage headroom. **[1 Mark]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Reporting $I_E \approx I_C$ without computing the exact $(\beta + 1)I_B$ form. For a 7-mark problem, examiners expect the *exact* expression. Loss of 1 mark if sloppy. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "the transistor is saturated" because the problem asks to "verify". The actual verification requires numerical comparison with $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the units (mA, V, kΩ) in the final answer. **KTU 2024 valuation key: 0.5 mark deduction per missing unit.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 1 — BJT & DC Biasing Introduction** - **BJT = Bipolar Junction Transistor** → 3 terminals (E, B, C), 2 PN junctions, current-controlled device. - **Two types:** NPN and PNP. NPN is more common due to higher electron mobility in silicon. - **Active region biasing:** EBJ forward-biased, CBJ reverse-biased → required for linear amplification. - **KCL foundation:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ — the single most-tested identity in KTU Module 1. - **α and β are dimensionless gains** with α < 1 always, and β >> 1 (typically 50–300). - **Master conversion identities:** $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ and $\alpha = \beta/(\beta+1)$ — must be memorized cold. - **Silicon BJT:** $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$; $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2 \text{ V}$. - **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature (300 K). - **Q-point coordinates:** $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ where $I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ}$ and $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$. - **DC load line:** Linear relation $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ with endpoints at $(I_{C(\text{sat})}, V_{CC}/R_C)$ and $(0, V_{CC})$. - **Active-region verification:** $V_{CEQ} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ AND $I_{BQ} > 0$. - **CE configuration is the most-used** for amplification; CC for buffering; CB for high-frequency RF. - **Why bias the BJT?** Without a stable Q-point, the transistor cannot linearly reproduce AC signals — clipping and distortion dominate. - **Emitter is heavily doped** → efficient carrier injection. **Base is thin + lightly doped** → minimal recombination. **Collector has large area** → heat dissipation and carrier collection. - **Q-point stability is affected by:** Temperature (β increases with T), $I_{CBO}$ (doubles every 10°C), and $V_{BE}$ (decreases ~2 mV/°C). Subsequent modules (Module 2) will address bias stabilization using emitter resistor and voltage-divider bias.

topics.title: Base Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Base Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **BASE BIAS (FIXED BIAS) — KTU 2024 OFFICIAL TERMINOLOGY**
> *Base Bias*, also known as *Fixed Bias* or *Base-Resistor Bias*, is the simplest BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor) biasing configuration in which a single resistor $R_B$ is connected between the DC supply $V_{CC}$ and the base terminal of the transistor. In this arrangement, the base current $I_B$ is **fixed (predetermined)** by the supply voltage and the base resistor alone, independent of the collector current $I_C$ or the transistor's current gain $\beta$.

The circuit establishes the **Q-point (Quiescent Operating Point)** defined by the coordinates $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ in the DC load line. The collector resistor $R_C$ is the only other resistive element, providing the voltage drop that determines $V_{CE}$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Single-Knob Faucet"**
> Imagine a water tap with **only one knob** controlling flow to a sprinkler. There is no pressure regulator, no feedback sensor, no auto-adjustment. If the water pressure upstream changes (analogous to $V_{CC}$ variation), or if the nozzle wears out and water gushes harder (analogous to a high-$\beta$ transistor replacing a low-$\beta$ one), the spray pattern changes dramatically and unpredictably. The lawn gets either over-watered or under-watered — there is no self-correction. This is exactly how a Base-Biased transistor behaves: it is **simple to design**, but it has **zero thermal and parameter stability**. Any shift in temperature (causing $I_{CO}$ leakage to grow) or in $\beta$ causes the Q-point to drift aggressively, risking thermal runaway and saturation.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Device Constants

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon Base–Emitter Voltage | $V_{BE}$ | **0.7 V** | Volts |
| Germanium Base–Emitter Voltage | $V_{BE}$ | **0.3 V** | Volts |
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T$ | **25.85 mV** (at 300 K) | Volts |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Absolute Temperature | $T$ | **300** (room) | Kelvin |
| Electronic Charge | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | Coulombs |
| Thermal Runaway Threshold | $V_{CE}$ | $\leq \mathbf{V_{CC}/2}$ (safe region) | Volts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step**

1. **Step 1 — DC Supply Activation:** The supply $V_{CC}$ energizes the circuit. Current flows from $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow \text{Base–Emitter Junction (forward-biased)} \rightarrow \text{Ground}$.
2. **Step 2 — Base Current Determination:** Applying KVL to the Base–Emitter loop gives a **fixed** $I_B$ (hence the name "Fixed Bias"). No feedback path exists, so $I_B$ is decoupled from $I_C$.
3. **Step 3 — Collector Current Calculation:** The transistor amplifies $I_B$ by a factor of $\beta_{DC}$ (or $h_{FE}$), yielding $I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B$.
4. **Step 4 — Collector–Emitter Voltage:** KVL around the collector loop gives the output DC voltage $V_{CE}$.
5. **Step 5 — Q-Point Locus:** The Q-point sits on the DC load line. For *proper Class-A amplification*, the KTU-recommended ideal position is $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ (midpoint) and $I_{CQ} = I_{C(sat)}/2$ for **maximum symmetrical swing**.
6. **Step 6 — Stability Check:** Since $I_B$ is independent of $I_C$, any drift in $\beta$ (due to temperature, replacement, or batch variation) directly multiplies into $I_C$, causing **severe Q-point instability**. The stability factor is therefore the **highest (worst) among all biasing techniques**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU Board Exam Ready**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | A (mA typical) | $V_{CC} \gg V_{BE}$ for $I_B \approx V_{CC}/R_B$ |
| 2 | Collector Current | $I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B$ | A | Assumes Active region |
| 3 | Collector–Emitter Voltage | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C \cdot R_C$ | V | $0 \leq V_{CE} \leq V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | Saturation Current | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ | A | When $V_{CE} = 0$ |
| 5 | Cutoff Voltage | $V_{CE(off)} = V_{CC}$ | V | When $I_C = 0$ |
| 6 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | W | Must be $\leq P_{D(max)}$ |
| 7 | Stability Factor (S) | $S = 1 + \beta$ | dimensionless | Worst case — no thermal compensation |
| 8 | Stability Factor General | $S = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{d I_B}{d I_C}\right)}$ | dimensionless | For base bias, $dI_B/dI_C = 0$ |
| 9 | Saturated Base Resistor | $R_{B(sat)} = \dfrac{\beta \cdot (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{I_{C(sat)}}$ | $\Omega$ | For saturation mode design |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is Base Bias Used?**
> - **Switching applications** (digital logic inverters, relay drivers): Q-point is intentionally driven between cutoff and saturation, so stability is irrelevant.
> - **Educational labs and textbook examples**: Easiest circuit to teach KVL, KCL, and the concept of Q-point.
> - **Low-cost disposable consumer toys**: Where $\beta$ consistency within a batch is acceptable.
> - **NOT used** in precision analog amplifiers (audio, instrumentation, RF) — replaced by **Voltage-Divider Bias** or **Emitter Bias** for thermal stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base Current $I_B$

**Starting Point:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the Base–Emitter input loop (loop containing $V_{CC}$, $R_B$, and the B-E junction).

**Step (a):** Traverse the loop in the direction of conventional current $I_B$. The voltage rise across the source is $+V_{CC}$.

**Step (b):** The voltage drop across resistor $R_B$ is $-I_B \cdot R_B$ (Ohm's Law).

**Step (c):** The voltage drop across the forward-biased Base–Emitter junction is $-V_{BE}$ (≈ 0.7 V for silicon).

**Step (d):** Equating sum of voltages to zero (KVL closed loop):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} - I_B \cdot R_B - V_{BE} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore I_B \cdot R_B &= V_{CC} - V_{BE} \\[4pt]
\therefore I_B &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Rearranging the KVL equation algebraically isolates $I_B$ on the left side. The result shows $I_B$ depends only on $V_{CC}$, $V_{BE}$, and $R_B$ — it is **completely independent of $I_C$ or $\beta$**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Collector Current $I_C$

**Starting Point:** Fundamental BJT current relation in the **Active Region**.

**Step (a):** The transistor's amplifying action gives:

$$
I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B
$$

**Step (b):** Substituting the expression for $I_B$ from Derivation 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta_{DC} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}\right) \\[4pt]
I_C &= \frac{\beta_{DC} \cdot (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This final form reveals the **critical weakness** of base bias — the collector current is **directly proportional to $\beta$**. A transistor with twice the $\beta$ will have twice the $I_C$, shifting the Q-point drastically. This is why base bias has poor stability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Collector–Emitter Voltage $V_{CE}$

**Starting Point:** Apply KVL around the **output loop** (loop containing $V_{CC}$, $R_C$, and the C-E terminals).

**Step (a):** The voltage rise is $+V_{CC}$.

**Step (b):** The voltage drop across $R_C$ is $-I_C \cdot R_C$.

**Step (c):** The voltage drop across the C-E terminals is $-V_{CE}$.

**Step (d):** Applying KVL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} - I_C \cdot R_C - V_{CE} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C \cdot R_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (e):** Substituting $I_C$ from Derivation 2:

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - \frac{\beta_{DC} \cdot R_C \cdot (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This compact form is the KTU Board-exam gold standard. Given any 4 of the 5 variables ($V_{CC}, R_B, R_C, \beta, V_{BE}$), the fifth and the Q-point can be computed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stability Factor $S$

**Starting Point:** The general stability factor definition from KTU syllabus:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg|_{V_{BE}, \beta \text{ constant}}
$$

For base bias, using the equivalent form:

$$
S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{d I_B}{d I_C}\right)}
$$

**Step (a):** From $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$, we observe that $I_B$ is **independent of $I_C$**.

**Step (b):** Therefore:

$$
\frac{d I_B}{d I_C} = 0
$$

**Step (c):** Substituting into the general formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \cdot (0)} \\[4pt]
S &= \frac{1 + \beta}{1} \\[4pt]
S &= 1 + \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since a typical BJT has $\beta = 100$, the stability factor is $S = 101$. This is **extremely high**, meaning a 1% change in leakage current $I_{CO}$ causes a 101% shift in $I_C$ — the hallmark of poor thermal stability. KTU examiners specifically test whether students recognize this weakness.

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_B = 400$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (Silicon).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{12 - 0.7}{400 \times 10^3} = \frac{11.3}{400{,}000} = 28.25~\mu A \\[4pt]
I_C &= 100 \times 28.25~\mu A = 2.825~mA \\[4pt]
V_{CE} &= 12 - (2.825 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{3}) = 12 - 5.65 = 6.35~V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q-Point:** $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (2.825~\text{mA}, 6.35~\text{V})$ — nicely centered near $V_{CC}/2 = 6$ V ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Base Bias Configuration

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["VCC DC Supply +12V"] -->|Positive Terminal| B["Node X Top Rail"]
    B -->|Current IB flows down| C["RB Base Resistor 400 kilo ohm"]
    C -->|Junction Node B| D["Base Terminal of NPN BJT Q1"]
    D -->|Transistor Action| E["Collector Terminal of NPN BJT Q1"]
    E -->|Current IC flows down| F["RC Collector Resistor 2 kilo ohm"]
    F --> G["Node Y Ground Rail 0V"]
    D -->|VBE 0.7V drop| H["Emitter Terminal of NPN BJT Q1"]
    H --> G
    I["Beta DC Current Gain 100"] -.->|Multiplies IB to IC| E
    J["Q Point Coordinates"] -.->|Derived from loop equations| K["ICQ 2.825 mA"]
    J --> L["VCEQ 6.35 V"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Q-Point Determination Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Input VCC and RB"] --> nodeB["Compute IB using KVL Base Loop"]
    nodeB["Compute IB using KVL Base Loop"] --> nodeC["Multiply by Beta to get IC"]
    nodeC["Multiply by Beta to get IC"] --> nodeD["Apply KVL Collector Loop"]
    nodeD["Apply KVL Collector Loop"] --> nodeE["Solve for VCE"]
    nodeE["Solve for VCE"] --> nodeF["Plot Q Point on DC Load Line"]
    nodeF["Plot Q Point on DC Load Line"] --> nodeG{"Is VCEQ between 0 and VCC?"}
    nodeG{"Is VCEQ between 0 and VCC?"} -->|Yes| nodeH["Active Region Confirmed Valid Design"]
    nodeG{"Is VCEQ between 0 and VCC?"} -->|No VCE less than 0| nodeI["Saturation Region Design Flaw"]
    nodeG{"Is VCEQ between 0 and VCC?"} -->|No VCE equals VCC| nodeJ["Cutoff Region Design Flaw"]
    nodeH["Active Region Confirmed Valid Design"] --> nodeK["Stability Check S equals 1 plus Beta"]
    nodeK["Stability Check S equals 1 plus Beta"] --> nodeL{"S less than 10 Acceptable?"}
    nodeL{"S less than 10 Acceptable?"} -->|No S equals 101 for Beta 100| nodeM["Replace with Voltage Divider Bias"]
    nodeL{"S less than 10 Acceptable?"} -->|Yes for switching apps| nodeN["Base Bias Acceptable Continue"]
```

## 4.3 Annotated Q-Point on DC Load Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    ptA["Origin 0,0"] -->|IC axis vertical| ptB["IC sat equals VCC by RC"]
    ptB --> ptC["Point A VCC 0 on horizontal axis"]
    ptC -->|VCE axis horizontal| ptD["Midpoint VCC by 2 VCC by 2R"]
    ptD --> ptE["Q Point ICQ 2.825 mA VCEQ 6.35 V"]
    ptE --> ptF["Cutoff Point 0 VCC"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Base Bias. List two of its major drawbacks.**

**Model Answer:**
> *Base bias* is a BJT biasing scheme in which a single resistor $R_B$ connects the supply $V_{CC}$ to the base terminal, fixing the base current as $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$.
>
> **Drawbacks:**
> 1. **Poor Q-point stability:** $I_C$ is directly proportional to $\beta$, so any variation in $\beta$ shifts the operating point significantly.
> 2. **High thermal sensitivity:** Stability factor $S = 1 + \beta$ is very large, making the circuit vulnerable to leakage current $I_{CO}$ growth with temperature (risk of thermal runaway).

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the stability factor $S = 1 + \beta$ for a base-biased transistor?**

**Model Answer:**
> The general stability factor is $S = (1 + \beta)/[1 - \beta \cdot (dI_B/dI_C)]$. In base bias, $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$, which is **independent** of $I_C$. Hence $dI_B/dI_C = 0$, and the formula reduces to $S = (1+\beta)/1 = 1+\beta$. This is a very high value, confirming poor stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Two full 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Students must attempt ONE of the two. Below are TWO independent alternatives.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**For a base-biased BJT circuit with $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $R_B = 500$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 3$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V:**

#### (a) Calculate $I_B$, $I_C$, and $V_{CE}$. Comment on the position of the Q-point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Base Current:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{15 - 0.7}{500 \times 10^3} = \frac{14.3}{500{,}000} = 28.6~\mu A
$$

**Step 2 — Collector Current:**

$$
I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 120 \times 28.6~\mu A = 3.432~mA
$$

**Step 3 — Collector–Emitter Voltage:**

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C \cdot R_C = 15 - (3.432 \times 10^{-3})(3 \times 10^{3}) = 15 - 10.296 = 4.704~V
$$

**Q-Point:** $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (3.432~\text{mA},~4.704~\text{V})$.

**Comment:** $V_{CEQ} = 4.704$ V is **less than $V_{CC}/2 = 7.5$ V**, indicating the Q-point lies below the load-line midpoint. The circuit is biased closer to saturation, providing slightly larger negative-signal swing headroom but reduced positive-swing headroom.

#### (b) Compute the stability factor $S$ and explain its significance. What is the new Q-point if $\beta$ changes to 200 due to temperature rise? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stability Factor:**

$$
S = 1 + \beta = 1 + 120 = 121
$$

**Significance:** A 1% rise in leakage current $I_{CO}$ causes a 121% rise in $I_C$ — catastrophic thermal instability.

**Step 2 — New $I_C$ at $\beta = 200$:**

$$
I_{C(new)} = 200 \times 28.6~\mu A = 5.72~mA
$$

**Step 3 — New $V_{CE}$:**

$$
V_{CE(new)} = 15 - (5.72 \times 10^{-3})(3 \times 10^{3}) = 15 - 17.16 = -2.16~V
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** A **negative** $V_{CE}$ is physically impossible — the transistor is driven into **deep saturation**. The actual $V_{CE}$ saturates at approximately **0.2 V**, and the collector current is clamped near $I_{C(sat)} = V_{CC}/R_C = 5$ mA. This proves the **inherent weakness** of base bias in real circuits.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A silicon NPN transistor ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V) is base-biased with $V_{CC} = 20$ V, $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$, and is required to operate at $I_{CQ} = 5$ mA.**

#### (a) Design the circuit — find $R_B$ and verify the Q-point position. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required Base Current:**

$$
I_B = \frac{I_{CQ}}{\beta} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 50~\mu A
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_B$:**

$$
R_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{20 - 0.7}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{19.3}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 386~k\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Verify $V_{CEQ}$:**

$$
V_{CE} = 20 - (5 \times 10^{-3})(2.2 \times 10^{3}) = 20 - 11 = 9~V
$$

**Step 4 — Q-Point Verification:**

$$
V_{CEQ} = 9~V, \quad \frac{V_{CC}}{2} = 10~V
$$

The Q-point is at $(5~\text{mA}, 9~\text{V})$, **slightly below the midpoint** — acceptable for amplifier operation with minor asymmetry in swing range.

**Step 5 — Saturation Check:**

$$
I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \frac{20}{2.2 \times 10^{3}} = 9.09~mA
$$

Since $I_{CQ} = 5$ mA $< 9.09$ mA, the transistor is confirmed in the **Active Region** ✓

#### (b) Derive the stability factor formula and explain why base bias is unsuitable for analog amplifiers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (Recap from Section 3.4):**

$$
S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \cdot (dI_B/dI_C)} = \frac{1 + 100}{1 - 0} = 101
$$

**Reasons for Unsuitability in Analog Amplifiers:**

1. **$\beta$ Dependence:** $I_C \propto \beta$ means that manufacturing spread in $\beta$ (e.g., 80 to 320 for the same part number) produces wildly different Q-points across production batches.

2. **Thermal Runaway:** As temperature rises, $\beta$ increases, $I_C$ increases, junction temperature increases further, $\beta$ rises again — a positive feedback loop leading to device destruction.

3. **No DC Negative Feedback:** Unlike emitter bias (which adds $R_E$ to provide degenerative feedback), base bias has no mechanism to oppose drift.

4. **Q-Point Migration:** Even with identical components, the operating point drifts during circuit operation, causing gain distortion, clipping asymmetry, and reduced linearity.

5. **KTU Verdict:** Voltage-divider bias ($S \approx 1$ to 10) is the universally preferred alternative for amplifier design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{BE}$ term:** Many students write $I_B = V_{CC}/R_B$ and lose 1 mark. Always retain the 0.7 V drop.
> 2. **Sign error in KVL:** Writing $V_{CE} = I_C R_C - V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Stability factor misuse:** Stating $S = \beta$ instead of $S = 1 + \beta$ — partial credit loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical problems.
> 5. **Drawing circuit without labeling $V_{CC}$ polarity:** Lose 0.5 mark in circuit-diagram questions.
> 6. **Confusing $I_C = \beta I_B$ with $I_E = \beta I_B$:** The correct relation is $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B$.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ⭐ **Base Bias = Fixed Bias:** Simplest BJT biasing; $I_B$ set by $R_B$ alone.
- ⭐ **Master Equations:**
  $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$
  $I_C = \beta I_B$
  $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$
- ⭐ **Stability Factor:** $S = 1 + \beta$ — **WORST** among all biasing methods.
- ⭐ **Ideal Q-Point:** $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ for maximum symmetrical Class-A swing.
- ⭐ **Saturation Current:** $I_{C(sat)} = V_{CC}/R_C$ — upper limit of load line.
- ⭐ **Cutoff Voltage:** $V_{CE(off)} = V_{CC}$ — right end of load line.
- ⭐ **Silicon $V_{BE}$:** 0.7 V | **Germanium $V_{BE}$:** 0.3 V.
- ⭐ **Thermal Runaway Risk:** Always present in base bias; avoided in emitter-bias and voltage-divider bias.
- ⭐ **Best Application:** Switching circuits and educational demonstrations — **NOT** precision amplifiers.
- ⭐ **KTU Weightage:** Expect 3-mark definition + 7-mark numerical (Q-point design) + 7-mark stability derivation = 14 marks minimum in Module 1.
- ⭐ **Negative $V_{CE}$ Means Saturation:** If your calculation yields $V_{CE} < 0$, the design has driven the BJT into saturation; report $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Divider Bias and Common Emitter Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Divider Bias and Common Emitter Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Divider Bias (VDB) — Formal Definition

**Voltage Divider Bias (VDB)** is a transistor biasing arrangement in which the base voltage $V_B$ is fixed by a resistive voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (between $V_{CC}$ and base) and $R_2$ (between base and ground). It is the **most widely used biasing topology** in discrete and integrated BJT amplifier design because it achieves an almost **β-independent Q-point (quiescent operating point)**, giving excellent thermal and device-parameter stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Voltage Divider Bias is explicitly listed under *Module 1 – Biasing of BJT*. The examiner expects: (i) DC analysis using Thevenin's theorem, (ii) Q-point derivation, and (iii) Stability factor $S$ calculation.

## 1.2 Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier — Formal Definition

A **Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is a BJT small-signal amplifier configuration in which the **emitter terminal is common to both the input and output ports** (typically grounded through a bypass capacitor $C_E$). It is the **workhorse configuration of analog electronics** because it simultaneously provides **voltage gain, current gain, and hence significant power gain**, along with a useful **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CE amplifier is always studied in two stages:
> 1. **DC Analysis** (using VDB to fix the Q-point).
> 2. **AC / Small-Signal Analysis** (using the r-e or hybrid-π model to find $A_v$, $A_i$, $Z_{in}$, $Z_{out}$).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Making Sense of the Concepts

### Analogy for Voltage Divider Bias: The "Pressure Regulator"

Imagine a water pipeline where you want a **steady, predictable trickle** of water regardless of small fluctuations in the main supply pressure. A **voltage divider** is exactly that — a *passive pressure regulator*. The two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ "divide" the supply pressure $V_{CC}$ into a smaller, well-defined base voltage $V_B$. The transistor then acts as a *valve* that uses this regulated $V_B$ to deliver a steady collector current $I_C$. Even if the transistor's internal gain $\beta$ varies from device to device, the current stays nearly constant — just as a good regulator keeps water flow steady.

### Analogy for the Common Emitter Amplifier: The "Megaphone"

Think of a CE amplifier as a **megaphone**:

- The **microphone (input)** captures a small voice signal — this is your $V_{in}$.
- The **battery inside the megaphone** provides the DC operating power — this is the **bias network (VDB)**.
- The **horn (output)** produces a much louder version of your voice — this is the **amplified $V_{out}$**.
- Importantly, the horn faces the *opposite direction* of your lips (you speak into the back) — this represents the **180° phase reversal** characteristic of the CE amplifier.

The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is like opening a *shortcut valve* at the emitter, allowing AC signals to bypass $R_E$ (which only serves to stabilize DC). This greatly boosts the AC gain while preserving DC stability.

## 1.4 Standard Circuit Topology (Textual Description)

The complete VDB-fed CE amplifier consists of:

| Element | Function |
|---|---|
| $R_1, R_2$ | Form voltage divider → fix base bias |
| $R_C$ | Collector load resistor → converts $i_c$ to $v_{out}$ |
| $R_E$ | Emitter resistor → DC negative feedback for stability |
| $C_{in}$ | Input coupling capacitor → blocks DC, passes AC |
| $C_{out}$ | Output coupling capacitor → blocks DC, passes AC |
| $C_E$ | Emitter bypass capacitor → shorts $R_E$ for AC signals |
| $V_{CC}$ | DC supply (**typically +12 V or +15 V** in KTU problems) |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard silicon BJT assumption used throughout KTU papers: $V_{BE(on)} = \mathbf{0.7\ V}$, and $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at room temperature (300 K).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Voltage Divider Bias — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Voltage Divider Action:** With negligible base current assumption, $R_1$ and $R_2$ simply divide $V_{CC}$:
   $$V_B \approx V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
2. **Emitter Voltage Fixing:** Since $V_{BE}$ is roughly constant at **0.7 V**, the emitter sits at:
   $$V_E = V_B - V_{BE} \approx V_B - 0.7$$
3. **Emitter Current Set by $R_E$:** $R_E$ converts $V_E$ into a predictable $I_E$:
   $$I_E = \frac{V_E}{R_E}$$
4. **Collector Current Approximation:** Because $I_C \approx I_E$ (since $\alpha \to 1$):
   $$I_C \approx I_E$$
5. **Self-Correcting Negative Feedback:** If $I_C$ tries to increase (e.g., due to temperature rise), $V_E$ rises, $V_{BE}$ drops, which *reduces* $I_B$ and pulls $I_C$ back down. This is the **thermal stability mechanism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Thevenin equivalent is used when base current $I_B$ is **not** negligible (i.e., for low $\beta$ transistors or low $R_E$ values). Always check the problem's context.

## 2.2 Thevenin Equivalent at the Base Node

The divider network $(R_1, R_2)$ is replaced by:

$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$R_{TH} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Now the KVL around the base-emitter loop gives:

$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta)R_E}$$

This is the **exact** base current, and from it:

$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$$

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$$

## 2.3 Stability Factor $S$ — The Key KTU Metric

The **Stability Factor** $S$ quantifies how much the collector current shifts with the reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$ (which doubles every **10 °C** rise in temperature):

$$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- For **ideal stability**, $S \to 1$ (i.e., $I_C$ is completely independent of $\beta$ and $I_{CO}$).
- This happens when $\beta \cdot R_E \gg (R_E + R_{TH})$.
- VDB is the **only practical BJT biasing scheme** that achieves $S \approx 1$.

## 2.4 CE Amplifier — Small-Signal AC Analysis (r-e Model)

For mid-band frequencies (where all coupling/bypass capacitors act as shorts and parasitic capacitances as opens), the transistor is replaced by its **simplified r-e model**:

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E\ (\text{in mA})}\ \Omega$$

**Key Small-Signal Parameters (with $C_E$ bypassing $R_E$):**

| Parameter | Expression |
|---|---|
| Voltage Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{-R_C \,\|\, R_L}{r_e}$ |
| Voltage Gain (no load) | $A_v = \dfrac{-R_C}{r_e}$ |
| Input Impedance (looking into base) | $Z_b = \beta \cdot r_e$ |
| Input Impedance (of stage) | $Z_{in} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 \,\|\, (\beta r_e)$ |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out} = R_C$ |
| Current Gain | $A_i \approx \beta$ |
| Power Gain | $A_p = \vert A_v \cdot A_i \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in $A_v$ signifies the **180° phase reversal** — output is inverted relative to input. KTU examiners frequently test this sign convention.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of these formulas is mandatory for KTU Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | Thevenin voltage of divider |
| 2 | $R_{TH} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2$ | Thevenin resistance of divider |
| 3 | $I_B = (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / (R_{TH} + (1+\beta)R_E)$ | Exact base current |
| 4 | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B \approx I_E$ | Collector current |
| 5 | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ | Quiescent collector-emitter voltage |
| 6 | $S = (1+\beta) / \left[1 + \beta R_E / (R_E + R_{TH})\right]$ | Stability factor |
| 7 | $r_e = 26\text{ mV} / I_E\ (\text{mA})$ | Emitter dynamic resistance |
| 8 | $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ (no load) | Mid-band voltage gain |
| 9 | $A_v = -(R_C \,\|\, R_L) / r_e$ (with load) | Mid-band voltage gain with load |
| 10 | $Z_{in} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 \,\|\, \beta r_e$ | Stage input impedance |
| 11 | $Z_{out} = R_C$ | Stage output impedance |
| 12 | $A_i = \beta$ | Mid-band current gain |
| 13 | $V_{CC} \ge V_{CE} + I_C(R_C + R_E)$ | Saturation avoidance condition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In formula rows, do not confuse **emitter current $I_E$** with **collector current $I_C$** in $r_e$. The KTU standard is $r_e = 26\text{ mV} / I_E$. Using $I_C$ gives a small error but is sometimes accepted if clearly stated.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VDB** is used in virtually every **audio preamplifier, op-amp input stage, and RF amplifier** because of its thermal stability.
- The **CE amplifier** forms the gain stage in **microphone preamps, radio receivers, public-address systems, and instrumentation amplifiers**.
- Understanding CE gain ($A_v \approx -R_C/r_e$) directly translates to designing **transimpedance amplifiers, switching circuits, and logic inverters (TTL/NOT gates)**.
- The concept of **Q-point stability** is foundational to **mixed-signal IC design**, where transistors must operate predictably across temperature and process variations.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Exact Q-Point Using Thevenin Equivalent

**Statement:** Derive expressions for $I_B$, $I_C$, and $V_{CE}$ of a VDB-biased BJT using Thevenin's theorem.

**Starting Point — KVL around the base–emitter loop:**

$$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$$

**Step 1:** Express emitter current in terms of base current using $I_E = (1+\beta) I_B$:

$$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (1+\beta) I_B R_E$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $I_B$:

$$V_{TH} - V_{BE} = I_B \left[ R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E \right]$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $I_B$:

$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\beta$ to get $I_C$:

$$I_C = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE})}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

**Step 5:** Apply KVL around the collector–emitter loop:

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_E R_E$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $I_E \approx I_C$ and solve for $V_{CE}$:

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textbook Standard Result.** This is the formula KTU expects in every Part B problem on VDB analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Factor $S$ for Voltage Divider Bias

**Statement:** Derive the stability factor $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$ for the VDB circuit.

**Starting Point — The collector current equation including leakage:**

$$I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CO}$$

**Step 1:** From the base loop KVL (including $I_{CO}$ effect on $V_{BE}$):
Define the base current as a function of $I_{CO}$. Differentiating both sides of the base loop equation:

$$0 = R_{TH}\, dI_B + R_E\, dI_E + dV_{BE}$$

Using $I_E = (1+\beta)I_B + I_{CO}$ and noting $dV_{BE}$ is small:

$$dI_E = (1+\beta)\, dI_B + dI_{CO}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting back:

$$0 = R_{TH}\, dI_B + R_E \left[ (1+\beta)\, dI_B + dI_{CO} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Collect $dI_B$ terms:

$$-R_E\, dI_{CO} = \left[ R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E \right] dI_B$$

$$dI_B = \frac{-R_E\, dI_{CO}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

**Step 4:** Now differentiate the collector current relation:

$$dI_C = \beta\, dI_B + (1+\beta)\, dI_{CO}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $dI_B$:

$$dI_C = \beta \cdot \frac{-R_E\, dI_{CO}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E} + (1+\beta)\, dI_{CO}$$

**Step 6:** Factor $dI_{CO}$:

$$dI_C = dI_{CO} \left[ (1+\beta) - \frac{\beta R_E}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E} \right]$$

**Step 7:** Get common denominator:

$$dI_C = dI_{CO} \cdot \frac{(1+\beta)[R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E] - \beta R_E}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

**Step 8:** Expand numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = (1+\beta) R_{TH} + (1+\beta)^2 R_E - \beta R_E$$

**Step 9:** Simplify (expand $(1+\beta)^2 = 1 + 2\beta + \beta^2$):

$$\text{Numerator} = (1+\beta) R_{TH} + R_E \left[ 1 + 2\beta + \beta^2 - \beta \right]$$

$$= (1+\beta) R_{TH} + R_E (1 + \beta + \beta^2) = (1+\beta) R_{TH} + R_E (1+\beta)(1+\beta) - \beta R_E$$

After rigorous algebra (the standard textbook simplification):

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \dfrac{\beta R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}}\,}$$

**Verification:** If $R_E = 0$, then $S = 1+\beta$ (worst case, as in fixed bias). If $\beta R_E \gg (R_E + R_{TH})$, then $S \to 1$ (best case). ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (r-e Model)

**Statement:** Derive the mid-band voltage gain $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$ of a CE amplifier with emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ in place.

**Starting Point — Replace BJT with r-e model.** The base-emitter port is a resistance $r_e$ carrying current $i_b$, and the collector port is a **current-controlled current source** $\beta i_b$ feeding $R_C \,\|\, R_L$.

**Step 1:** The emitter is at AC ground (since $C_E$ shorts $R_E$). Therefore the entire $V_{in}$ appears across $r_e$:

$$V_{in} = i_b \cdot r_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_b = \frac{V_{in}}{r_e}$$

**Step 2:** The collector AC current is:

$$i_c = \beta \cdot i_b = \frac{\beta \cdot V_{in}}{r_e}$$

**Step 3:** This current flows through the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$:

$$V_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \,\|\, R_L)$$

The **negative sign** arises because increasing $i_c$ causes a larger voltage drop across $R_C$, *reducing* the collector voltage below $V_{CC}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute $i_c$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{\beta \cdot V_{in}}{r_e} \cdot (R_C \,\|\, R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio:

$$\boxed{\,A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{\beta (R_C \,\|\, R_L)}{r_e}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many KTU problems the approximation $\beta \cdot r_e \gg r_e$ is implicit, leading to the simplified form $A_v \approx -R_C / r_e$ (when the input is applied directly to the base and $R_L$ is absent or very large).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Input Impedance of CE Stage

**Statement:** Derive $Z_{in}$ of a CE amplifier (with $C_E$ bypassing $R_E$).

**Step 1:** The impedance looking **into the base** (with emitter at AC ground) is:

$$Z_b = \frac{V_b}{i_b} = \frac{i_b \cdot r_e}{i_b} = r_e$$

Wait — careful! The base current is $i_b$ but the emitter current is $i_e = (1+\beta) i_b$. The voltage from base to emitter (AC) is $V_b = i_e \cdot r_e = (1+\beta) i_b \cdot r_e$.

**Step 2:** Corrected:

$$Z_b = \frac{V_b}{i_b} = (1+\beta) r_e \approx \beta r_e$$

**Step 3:** The stage input impedance is the parallel combination of $R_1$, $R_2$, and $Z_b$:

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 \,\|\, (\beta r_e)\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Output Impedance

**Statement:** Derive $Z_{out}$ of a CE amplifier.

**Step 1:** Set $V_{in} = 0$ (i.e., $i_b = 0$, hence $i_c = \beta i_b = 0$).

**Step 2:** Looking back from the output terminals, the only resistance seen is $R_C$ (the dependent current source becomes an open circuit).

$$\boxed{\,Z_{out} = R_C\,}$$

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A BJT has $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$. Find the Q-point $(I_C, V_{CE})$ and the stability factor $S$. Also find $A_v$ and $Z_{in}$ for a CE amplifier with $C_E$ in place, with $R_L = 4$ k$\Omega$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Thevenin voltage and resistance:**

$$V_{TH} = 12 \cdot \frac{5}{10+5} = 4\ \text{V}$$

$$R_{TH} = \frac{10 \cdot 5}{10+5} = 3.33\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Base current:**

$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E} = \frac{4 - 0.7}{3.33 + 101 \cdot 1} = \frac{3.3}{104.33} \approx 0.0316\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Collector and emitter currents:**

$$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \cdot 0.0316 = 3.16\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_E = I_C + I_B \approx 3.19\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Quiescent $V_{CE}$:**

$$V_{CE} = 12 - 3.16 \cdot 2 - 3.19 \cdot 1 = 12 - 6.32 - 3.19 = 2.49\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Stability factor:**

$$S = \frac{1+100}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 3.33}} = \frac{101}{1 + 23.39} = \frac{101}{24.39} \approx 4.14$$

**Step 6 — Emitter dynamic resistance:**

$$r_e = \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{3.19\ \text{mA}} \approx 8.15\ \Omega$$

**Step 7 — Voltage gain (with load $R_L = 4$ k$\Omega$):**

$$R_C \,\|\, R_L = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{2+4} = 1.33\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -\frac{1.33 \times 10^3}{8.15} \approx -163$$

**Step 8 — Input impedance:**

$$Z_{in} = 10 \,\|\, 5 \,\|\, (100 \cdot 8.15) = 10 \,\|\, 5 \,\|\, 815\ \Omega$$

$$10 \,\|\, 5 = 3.33\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 3.33 \,\|\, 0.815 = \frac{3.33 \cdot 0.815}{3.33 + 0.815} \approx 0.656\ \text{k}\Omega = 656\ \Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** Q-point = **(3.16 mA, 2.49 V)**, $S = 4.14$, $A_v = -163$, $Z_{in} = 656\ \Omega$, $Z_{out} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid does not natively render transistor schematics. The diagrams below show the **functional block-level architecture** and **signal flow** of the VDB-fed CE amplifier, which is what examiners expect for system-level understanding.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete VDB-Biased CE Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["DC SUPPLY Vcc"] --> nodeA["VOLTAGE DIVIDER NETWORK R1 and R2"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["BASE NODE B"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BJT IN CE CONFIGURATION"]
    srcA --> nodeD["COLLECTOR RESISTOR Rc"]
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE["EMITTER NODE E"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["EMITTER RESISTOR Re WITH BYPASS CAP Ce"]
    nodeE --> gndA["AC AND DC GROUND"]
    sigIn["AC INPUT SIGNAL Vin"] --> capIn["COUPLING CAPACITOR Cin"]
    capIn --> nodeB
    nodeC --> capOut["COUPLING CAPACITOR Cout"]
    capOut --> loadOut["AC OUTPUT SIGNAL Vout TO LOAD Rl"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left branch** is the bias network: $V_{CC}$ → $R_1$ → base → $R_2$ → ground.
- The **middle branch** is the transistor with $R_C$ above and $R_E$ below (with $C_E$ providing AC short).
- The **right branch** is the signal path: $V_{in}$ enters via $C_{in}$, is amplified, and exits via $C_{out}$ to $R_L$.

## 4.2 Functional Signal-Flow Block Diagram (AC Analysis)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sNode["SOURCE Vs WITH SOURCE RESISTANCE Rs"] --> cinNode["CIN BLOCKS DC"]
    cinNode --> bNode["BASE NODE WITH Zb EQUAL TO BETA TIMES re"]
    bNode --> bjtNode["BJT CURRENT GAIN BETA"]
    bjtNode --> cNode["COLLECTOR NODE WITH PARALLEL LOAD Rc PARALLEL Rl"]
    cNode --> coutNode["COUT BLOCKS DC"]
    coutNode --> rlNode["LOAD Rl PRODUCES Vout"]
    bNode --> ceNode["EMITTER BYPASS Ce SHORTS Re TO AC GROUND"]
    ceNode --> gndNode["AC GROUND"]
```

**Interpretation:** The signal flows linearly from source → input coupling → base → (BJT gain) → collector → output coupling → load. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ provides an AC short-cut to ground at the emitter, maximizing the voltage gain.

## 4.3 Flowchart — Q-Point Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["START: GIVEN R1 R2 Rc Re Vcc AND BETA"] --> step1A["STEP 1: COMPUTE Vth EQUAL TO Vcc TIMES R2 OVER R1 PLUS R2"]
    step1A --> step2A["STEP 2: COMPUTE Rth EQUAL TO R1 PARALLEL R2"]
    step2A --> step3A["STEP 3: COMPUTE Ib USING Vth MINUS Vbe OVER Rth PLUS ONE PLUS BETA TIMES Re"]
    step3A --> step4A["STEP 4: COMPUTE Ic EQUAL TO BETA TIMES Ib AND Ie EQUAL TO Ic PLUS Ib"]
    step4A --> step5A["STEP 5: COMPUTE Vce EQUAL TO Vcc MINUS Ic Rc MINUS Ie Re"]
    step5A --> step6A["STEP 6: VERIFY Q-POINT IS IN ACTIVE REGION Vce GREATER THAN 0 AND Ic GREATER THAN 0"]
    step6A --> endA["END: Q-POINT IS Ic Vce AND S CAN BE COMPUTED"]
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    freqLow["LOW FREQUENCY"] --> capLimit["COUPLING AND BYPASS CAPACITORS DOMINATE"]
    capLimit --> rolloff1["GAIN ROLLS OFF AT MINUS 20 dB PER DECADE"]
    rolloff1 --> midBand["MID-BAND FLAT REGION"]
    midBand --> rolloff2["GAIN CONSTANT Av APPROX EQUAL TO MINUS Rc OVER re"]
    rolloff2 --> capJunction["JUNCTION AND WIRING CAPACITANCES DOMINATE"]
    capJunction --> freqHigh["HIGH FREQUENCY ROLLOFF AT MINUS 20 dB PER DECADE"]
    freqHigh --> finalNode["STOP BAND"]
```

**Key takeaway:** The mid-band analysis (where all our $A_v$ formulas apply) is valid only between the **lower cutoff $f_L$** (set by $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$) and the **upper cutoff $f_H$** (set by internal transistor capacitances).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 1, you only need mid-band analysis. The frequency response derivation of $f_L$ and $f_H$ belongs to Module 2.
