# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are **direct, definition-based, or single-formula application** items. Each must be answered in **2–3 sentences** with a labeled diagram if applicable. Average time: **3 minutes**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Voltage Divider Bias? Why is it considered the most stable biasing method for a BJT?**

**Model Answer:**

Voltage Divider Bias is a transistor biasing scheme in which the base voltage is fixed by a resistive divider $R_1$–$R_2$ connected across $V_{CC}$, with the emitter resistor $R_E$ providing DC negative feedback. It is the most stable method because (i) the base voltage is set by the ratio $R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ which is independent of $\beta$, and (ii) $R_E$ provides strong negative feedback against thermal variations in $I_C$. The stability factor $S$ approaches **1** under proper design ($\beta R_E \gg R_E + R_{TH}$), making $I_C$ nearly independent of transistor parameters.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the stability factor $S$. Write its expression for a Voltage Divider biased transistor and explain the condition for ideal stability.**

**Model Answer:**

The stability factor $S$ is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$: $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$. For VDB:

$$S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta R_E / (R_E + R_{TH})}$$

**Condition for ideal stability:** $S \to 1$ when $\beta R_E \gg (R_E + R_{TH})$, which physically means the emitter resistor $R_E$ is large enough to dominate the feedback loop, making $I_C$ essentially independent of $\beta$ and $I_{CO}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (the student picks ONE of two alternatives). Each part typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Average time: **20–25 minutes per question**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** For a Silicon BJT ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V) with $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $R_1 = 12$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, calculate:
  (i) Thevenin equivalent ($V_{TH}$, $R_{TH}$) of the bias network.
  (ii) Quiescent base current $I_B$, collector current $I_C$, and $V_{CE}$.
  (iii) Stability factor $S$.

**(b)** With the Q-point from part (a), design a CE amplifier (assume $C_E$ perfectly bypasses $R_E$ and $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ are short at mid-band) and determine:
  (i) Voltage gain $A_v$ with and without a load $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$.
  (ii) Input impedance $Z_{in}$ and output impedance $Z_{out}$.
  (iii) Power gain $A_p$ (in dB) without load.

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) (i) — Thevenin Equivalent:**

$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 15 \cdot \frac{4.7}{12 + 4.7} = 15 \cdot \frac{4.7}{16.7} \approx 4.22\ \text{V}$$

$$R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{12 \cdot 4.7}{16.7} \approx 3.38\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (a) (ii) — Q-Point:**

$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E} = \frac{4.22 - 0.7}{3.38 + 101 \cdot 1} = \frac{3.52}{104.38} \approx 0.0337\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \cdot 0.0337 = 3.37\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_E \approx I_C + I_B = 3.40\ \text{mA}$$

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 15 - 3.37(2.2) - 3.40(1) = 15 - 7.41 - 3.40 = 4.19\ \text{V}$$

**Part (a) (iii) — Stability Factor:**

$$S = \frac{1 + 100}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 3.38}} = \frac{101}{1 + 22.88} = \frac{101}{23.88} \approx 4.23$$

**Part (b) (i) — Voltage Gain:**

$$r_e = \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E} = \frac{26}{3.40} \approx 7.65\ \Omega$$

$$A_v \text{ (no load)} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e} = -\frac{2200}{7.65} \approx -287.6$$

$$R_C \,\|\, R_L = \frac{2.2 \cdot 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} \approx 1.499\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v \text{ (with load)} = -\frac{1499}{7.65} \approx -195.9$$

**Part (b) (ii) — Input and Output Impedance:**

$$Z_b = \beta r_e = 100 \cdot 7.65 = 765\ \Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 \,\|\, Z_b = 12 \,\|\, 4.7 \,\|\, 0.765\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$12 \,\|\, 4.7 = \frac{12 \cdot 4.7}{16.7} = 3.38\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 3.38 \,\|\, 0.765 = \frac{3.38 \cdot 0.765}{3.38 + 0.765} = \frac{2.586}{4.145} \approx 0.624\ \text{k}\Omega = 624\ \Omega$$

$$Z_{out} = R_C = 2.2\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (b) (iii) — Power Gain (no load):**

$$A_p = \vert A_v \cdot A_i \vert = 287.6 \cdot 100 = 28{,}760$$

$$A_p \text{ (in dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(28{,}760) = 10 \cdot 4.459 \approx 44.6\ \text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **−2 marks** if student uses $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in $r_e$ without justification.
> - **−2 marks** if $V_{CE}$ is computed using $I_C$ alone (forgetting $I_E R_E$ drop).
> - **−1 mark** if the negative sign in $A_v$ is omitted (sign convention matters!).
> - **−1 mark** if units are inconsistent (e.g., mixing mA with A in $r_e$ calculation).
> - **−3 marks** if Thevenin step is skipped and approximate formula $V_B = V_{CC} R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ is used directly to get $I_C$ (this is a **3-mark penalty** for missing the exact derivation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** For a CE amplifier using Voltage Divider Bias with $V_{CC} = 20$ V, $R_1 = 22$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 2.2$ k$\Omega$, and a silicon transistor with $\beta = 150$:
  (i) Calculate the Q-point $(I_C, V_{CE})$ and the stability factor $S$.
  (ii) If $\beta$ changes to 200 due to device replacement, find the **percentage change in $I_C$** and comment on stability.

**(b)** For the Q-point obtained in part (a) with $\beta = 150$:
  (i) Calculate the small-signal voltage gain with and without bypass capacitor $C_E$ across $R_E$. Take $R_L = 5.6$ k$\Omega$.
  (ii) Determine $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ when $C_E$ is present.
  (iii) Sketch (describe) the mid-band frequency response indicating $A_v$ in dB and the cut-off frequencies $f_L$ and $f_H$.

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) (i) — Q-Point with $\beta = 150$:**

$$V_{TH} = 20 \cdot \frac{10}{32} = 6.25\ \text{V}$$

$$R_{TH} = \frac{22 \cdot 10}{32} = 6.875\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$I_B = \frac{6.25 - 0.7}{6.875 + 151 \cdot 2.2} = \frac{5.55}{6.875 + 332.2} = \frac{5.55}{339.075} \approx 0.01637\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_C = 150 \cdot 0.01637 = 2.456\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_E = I_C + I_B = 2.472\ \text{mA}$$

$$V_{CE} = 20 - 2.456 \cdot 4.7 - 2.472 \cdot 2.2 = 20 - 11.54 - 5.44 = 3.02\ \text{V}$$

$$S = \frac{151}{1 + 150 \cdot \dfrac{2.2}{2.2 + 6.875}} = \frac{151}{1 + 150 \cdot 0.2424} = \frac{151}{1 + 36.36} = \frac{151}{37.36} \approx 4.04$$

**Part (a) (ii) — Q-Point with $\beta = 200$:**

$$I_B' = \frac{5.55}{6.875 + 201 \cdot 2.2} = \frac{5.55}{6.875 + 442.2} = \frac{5.55}{449.075} \approx 0.01236\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_C' = 200 \cdot 0.01236 = 2.472\ \text{mA}$$

**Percentage change in $I_C$:**

$$\%\Delta I_C = \frac{2.472 - 2.456}{2.456} \cdot 100\% \approx 0.65\%$$

**Comment:** Despite a 33% increase in $\beta$ (from 150 to 200), the collector current changes by less than 1%. This confirms the **excellent stability** of VDB. The small change is consistent with $S \approx 4$ (very low compared to fixed bias where $S = 1 + \beta$).

**Part (b) (i) — Voltage Gain:**

$$r_e = \frac{26}{2.472} \approx 10.52\ \Omega$$

**With $C_E$ (bypassed):**

$$A_v = -\frac{R_C \,\|\, R_L}{r_e} = -\frac{4.7 \,\|\, 5.6}{0.01052} = -\frac{2.557}{0.01052} \approx -243$$

$$A_v \text{ (no load)} = -\frac{4.7 \cdot 10^3}{10.52} \approx -447$$

**Without $C_E$ (unbypassed):**

$$A_v = -\frac{R_C \,\|\, R_L}{r_e + R_E} = -\frac{2557}{10.52 + 2200} \approx -1.16$$

**Observation:** Removing $C_E$ reduces $|A_v|$ drastically (from 243 to 1.16) — this is why $C_E$ is always used in practical CE amplifiers.

**Part (b) (ii) — Input and Output Impedance (with $C_E$):**

$$Z_b = \beta r_e = 150 \cdot 10.52 = 1578\ \Omega = 1.578\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 22 \,\|\, 10 \,\|\, 1.578\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$22 \,\|\, 10 = \frac{220}{32} = 6.875\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 6.875 \,\|\, 1.578 = \frac{6.875 \cdot 1.578}{8.453} = \frac{10.85}{8.453} \approx 1.284\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{out} = R_C = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (b) (iii) — Mid-band Frequency Response:**

The mid-band gain in dB:

$$A_{v(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}(243) \approx 47.7\ \text{dB}$$

The frequency response has three regions:
- **Below $f_L$** (typically 20 Hz – 1 kHz): Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** due to $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$.
- **Mid-band** (between $f_L$ and $f_H$): Flat gain of **47.7 dB** (with load).
- **Above $f_H$** (typically 100 kHz – few MHz): Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** due to internal transistor capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **−2 marks** if student computes $r_e$ using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ (this gives $r_e = 10.58\ \Omega$ — close, but a **conceptual error**).
> - **−3 marks** if the unbypassed $A_v$ case is solved as $-\beta R_C / (r_e + R_E)$ instead of the correct $-R_C / (r_e + R_E)$ (the $\beta$ cancels out in unbypassed case — students often miss this).
> - **−2 marks** if $Z_{in}$ is reported as just $\beta r_e$ without the parallel combination with $R_1$, $R_2$.
> - **−2 marks** if the dB conversion of $A_v$ is wrong (use $20 \log_{10}$, not $10 \log_{10}$, for voltage gain).
> - **−1 mark** for missing the units (k$\Omega$ vs $\Omega$, mA vs A).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU Module 1 — Voltage Divider Bias and CE Amplifier**

### **Core Concepts**
- [x] VDB uses a resistive divider $R_1, R_2$ to set $V_B$ independent of $\beta$.
- [x] $R_E$ provides DC negative feedback for thermal stability.
- [x] Thevenin equivalent ($V_{TH}$, $R_{TH}$) is mandatory for **exact** base-current analysis.
- [x] CE amplifier gives voltage gain, current gain, and 180° phase inversion.
- [x] Bypass capacitor $C_E$ boosts AC gain by shorting $R_E$ at signal frequencies.

### **Critical Formulas to Memorize**
- [x] $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$
- [x] $R_{TH} = R_1 \,||\, R_2$
- [x] $I_B = (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / [R_{TH} + (1+\beta)R_E]$
- [x] $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$
- [x] $S = (1+\beta) / [1 + \beta R_E / (R_E + R_{TH})]$ — ideal: $S \to 1$
- [x] $r_e = 26\ \text{mV} / I_E\ (\text{mA})$
- [x] $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ (no load, $C_E$ present)
- [x] $A_v = -(R_C \,||\, R_L) / r_e$ (with load, $C_E$ present)
- [x] $A_v = -R_C / (r_e + R_E)$ (without $C_E$)
- [x] $Z_{in} = R_1 \,||\, R_2 \,||\, (\beta r_e)$
- [x] $Z_{out} = R_C$

### **Standard Assumptions in KTU Papers**
- [x] Silicon BJT: $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (NOT 0.2 V — that's germanium).
- [x] Thermal voltage $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K.
- [x] $\alpha \approx 1$, so $I_C \approx I_E$.
- [x] Mid-band analysis only (coupling caps = short, junction caps = open).
- [x] Use $I_E$ (not $I_C$) in $r_e$ formula.

### **Common Mistakes to Avoid**
- [x] ❌ Using $V_B = V_{TH}$ directly to compute $I_C$ via $I_C = (V_B - V_{BE})/R_E$ — this is the **approximate** method, valid only when $\beta R_E \gg R_{TH}$.
- [x] ❌ Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$ — sign indicates phase inversion.
- [x] ❌ Using $r_e = V_T/I_C$ instead of $V_T/I_E$.
- [x] ❌ Confusing **current gain** $A_i = \beta$ with the **power gain** $A_p = A_v \cdot A_i$.
- [x] ❌ Mixing up $R_C \,||\, R_L$ with $R_C + R_L$ in loaded gain.

### **Rapid-Fire Q&A for Last-Minute Revision**
- **Q:** Why is VDB preferred over fixed bias? **A:** Stability factor approaches 1 vs $(1+\beta)$ in fixed bias.
- **Q:** What is the role of $C_E$? **A:** Increases AC gain by shorting $R_E$ for AC signals while keeping DC stability.
- **Q:** Why 180° phase shift in CE? **A:** Increasing $V_{BE}$ increases $I_C$, which drops more voltage across $R_C$, lowering $V_C$.
- **Q:** What happens if $C_E$ is removed? **A:** Voltage gain drops from $-R_C/r_e$ to $-R_C/(r_e + R_E)$ — a huge loss.
- **Q:** What is the typical mid-band $Z_{out}$ of a CE amplifier? **A:** $R_C$ (a few hundred ohms to a few k$\Omega$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Always* use $V_{TH}, R_{TH}$ for the bias calculation, *always* use $I_E$ (not $I_C$) in $r_e$, and *always* include the negative sign in $A_v$. These three habits alone will fetch you **at least 4–6 extra marks** in the KTU examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AC Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Alternating Current (AC)

An **Alternating Current (AC)** is a periodic electrical current whose magnitude and polarity (direction) reverse cyclically with respect to time, in contrast to Direct Current (DC) which flows in only one direction. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, AC is mathematically represented as a time-varying sinusoid:

$$
v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)
$$

where:
- $V_m$ is the **peak (maximum) amplitude** in Volts
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** in rad/s
- $\phi$ is the **phase angle** in radians
- $f$ is the **frequency** in Hertz (**Hz**)
- $T = 1/f$ is the **time period** in seconds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental AC parameters a student *must* master are: Instantaneous value, Peak value, Peak-to-Peak value, RMS value, Average value, Time period, Frequency, Angular frequency, and Phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a swing** in a playground. You push forward, the swing goes up, comes back, then you push backward as it returns. The motion of the swing is a perfect mechanical analogy for AC:
- The swing's position oscillates back and forth around a central point (zero), just like voltage oscillates around **0 V**.
- The maximum displacement of the swing from its resting position corresponds to the **peak amplitude** $V_m$.
- The number of complete back-and-forth cycles per second corresponds to the **frequency** $f$ (**Hz**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AC for power transmission?** The Indian national grid operates at **50 Hz** (US uses 60 Hz). AC won the "War of Currents" because transformers can efficiently step AC voltages up/down for long-distance transmission with minimal loss — a feat impossible with DC until modern electronics.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Mains frequency (India) | **50 Hz** |
| Mains frequency (US) | **60 Hz** |
| Standard domestic supply (India) | **230 V RMS, 50 Hz** |
| Audio frequency range | **20 Hz to 20 kHz** |
| Radio frequency range | **30 kHz to 300 GHz** |
| Form factor for sine wave | **1.11** ($V_{rms}/V_{avg}$) |
| Crest factor for sine wave | **1.414** ($\sqrt{2}$, $V_m/V_{rms}$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind AC Concepts

An AC waveform is characterized by three orthogonal attributes — **Amplitude, Frequency, and Phase**. Mastery of these three unlocks every AC analysis:

1. **Amplitude Quantification** — A sinusoid is not a single value; it is *time-varying*. Engineers needed a single number to express its "effective heating/working capacity" comparable to DC. This birthed the **RMS value** concept.
2. **Frequency Response** — Capacitors and Inductors are *frequency-dependent* elements. Their opposition to AC ($X_C$ and $X_L$) changes with $\omega$. This is the foundation of every filter, oscillator, and tuned amplifier in Analog Electronics.
3. **Phase Shifting** — Inductors *lag* voltage by current, capacitors *lead*. Phase determines **power factor**, which dictates how efficiently electrical energy is converted to useful work.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous voltage | $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | V | Function of time |
| 2 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s | $\omega$ in rad/s, $f$ in Hz |
| 3 | Peak-to-Peak value | $V_{pp} = 2 V_m$ | V | Distance between max and min |
| 4 | RMS value (sine) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_m$ | V | Heating equivalent of DC |
| 5 | Average value (half cycle) | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637\,V_m$ | V | Rectified DC equivalent |
| 6 | Form Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = 1.11$ | — | Sine wave constant |
| 7 | Crest / Peak Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ | — | Sine wave constant |
| 8 | Inductive Reactance | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ | $\Omega$ | Frequency-dependent |
| 9 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | $\Omega$ | Inversely freq-dependent |
| 10 | Impedance (Series RLC) | $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$, $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Complex quantity |
| 11 | Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$ | rad or ° | +ve for inductive, -ve for capacitive |
| 12 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$ | — | 0 to 1 (pure R = 1) |
| 13 | Real Power | $P = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}\cos\phi$ | W | Average useful power |
| 14 | Reactive Power | $Q = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}\sin\phi$ | VAR | Stored/returned power |
| 15 | Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}$ | VA | Vector sum magnitude |
| 16 | Power Triangle | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | — | Pythagorean relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $V_{rms}$ concept is used universally on **digital multimeters** (DMMs). When you measure "230 V" on a wall socket, the meter is internally computing $\sqrt{\text{mean}(v^2(t))}$. The same RMS principle heats your **electric iron** and lights your **incandescent bulb**.

### 2.3 AC Behavior of Pure Passive Elements

- **Pure Resistor (R):** Voltage and current are **in-phase** ($\phi = 0^\circ$). $V_R = I R$. No phase shift.
- **Pure Inductor (L):** Current **lags** voltage by $90^\circ$. $V_L = I X_L$. Stores energy in magnetic field.
- **Pure Capacitor (C):** Current **leads** voltage by $90^\circ$. $V_C = I X_C$. Stores energy in electric field.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RMS Value of a Sinusoidal AC Waveform

**Goal:** Find the DC equivalent voltage that delivers the same average power to a resistor as the AC waveform.

**Step 1 — Define RMS (Root Mean Square).** RMS is obtained by (i) Squaring the instantaneous value, (ii) taking the Mean (average) over one full period, (iii) taking the Root.

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the sinusoidal expression.** Let $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity.** Use $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral into two terms.**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2T}\left[\int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt - \int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt\right]}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each integral.** Over one full period $T$, $\int_0^T \cos(2\omega t)\, dt = \dfrac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\Big|_0^T = \dfrac{\sin(4\pi)}{2\omega} - 0 = 0$.

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2T}\bigl[T - 0\bigr]} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final RMS expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, V_m\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Value of a Half Sinusoidal Cycle

**Step 1 — Definition.** The average value of an AC waveform is taken over a **half cycle** (since the integral over a full cycle equals zero due to symmetry).

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T/2}\int_{0}^{T/2} v(t)\, dt = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the sinusoid.**

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{T}\left[-\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega}\right]_{0}^{T/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the limits.** At $t = T/2$, $\omega t = \pi$, so $\cos(\pi) = -1$. At $t = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{T\omega}\bigl[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)\bigr] = \frac{2 V_m}{T\omega}\bigl[1 + 1\bigr] = \frac{4 V_m}{T\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi/T$ and simplify.**

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{4 V_m}{T \cdot (2\pi/T)} = \frac{4 V_m}{2\pi} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637\, V_m\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Average Power in a Single-Phase AC Circuit

**Step 1 — Write instantaneous voltage and current** with phase difference $\phi$.

$$
v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t), \qquad i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous power** $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$.

$$
p(t) = V_m I_m \sin(\omega t)\,\sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product-to-sum identity** $\sin A \sin B = \dfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)\bigr]$.

$$
p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\bigl[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Average over one full cycle.** The cosine term $\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ averages to zero.

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} p(t)\, dt = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\phi) - 0
$$

**Step 5 — Express in RMS form** using $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$ and $I_m = \sqrt{2}\,I_{rms}$.

$$
\boxed{\,P = V_{rms}\, I_{rms}\, \cos\phi\,} \quad \text{(Real Power in Watts)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AC Behavior of R, L, C

| Element | Opposition | Phase of I w.r.t V | Energy Behavior | Frequency Dependency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor $R$ | $R$ (constant) | In-phase ($\phi = 0$) | Dissipates as heat | Independent of $f$ |
| Inductor $L$ | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Lags by $90^\circ$ | Stores in magnetic field | $\uparrow$ with $f$ |
| Capacitor $C$ | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Leads by $90^\circ$ | Stores in electric field | $\downarrow$ with $f$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AC Analysis Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AC WAVEFORM INPUT] --> B[PARAMETER EXTRACTION]
    B --> B1[Peak Value Vm]
    B --> B2[Frequency f and Omega]
    B --> B3[Phase Angle phi]
    A --> C[VALUE QUANTIFICATION]
    C --> C1[RMS VALUE Vm over sqrt2]
    C --> C2[AVERAGE VALUE 2Vm over pi]
    C --> C3[FORM FACTOR 1.11]
    C --> C4[CREST FACTOR 1.414]
    A --> D[CIRCUIT RESPONSE]
    D --> D1[Resistor R - In Phase]
    D --> D2[Inductor L - Current Lags]
    D --> D3[Capacitor C - Current Leads]
    D1 --> E[IMPEDANCE Z]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Series RLC Magnitude]
    E --> E2[Phase Angle phi]
    E2 --> F[POWER ANALYSIS]
    F --> F1[Real Power P]
    F --> F2[Reactive Power Q]
    F --> F3[Apparent Power S]
    F1 --> G[POWER FACTOR cos phi]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: RMS Value Computation Algorithm

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Instantaneous Voltage v of t] --> B[SQUARE the signal]
    B --> C[MEAN average over T]
    C --> D[SQUARE ROOT]
    D --> E[RMS Value Vrms]
    E --> F[Compare with Vm over sqrt2]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Phasor Diagram (Series RLC Circuit)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PhasorReference[Phasor Reference Frame]
        Vdir[Voltage Phasor V along x axis]
        Idir[Current Phasor I along x axis]
        VL[VL leads I by 90 degrees along y axis]
        VC[VC lags I by 90 degrees along negative y axis]
        VR[VR along same axis as I]
    end
    Vdir --> NetV[Net Voltage V equals VR plus VL minus VC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Phasor:** The reference axis is conventionally taken as the current $I$ (since the same current flows through series R, L, C). $V_R$ is collinear with $I$. $V_L$ points "up" ($+90^\circ$) and $V_C$ points "down" ($-90^\circ$). The resultant $V$ tilts up/down depending on whether the circuit is net inductive ($X_L > X_C$) or capacitive ($X_C > X_L$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define RMS value and Average value of an AC waveform. Derive the relationship between peak and RMS values for a pure sinusoidal signal.**

**Model Answer:**
The **RMS (Root Mean Square) value** of an AC waveform is the DC equivalent value that produces the same heating effect in a resistor. It is computed by squaring the instantaneous value, taking the time-average over one full period, and then taking the square root.

The **Average value** is the mean of the instantaneous values taken over a **half cycle** (since the full-cycle mean of a symmetric sine is zero).

For $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}
$$

Relationship: $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms} \approx 1.414\,V_{rms}$.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between RMS value, Average value, and Peak value. What is Form Factor and Crest Factor for a pure sine wave?**

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | Definition | Sine-wave Value |
|---|---|---|
| Peak value $V_m$ | Maximum instantaneous value | $V_m$ |
| RMS value $V_{rms}$ | DC equivalent heating value | $0.707\,V_m$ |
| Average value $V_{avg}$ | Arithmetic mean over half cycle | $0.637\,V_m$ |

$$
\text{Form Factor} = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{0.707}{0.637} = 1.11
$$

$$
\text{Crest Factor} = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{1}{0.707} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`(a)` Derive the expression for RMS value and Average value of a sinusoidal AC waveform. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Define RMS:**
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Use identity** $\sin^2\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$:
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2T}\int_0^T \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr] dt = \frac{V_m^2}{2T}\bigl[T - 0\bigr] = \frac{V_m^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Take square root:**
$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Average value over half cycle:**
$$
V_{avg} = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, dt = \frac{2V_m}{T\omega}\bigl[1+1\bigr] = \frac{4V_m}{2\pi}
$$

**Step 5 — Final:**
$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}}
$$

---

**`(b)` Explain phasor representation of sinusoidal signals. Draw the phasor diagram of a series R-L circuit and derive its impedance and phase angle. (7 Marks)**

**Phasor Definition:** A phasor is a rotating vector of length equal to the peak amplitude rotating counter-clockwise with angular velocity $\omega$. For $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the phasor is $\vec{V} = V_m \angle \phi$.

**Series R-L Circuit Analysis:**
- Voltage across resistor: $\vec{V_R} = \vec{I} \cdot R$ (in-phase with current)
- Voltage across inductor: $\vec{V_L} = \vec{I} \cdot jX_L$ (leads current by $90^\circ$)

Total voltage phasor: $\vec{V} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_L}$.

Magnitude:
$$
\vert V \vert = \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_L^2} = I\sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}
$$

Impedance:
$$
Z = R + jX_L, \qquad \vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}
$$

Phase angle (voltage leads current):
$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right)
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`(a)` Derive the expression for instantaneous power, real power, reactive power, and apparent power in a single-phase AC circuit. (7 Marks)**

Let $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

**Instantaneous power:**
$$
p(t) = v(t)\,i(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\bigl[\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\bigr]
$$

**Average (Real) Power** — the second term averages to zero:
$$
\boxed{P = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}\cos\phi} \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

**Apparent Power** — magnitude of $V \cdot I$:
$$
\boxed{S = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}} \quad \text{[VA]}
$$

**Reactive Power** — power oscillating between source and reactive elements:
$$
\boxed{Q = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}\sin\phi} \quad \text{[VAR]}
$$

**Power Triangle:**
$$
S^2 = P^2 + Q^2, \qquad \text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}
$$

---

**`(b)` A series RLC circuit has $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 50\,\text{mH}$, $C = 100\,\mu\text{F}$, connected to $230\,\text{V}$, $50\,\text{Hz}$ supply. Calculate the impedance, current drawn, and real power consumed. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:**
$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Reactances:**
$$
X_L = \omega L = 314.16 \times 0.050 = 15.71\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{314.16 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 31.83\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Net reactance and impedance:**
$$
X = X_L - X_C = 15.71 - 31.83 = -16.12\,\Omega \quad (\text{capacitive})
$$

$$
\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 16.12^2} = \sqrt{100 + 259.85} = \sqrt{359.85} \approx 18.97\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Current drawn:**
$$
I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230}{18.97} \approx 12.12\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Power factor and real power:**
$$
\cos\phi = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{10}{18.97} \approx 0.527 \quad (\text{leading})
$$

$$
P = V_{rms}\, I_{rms}\, \cos\phi = 230 \times 12.12 \times 0.527 \approx 1469\,\text{W} \approx 1.47\,\text{kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing Peak and RMS:** A very common error is using $V_m = 230\,\text{V}$ instead of $V_{rms} = 230\,\text{V}$ when plugging into power equations. Always check whether the given voltage is **peak** or **RMS** — domestic supplies are quoted in **RMS**.
> 2. **Half-cycle vs Full-cycle Average:** Average value of a sine wave over a **full cycle is ZERO**. Students who write $V_{avg} = 0$ without clarifying "over half cycle" lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign of $\phi$:** Forgetting to indicate whether phase angle is **leading** (capacitive) or **lagging** (inductive) costs a mark. Always state it explicitly.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $X_L = 15.71$ without the $\Omega$ symbol, or computing power without [W] annotation, is penalized.
> 5. **Trigonometric Identity Errors:** In RMS derivation, forgetting the identity $\sin^2\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$ is a frequent stumbling block. Memorize it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **AC** = Alternating Current; mathematically $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$.
- [ ] Three pillars of AC: **Amplitude ($V_m$), Frequency ($f$), Phase ($\phi$)**.
- [ ] **Peak value** $V_m$ is the maximum instantaneous value; **Peak-to-Peak** $V_{pp} = 2V_m$.
- [ ] **RMS value** $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707\,V_m$ — represents the **DC equivalent heating** value.
- [ ] **Average value** $V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi \approx 0.637\,V_m$ — taken over a **half cycle**.
- [ ] **Form Factor** for sine = $\mathbf{1.11}$; **Crest Factor** for sine = $\mathbf{\sqrt{2}}$.
- [ ] **Inductive Reactance** $X_L = \omega L$ **increases** with frequency (open at high $f$).
- [ ] **Capacitive Reactance** $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ **decreases** with frequency (short at high $f$).
- [ ] **Impedance** $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$; magnitude $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$.
- [ ] **Resistor:** $V$ and $I$ in phase; **Inductor:** $I$ lags $V$ by $90^\circ$; **Capacitor:** $I$ leads $V$ by $90^\circ$.
- [ ] **Real Power** $P = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}\cos\phi$ in **Watts**; **Apparent Power** $S = V_{rms}\,I_{rms}$ in **VA**.
- [ ] **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$; **Power Factor** $\cos\phi = R/\vert Z \vert$.
- [ ] Domestic mains in India: **230 V RMS, 50 Hz** — *always RMS, never peak*.
- [ ] In **phasor diagrams**, current $I$ is the reference in series circuits; voltage phasors are drawn relative to $I$.
- [ ] **High-Yield Trick:** If only $V_m$ is given for a "230 V" problem, convert: $V_{rms} = 230/\sqrt{2} \approx 162.6\,V$ (but normally 230 V *is* the RMS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Coupling Capacitors and Emitter Bypass Capacitor, Common Emitter AC Equivalent Circuit, Amplifier Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Role of Coupling Capacitors and Emitter Bypass Capacitor, Common Emitter AC Equivalent Circuit, Amplifier Gain ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier – A Quick Recap The **Common Emitter (CE) configuration** is the most widely used BJT amplifier topology in analog electronics because it provides **simultaneous voltage and current amplification** (i.e., good power gain) with a **180° phase inversion** between input and output. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the CE stage as the *workhorse* amplifier, and every other BJT configuration (CB, CC) is benchmarked against it. A complete CE stage typically consists of: 1. **DC Biasing Network** – Voltage Divider Bias using $R_1$ and $R_2$ (sets the Q-point in the active region). 2. **Coupling Capacitors** – $C_{in}$ at the input and $C_{out}$ at the output. 3. **Emitter Resistor $R_E$** – for thermal stability of the Q-point. 4. **Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$** – in parallel with $R_E$. 5. **Collector Resistor $R_C$** – converts collector current variations into output voltage. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Definition:** *Coupling Capacitor* – A capacitor used to connect two amplifier stages (or a source to a stage) such that it **blocks DC** but **passes AC** signals, thereby preventing the DC operating point of one stage from disturbing another. --- ### 1.2 Role of Coupling Capacitors ($C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$) **Intuitive Analogy — "The One-Way Valve for Water Pressure"** Imagine two water tanks connected by a thin rubber diaphragm. The diaphragm allows the *oscillating water waves* (AC ripple) to pass from one tank to the other, but it **stops the steady, constant water level** (DC bias) of one tank from spilling into the other. A coupling capacitor behaves exactly like this diaphragm in an electronic circuit. The two main purposes are: - **DC Isolation:** The input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ prevents the DC bias voltage of the source (or the previous stage) from altering the carefully designed Q-point voltage $V_{BE}$ of the transistor's base. Similarly, $C_{out}$ prevents the DC collector voltage $V_C$ from reaching the load. - **AC Signal Transfer:** For AC signals, the capacitor's impedance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ is designed to be very small (ideally a short circuit) at the **lowest signal frequency of interest** (often **20 Hz** for audio amplifiers). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the AC equivalent circuit, **all coupling and bypass capacitors are replaced by a short circuit (a plain wire)**, while the DC supply $V_{CC}$ is replaced by a **ground (0 V)**. This single rule is the most repeated step in KTU derivations. --- ### 1.3 Role of the Emitter Bypass Capacitor ($C_E$) The emitter resistor $R_E$ is essential for DC stability (it provides *negative feedback* for temperature variations). However, the same $R_E$ also causes severe AC signal loss because the AC emitter current $i_e$ flowing through it creates an AC voltage drop that **opposes the input signal** — drastically reducing the voltage gain. **Intuitive Analogy — "Bypassing the Speed Bump"** Think of $R_E$ as a *speed bump* on a road. Cars (AC signals) slow down when they go over it. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is like a **parallel tunnel built right next to the speed bump** — at high frequency, cars choose the tunnel, completely bypassing the bump. DC, however, is a parked car — it cannot enter the tunnel (capacitor blocks DC) and stays on the speed bump, where its stabilizing effect is still felt. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Without $C_E$:** Voltage gain falls to $A_v \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{R_E}$ (very small, since $R_E$ is usually much larger than the internal emitter resistance). > **With $C_E$:** Voltage gain rises dramatically to $A_v \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{r'_e}$ (large, since $r'_e$ is typically **25 Ω – 50 Ω**). --- ### 1.4 The AC Equivalent Circuit The **AC equivalent circuit** of a CE amplifier is a simplified version of the original circuit in which: | Original Element | AC Equivalent | Reason | |---|---|---| | $V_{CC}$ Battery | **Ground (0 V)** | Ideal DC source has zero internal AC impedance. | | Coupling Capacitors ($C_{in}, C_{out}$) | **Short Circuit** | $X_C \approx 0$ at signal frequency. | | Bypass Capacitor ($C_E$) | **Short Circuit** | $X_{C_E} \approx 0$ at signal frequency. | | BJT Transistor | **Small-signal model** (hybrid-π or T-model or h-parameter) | Replaces nonlinear device with linear equivalent. | | $R_E$ (with $C_E$ shorted) | **Shunted (bypassed)** | Appears as a short across $R_E$ for AC. | | $R_E$ (without $C_E$) | **Remains in circuit** | $(1+\beta)R_E$ term appears in the gain formula. | > [!NOTE] > **Concept of Small-Signal Analysis:** The AC equivalent circuit assumes the input signal $v_i$ is *small* (typically $v_i < 10$ mV) so that the BJT's nonlinear $I$–$V$ characteristics can be **linearized** around the Q-point using the Taylor series. This is the foundation of the **h-parameter model** and the **hybrid-π model**. --- ### 1.5 Amplifier Gain – The Three Faces of "Gain" An amplifier can quantify its performance through three different gain definitions: - **Voltage Gain ($A_v$):** Ratio of output AC voltage to input AC voltage. $$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}$$ - **Current Gain ($A_i$):** Ratio of output AC current to input AC current. $$A_i = \dfrac{i_{out}}{i_{in}}$$ - **Power Gain ($A_p$):** Ratio of output AC power to input AC power. $$A_p = A_v \times A_i = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$ For the CE configuration, $A_v$ is **high** (50–500), $A_i$ is **high** (~β, i.e., 50–300), and therefore $A_p$ is **very high** (typically 40–50 dB). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Coupling Capacitors Are Chosen with a Specific Capacitance The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the **design choice** of $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$. The value is selected such that the reactance $X_C$ at the **lowest operating frequency $f_L$** is small — typically less than **one-tenth** of the associated resistance. The general design rule is: $$C \ge \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_L X_C}$$ For $C_{in}$: $X_{C_{in}} \le 0.1 \times R_{in}$ For $C_{out}$: $X_{C_{out}} \le 0.1 \times R_L$ (where $R_L$ is the load resistance) For $C_E$: $X_{C_E} \le 0.1 \times R_E$ (this is the *most stringent* condition because $R_E$ can be large) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why one-tenth?** Choosing $X_C = R/10$ ensures the **maximum signal loss across the capacitor is only ~5%** (since it forms a voltage divider with the resistance). This is a standard engineering thumb-rule taught across all KTU analog electronics modules. --- ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Reduction to the AC Equivalent Circuit To obtain the small-signal equivalent circuit, KTU students must follow a strict 5-step reduction: 1. **Mark the Q-point** ($I_C, V_{CE}$) and the small-signal parameters ($r'_e, \beta, h_{ie}$). 2. **Short all capacitors** ($C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$) — replace with a plain wire. 3. **Ground the $V_{CC}$ supply** — since AC signals cannot develop across an ideal DC source. 4. **Replace the BJT** with its small-signal model. The most common choice in KTU is the **simplified hybrid model**, where: - Input side: $r_\pi$ (= $h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$) between base and emitter. - Output side: A **current source $\beta i_b$** from collector to emitter. - The transistor output resistance $r_o$ is **neglected** in basic analysis. 5. **Identify input port and output port** — base node is the input, collector node is the output, emitter is the **common** terminal. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table** — it covers approximately **70% of all numerical questions** asked on this topic in KTU ESE. | # | Parameter | Formula (with Bypass Capacitor $C_E$) | Formula (without $C_E$) | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Emitter dynamic resistance | $r'_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$ | Same | Ω | | 2 | Base-emitter input resistance | $r_\pi = \beta r'_e = h_{ie}$ | Same | Ω | | 3 | **Voltage Gain** $A_v$ | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \,\Vert\, R_L}{r'_e}$ | $A_v \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{r'_e + R_E}$ (exact: $-\dfrac{\beta R_C}{h_{ie} + (1+\beta)R_E}$) | dimensionless | | 4 | Voltage Gain in dB | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$ | Same | dB | | 5 | **Current Gain** $A_i$ | $A_i = \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_B \,\Vert\, h_{ie}}{R_B \,\Vert\, h_{ie}} = \beta$ (approx.) | $\beta$ | dimensionless | | 6 | **Input Resistance** $R_{in}$ | $R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, h_{ie}$ | $R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, [h_{ie} + (1+\beta)R_E]$ | Ω | | 7 | **Output Resistance** $R_{out}$ | $R_{out} = R_C$ | $R_C$ | Ω | | 8 | **Power Gain** $A_p$ | $A_p = A_v \cdot A_i$ | Same | dimensionless | | 9 | Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$ (for $C_{in}$ dominated) | Same | Hz | | 10 | Load Power | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{2 R_L}$ (RMS) | Same | W | > **Note on Notation:** $R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2$ denotes the **parallel combination** $R_1 \Vert R_2 = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Preamplifiers:** The CE stage with a properly designed coupling capacitor sets the **lower cutoff frequency** (e.g., 20 Hz for Hi-Fi audio) and blocks the DC offset between cascaded stages (e.g., microphone pre-amp → tone-control stage → power amp). - **Communication Receivers:** The CE stage provides the *gain block* in IF (Intermediate Frequency) amplifiers at **455 kHz** and **10.7 MHz**, where the coupling and bypass capacitors can be much smaller (in nF range). - **Instrumentation:** The high input impedance of a CE stage (when preceded by an emitter follower) ensures that weak sensor signals (e.g., from a thermocouple) are not loaded down. --- ### 2.5 Effect of Load Resistance $R_L$ When a load resistor $R_L$ is connected through $C_{out}$, the effective AC collector resistance becomes the **parallel combination** of $R_C$ and $R_L$: $$R_{ac} = R_C \,\Vert\, R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$ The voltage gain must then be re-computed using $R_{ac}$ instead of $R_C$. This is a common KTU follow-up question. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$ for a CE Amplifier WITH Bypass Capacitor We will use the **simplified hybrid-π model** (also called the *approximate CE small-signal model*). **Given circuit (after AC reduction):** - Input $v_i$ is applied to the base. - $R_B = R_1 \Vert R_2$ (Thevenin bias resistance at the base). - $C_E$ has been shorted, so the emitter is at AC ground. - $R_C$ is at the collector; $R_L$ is the load. **Step 1: Express the input loop equation** The input AC voltage $v_i$ appears entirely across $r_\pi$ (the base-emitter junction resistance) because the emitter is grounded by $C_E$. $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi$$ > This is the **key step** — students often forget that the bypass capacitor eliminates the $(1+\beta)R_E$ term that would otherwise appear. **Step 2: Express the output current** The collector AC current is controlled by the base current: $$i_c = \beta \cdot i_b$$ **Step 3: Express the output voltage** The output voltage is developed across the effective AC collector resistance $R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L$, with a **180° phase inversion** (indicated by the negative sign): $$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_{ac} = -\beta \, i_b \cdot (R_C \Vert R_L)$$ **Step 4: Form the ratio $A_v = v_{out}/v_i$** $$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta \, i_b \, (R_C \Vert R_L)}{i_b \, r_\pi}$$ The $i_b$ terms cancel: $$A_v = -\dfrac{\beta \, (R_C \Vert R_L)}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 5: Use the identity $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r'_e$** Substituting $r_\pi = \beta r'_e$: $$A_v = -\dfrac{\beta \, (R_C \Vert R_L)}{\beta \, r'_e}$$ The $\beta$ cancels, giving the **canonical KTU result**: $$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e}}$$ > The negative sign indicates the **180° phase reversal** between input and output — a hallmark feature of the CE amplifier. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of $A_v$ for a CE Amplifier WITHOUT Bypass Capacitor If $C_E$ is removed, the AC emitter current $i_e = (1+\beta)i_b$ must also flow through $R_E$, producing a voltage drop. **Step 1: KVL around the base-emitter loop** $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi + i_e \cdot R_E$$ Substitute $i_e = (1+\beta) i_b$: $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi + (1+\beta) \, i_b \cdot R_E$$ $$v_i = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E \right]$$ **Step 2: Substitute the identity $r_\pi = h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$** $$v_i = i_b \left[ \beta r'_e + (1+\beta) R_E \right]$$ For $\beta \gg 1$, $(1+\beta) \approx \beta$, so: $$v_i \approx i_b \cdot \beta \left[ r'_e + R_E \right]$$ **Step 3: Output voltage remains the same** $$v_{out} = -\beta \, i_b \cdot (R_C \Vert R_L)$$ **Step 4: Form the ratio** $$A_v = \dfrac{-\beta \, i_b \, (R_C \Vert R_L)}{i_b \cdot \beta (r'_e + R_E)}$$ $$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e + R_E}}$$ > This explains why $R_E$ *kills the gain* — the denominator jumps from $r'_e \approx 25$ Ω to $r'_e + R_E \approx 25 + 1000 = 1025$ Ω, dropping $A_v$ by a factor of **~40**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_{in}$ The input resistance looking into the base of the BJT is $R_{ib} = h_{ie} = r_\pi$ (when $C_E$ is present). The total input resistance of the amplifier is the parallel combination of the bias network and this base resistance: $$\boxed{R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert h_{ie}}$$ When $C_E$ is absent: $$\boxed{R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert \left[ h_{ie} + (1+\beta) R_E \right]}$$ This *boost* in $R_{in}$ when $C_E$ is removed is a useful side effect in some designs (e.g., high-impedance probe amplifiers). --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Current Gain $A_i$ The output current $i_L = i_c \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$ flows through the load. The input current is the source current $i_s$ that splits between the bias network and the base. $$A_i = \dfrac{i_L}{i_{in}} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_1 \Vert R_2}{R_1 \Vert R_2 + h_{ie}} \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$ In the ideal case (where the source impedance is zero, bias resistances are large, and $R_L \to \infty$): $$\boxed{A_i \approx \beta}$$ --- ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A CE amplifier uses $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_L = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$. The transistor is silicon ($V_{BE} = 0.7$ V). The emitter is fully bypassed by $C_E$. Calculate $A_v$, $A_v$ in dB, and $R_{in}$. **Step 1: Calculate the Thevenin voltage at the base** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{5}{10 + 5} = 4 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Thevenin resistance** $$R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2 = \dfrac{10 \cdot 5}{10 + 5} = 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3: Calculate the emitter current $I_E$** Using the KVL equation around the base-emitter loop: $$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$ Substitute $I_B = I_E / (1+\beta) = I_E / 101$: $$4 - \dfrac{I_E}{101}(3.33\text{k}) - 0.7 - I_E (1\text{k}) = 0$$ $$3.3 = I_E \left( \dfrac{3330}{101} + 1000 \right)$$ $$3.3 = I_E (32.97 + 1000) = I_E (1032.97)$$ $$I_E = \dfrac{3.3}{1032.97} = 3.19 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the dynamic emitter resistance** $$r'_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})} = \dfrac{26}{3.19} = 8.15 \text{ }\Omega$$ **Step 5: Calculate $h_{ie}$** $$h_{ie} = \beta r'_e = 100 \times 8.15 = 815 \text{ }\Omega = 0.815 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 6: Calculate the effective AC collector resistance** $$R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L = \dfrac{2 \text{k} \times 4 \text{k}}{2 \text{k} + 4 \text{k}} = 1.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 7: Calculate the voltage gain** $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_{ac}}{r'_e} = -\dfrac{1330}{8.15} = -163.2$$ $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v\vert = 20 \log_{10}(163.2) = 44.25 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 8: Calculate the input resistance** $$R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert h_{ie} = 10\text{k} \Vert 5\text{k} \Vert 0.815\text{k}$$ $$R_{in} = 3.33 \text{ k} \Vert 0.815 \text{ k} = \dfrac{3.33 \times 0.815}{3.33 + 0.815} = 0.656 \text{ k}\Omega = 656 \text{ }\Omega$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Result Summary:** > - Voltage Gain: $A_v = -163.2$ > - Voltage Gain in dB: **44.25 dB** > - Input Resistance: **656 Ω** > - Phase: **180° inverted** (inverting amplifier) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow of a CE Stage ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Vin AC Source] -->|through Cin| node2[Base Node of BJT] node3[VCC Battery] -->|grounded for AC| node4[AC Ground Reference] node5[R1 R2 Voltage Divider] -->|DC bias| node2 node2 -->|controls current ib| node6[BJT in Active Region] node6 -->|ic = beta ib| node7[Collector Node] node7 -->|through Cout| node8[Vout to Load RL] node9[RE with CE bypass] -->|shorted at AC| node4 node7 -->|through RC| node4 node10[hie input resistance] --- node2 node11[beta ib current source] --- node7 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cascaded Two-Stage CE Amplifier (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB srcA[Input Signal Source] --> capA[Coupling Capacitor Cin1] capA --> stageA[CE Amplifier Stage 1
BJT1 with RC1 RE1 CE1] stageA --> capB[Coupling Capacitor Cin2 equals Cout1] capB --> stageB[CE Amplifier Stage 2
BJT2 with RC2 RE2 CE2] stageB --> capC[Coupling Capacitor Cout2] capC --> loadA[Load Resistor RL] biasA[DC Bias VCC] -.-> stageA biasA -.-> stageB note1[DC Blocked Between Stages] --- capA note2[AC Signal Passes Freely] --- capB note3[180 Degree Phase Inversion Per Stage] --- stageA note4[Total Phase Shift Equals 360 Degrees = 0 Degrees] --- stageB ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — AC Equivalent Circuit Reduction Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Original CE Circuit with VCC and Capacitors] --> step1[Step 1: Identify Q Point IC and VCE] step1 --> step2[Step 2: Short all Coupling Capacitors Cin and Cout] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Short the Bypass Capacitor CE] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Replace VCC with AC Ground] step4 --> step5[Step 5: Replace BJT with hybrid pi model] step5 --> step6[Step 6: Calculate re equals 26mV divided by IE] step6 --> step7[Step 7: Calculate Rac equals RC parallel RL] step7 --> step8[Step 8: Apply formula Av equals minus Rac divided by re] step8 --> step9[Step 9: Calculate Rin equals R1 parallel R2 parallel hie] step9 --> step10[Step 10: Final Answers Av Rin Rout] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Components and Their AC Equivalents ```mermaid flowchart LR capX[Coupling Capacitor Cin] ---|AC equivalent| shortX[Short Circuit] capY[Coupling Capacitor Cout] ---|AC equivalent| shortY[Short Circuit] capZ[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE] ---|AC equivalent| shortZ[Short Circuit] vccX[DC Supply VCC] ---|AC equivalent| gndX[Ground] bjtX[BJT Transistor] ---|AC equivalent| modX[Small Signal Model] reX[Emitter Resistor RE] ---|with CE| bypX[Bypassed Shorted] reX2[Emitter Resistor RE] ---|without CE| actX[Active in AC Loop] rcX[Collector Resistor RC] ---|AC equivalent| racX[Parallel with RL forms Rac] ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above render the *topology* of the AC equivalent circuit. KTU 2024 board exams typically expect the **schematic diagram** of the AC equivalent circuit to be drawn by hand (using a hybrid-π model with $h_{ie}$ and the dependent current source $\beta i_b$). Use the diagrams above as a *guide* for your own neat schematic drawing. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the role of coupling capacitors and emitter bypass capacitor in a single-stage CE amplifier. Why are they replaced by short circuits during AC analysis?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Coupling Capacitors ($C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$):** Coupling capacitors serve two purposes in a CE amplifier: 1. **DC Blocking:** They prevent the DC bias of one stage from disturbing the DC operating point (Q-point) of the next stage. $C_{in}$ isolates the source's DC level from the base bias, and $C_{out}$ isolates the collector's DC voltage from the load. 2. **AC Coupling:** They allow the AC signal to pass from one stage to the next with minimal attenuation. The value is chosen so that $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} \le \dfrac{R}{10}$ at the lowest frequency of interest. **Emitter Bypass Capacitor ($C_E$):** The emitter resistor $R_E$ is required for DC thermal stability but causes severe AC signal attenuation (because the AC emitter current $i_e$ flowing through $R_E$ creates a degenerative voltage drop). The bypass capacitor $C_E$, connected in parallel with $R_E$, offers a low-impedance path ($X_{C_E} \approx 0$) to AC signals, effectively short-circuiting $R_E$ at signal frequencies. This restores the high voltage gain of the CE stage. **Why replaced by short circuits:** At signal frequencies, the reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} \to 0$, so the capacitor behaves as a wire. This simplifies the circuit for small-signal analysis by eliminating the reactive elements and leaving only resistive components and the linear BJT model. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Draw the AC equivalent circuit of a CE amplifier using the hybrid-π model. Write the expression for voltage gain with and without the bypass capacitor.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **AC Equivalent Circuit:** The original CE circuit is reduced by: (a) shorting $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$, (b) grounding $V_{CC}$, and (c) replacing the BJT with a hybrid-π model consisting of $r_\pi$ (between base and emitter) and a dependent current source $\beta i_b$ (from collector to emitter). The output is taken from the collector node. **Voltage Gain Expressions:** $$\text{With } C_E: \quad A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e}$$ $$\text{Without } C_E: \quad A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e + R_E}$$ The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** For the CE amplifier shown below, draw the complete AC equivalent circuit using the **hybrid-π model** and derive the expression for: (i) Voltage gain $A_v$ (ii) Input resistance $R_{in}$ Assume the emitter is fully bypassed by $C_E$. (7 marks) **(b)** A CE amplifier has $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $I_E = 1.2 \text{ mA}$, and load $R_L = 5.6 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) $r'_e$ and $h_{ie}$ (ii) Voltage gain $A_v$ in linear and dB scale (iii) Current gain $A_i$ (iv) Power gain $A_p$ in dB (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1: Draw the AC equivalent circuit** - Replace $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$ with wires. - Replace $V_{CC}$ with ground. - Replace the BJT with the hybrid-π model: $r_\pi$ between B and E, current source $\beta i_b$ from C to E. **Step 2: Input loop equation** With $C_E$ shorted, the emitter is at AC ground. The input voltage $v_i$ appears across $r_\pi$: $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi$$ **Step 3: Output loop equation** The collector current is $\beta i_b$, which flows through the effective AC load $R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L$, producing a 180° inverted output voltage: $$v_{out} = -\beta i_b \cdot (R_C \Vert R_L)$$ **Step 4: Voltage gain derivation** $$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta i_b (R_C \Vert R_L)}{i_b \, r_\pi}$$ Using $r_\pi = h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$: $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e}$$ **Step 5: Input resistance derivation** Looking into the base, we see $h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$. The total input resistance is the bias network in parallel with this: $$R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert h_{ie}$$ (7 marks awarded for the AC equivalent diagram + complete derivation with all five steps.) --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) $r'_e$ and $h_{ie}$:** $$r'_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})} = \dfrac{26}{1.2} = 21.67 \text{ }\Omega$$ $$h_{ie} = \beta r'_e = 120 \times 21.67 = 2600 \text{ }\Omega = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **(ii) Voltage gain $A_v$ in linear and dB:** $$R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 5.6}{4.7 + 5.6} = \dfrac{26.32}{10.3} = 2.555 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$A_v = -\dfrac{2555}{21.67} = -117.9$$ $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(117.9) = 20 \times 2.071 = 41.43 \text{ dB}$$ **(iii) Current gain $A_i$:** $$A_i = \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L} = 120 \times \dfrac{4.7}{4.7 + 5.6} = 120 \times 0.4563 = 54.76$$ **(iv) Power gain $A_p$ in dB:** $$A_p = A_v \times A_i = 117.9 \times 54.76 = 6456.2$$ $$A_p(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(6456.2) = 10 \times 3.81 = 38.1 \text{ dB}$$ > **Final Answers:** > - $r'_e = 21.67 \text{ }\Omega$ > - $h_{ie} = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$ > - $A_v = -117.9 \rightarrow 41.43 \text{ dB}$ > - $A_i = 54.76$ > - $A_p = 6456.2 \rightarrow 38.1 \text{ dB}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **h-parameter model** of a BJT. Show how it can be simplified into the **hybrid-π model** for the CE configuration. (7 marks) **(b)** A CE amplifier has the following specifications: $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $R_1 = 33$ kΩ, $R_2 = 10$ kΩ, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_E = 1$ kΩ, $R_L = 5$ kΩ, $\beta = 150$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V. The emitter is bypassed by $C_E$. Calculate: (i) The Q-point ($I_C$, $V_{CE}$) (ii) The voltage gain with and without bypass capacitor (iii) Compare and comment on the results (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — h-parameter Model (7 marks):** The h-parameter (hybrid parameter) model represents the BJT as a **two-port network** with four parameters: $h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$. - $h_{ie}$: Input impedance with output shorted (typical: 1–4 kΩ) - $h_{re}$: Reverse voltage ratio (typical: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$, often neglected) - $h_{fe}$: Forward current gain (≈ β, typical: 50–300) - $h_{oe}$: Output admittance (typical: 10–100 μS, often neglected) The **CE h-parameter equivalent circuit** has: - Input side: $h_{ie}$ in series, $h_{re} v_{ce}$ as a dependent voltage source. - Output side: A current source $h_{fe} i_b$ in parallel with $\dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}$. **Simplification to hybrid-π model:** 1. Neglect $h_{re}$ (since it is very small). 2. Neglect $\dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}$ (since $h_{oe}$ is very small). 3. Replace the input resistance $h_{ie}$ with $r_\pi = h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$. 4. The dependent current source becomes $\beta i_b$. This yields the **simplified hybrid-π model** used in most KTU problems. (7 marks) --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Q-point calculation:** Thevenin voltage at the base: $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 15 \cdot \dfrac{10}{33 + 10} = 15 \cdot \dfrac{10}{43} = 3.488 \text{ V}$$ Thevenin resistance: $$R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2 = \dfrac{33 \times 10}{33 + 10} = \dfrac{330}{43} = 7.674 \text{ k}\Omega$$ Applying KVL around the base-emitter loop: $$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$ Substitute $I_B = I_E / (1+\beta) = I_E / 151$: $$3.488 - 0.7 = I_E \left[ \dfrac{7674}{151} + 1000 \right]$$ $$2.788 = I_E [50.82 + 1000] = I_E (1050.82)$$ $$I_E = \dfrac{2.788}{1050.82} = 2.653 \text{ mA}$$ $$I_C \approx I_E = 2.653 \text{ mA}$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E) = 15 - 2.653 \times 5.7 = 15 - 15.12 = -0.12 \text{ V}$$ > This result indicates the transistor is **near saturation**. The student should comment that the design needs to be revised (e.g., increase $R_C$ or $R_E$) to keep the transistor in the active region. For a properly biased design, $V_{CE}$ should be around $V_{CC}/2 \approx 7.5$ V. However, we will proceed with the calculation as given. Let us re-compute assuming the Q-point was given implicitly. For pedagogical purposes, we proceed: $$r'_e = \dfrac{26}{2.653} = 9.8 \text{ }\Omega$$ **(ii) Voltage gain:** Effective AC resistance: $$R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 5}{4.7 + 5} = \dfrac{23.5}{9.7} = 2.42 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **With bypass capacitor:** $$A_v(\text{with}) = -\dfrac{R_{ac}}{r'_e} = -\dfrac{2420}{9.8} = -246.9$$ $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(246.9) = 47.85 \text{ dB}$$ **Without bypass capacitor:** $$A_v(\text{without}) = -\dfrac{R_{ac}}{r'_e + R_E} = -\dfrac{2420}{9.8 + 1000} = -\dfrac{2420}{1009.8} = -2.397$$ $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(2.397) = 7.6 \text{ dB}$$ **(iii) Comparison and Comment:** The voltage gain drops from **47.85 dB (with bypass)** to **7.6 dB (without bypass)** — a loss of **40.25 dB**, i.e., a factor of **~103** in linear scale. This dramatic reduction confirms that the bypass capacitor $C_E$ is **essential** for achieving high voltage gain in CE amplifiers. The emitter resistor $R_E$ provides DC stability at the cost of AC gain, and $C_E$ resolves this trade-off by providing a low-impedance path for AC signals. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$** → Lose 1 mark. The 180° phase inversion is a defining feature of CE. > 2. **Using $R_C$ instead of $R_C \Vert R_L$** when a load is connected → Lose 2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up $r'_e$ and $r_\pi$** → $r'_e = 26 \text{ mV} / I_E$, while $r_\pi = \beta r'_e = h_{ie}$. The input resistance formula uses $h_{ie}$, not $r'_e$. > 4. **Computing $A_p$ in dB using $20 \log$ instead of $10 \log$** → Lose 1 mark. Power uses $10 \log$, voltage/current use $20 \log$. > 5. **Forgetting to ground $V_{CC}$** in the AC equivalent → Lose 1 mark in diagram questions. > 6. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in the $r'_e = 26/I$ formula** → Minor error (since $I_C \approx I_E$ for $\beta \gg 1$, but a careful examiner will notice). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Coupling Capacitors** ($C_{in}, C_{out}$): **Block DC, pass AC**; isolate Q-points between stages. - **Bypass Capacitor** ($C_E$): **Shorts $R_E$ at AC**, restoring high gain while preserving DC stability. - **AC Equivalent Circuit Rules:** Short all capacitors, ground $V_{CC}$, replace BJT with small-signal model. - **Hybrid-π Model:** $r_\pi$ (= $h_{ie} = \beta r'_e$) at input, current source $\beta i_b$ at output, $r_o$ neglected. - **Key Gain Formula (bypassed):** $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e}$ - **Key Gain Formula (unbypassed):** $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \Vert R_L}{r'_e + R_E}$ - **Input Resistance (bypassed):** $R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert h_{ie}$ - **Output Resistance:** $R_{out} = R_C$ - **Current Gain (approximate):** $A_i \approx \beta$ - **Power Gain:** $A_p = A_v \cdot A_i$, in dB use $10 \log_{10}$. - **Voltage Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$. - **Dynamic Emitter Resistance:** $r'_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$ — derived from the *thermal voltage* $V_T \approx 26$ mV at room temperature (300 K). - **Design Rule for Capacitor Value:** $X_C \le \dfrac{R}{10}$ at the lowest frequency of interest. - **Phase Inversion:** The CE amplifier is an **inverting amplifier** with a 180° phase shift. - **Two Cascaded CE Stages:** The total phase shift is $180° \times 2 = 360° \equiv 0°$ (i.e., the output is in phase with the input for two stages). - **Why the Bypass Capacitor is "Bigger":** Since $C_E$ must satisfy $X_{C_E} \le R_E / 10$ and $R_E$ is usually large (1 kΩ), $C_E$ is typically the **largest** capacitor in the circuit (often 10–100 μF).

topics.title: Calculation of Amplifier Gains and Impedances Using h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Amplifier Gains and Impedances Using h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Parameters (h-parameters)** are a set of four small-signal, low-frequency parameters used to model the behaviour of a linear two-port network (such as a BJT amplifier) by mixing an impedance parameter at the input with admittance and gain parameters at the output. The word *hybrid* is used because the parameters are not of one single consistent unit type — they are a **hybrid** mixture of dimensions (Ω, Siemens, and pure ratios).

The general **h-parameter two-port network** is mathematically defined by the two linear equations:

$$
V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2
$$

$$
I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2
$$

Where the four parameters are:

| Symbol | Standard Name | Physical Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **$h_{11} = h_i$** | Input Impedance | Input impedance with output port short-circuited ($V_2 = 0$) | Ohms (Ω) |
| **$h_{12} = h_r$** | Reverse Voltage Ratio | Reverse open-circuit voltage gain with input open ($I_1 = 0$) | Dimensionless |
| **$h_{21} = h_f$** | Forward Current Gain | Short-circuit forward current gain with output shorted | Dimensionless |
| **$h_{22} = h_o$** | Output Admittance | Output admittance with input port open-circuited | Siemens (S) or mho |

For a BJT, an extra subscript identifies the configuration:
- **CE:** $h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$
- **CB:** $h_{ib}, h_{rb}, h_{fb}, h_{ob}$
- **CC:** $h_{ic}, h_{rc}, h_{fc}, h_{oc}$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Black Box Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Multilingual Translator Booth**
>
> Imagine a customs officer (the BJT) at a port. You cannot see inside his brain (the internal transistor physics of Ebers-Moll, etc.), but you can describe how he responds to two different "stimuli": (1) the question you ask him (input current) and (2) the noise from the speaker next door (output voltage feedback). The **h-parameters** are simply *four standardised measurements* — like height, weight, temperature, and pulse rate of a patient — that fully describe his behaviour *without* needing to know his internal anatomy. Doctors (engineers) use these four numbers to predict how he will respond to *any* future situation.

The reason the h-model is so popular for transistors is that **all four parameters are conveniently measured in the laboratory** using a simple AC signal generator, AC voltmeter, and AC ammeter — making it the *de-facto* standard taught in B.Tech curricula worldwide.

---

## 1.3 Typical Order of Magnitudes (Must Memorise)

> [!TIP]
> For a **standard small-signal silicon BJT** at room temperature ($I_C \approx 1\text{ mA}$):
> - $h_{ie} \approx 1\text{ k}\Omega$ to $4\text{ k}\Omega$
> - $h_{re} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ *(often taken as $\approx 0$)*
> - $h_{fe} \approx 50$ to $500$ *(typically 100)*
> - $h_{oe} \approx 25\text{ }\mu\text{S}$ *(i.e. $1/h_{oe} \approx 40\text{ k}\Omega$, often taken as $\approx 0$)*
>
> These approximations simplify all derivations drastically and are the **backbone of KTU numerical problems**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit

The defining equations $V_1 = h_i I_1 + h_r V_2$ and $I_2 = h_f I_1 + h_o V_2$ are realised by the equivalent circuit as follows:

- **Input loop** contains an impedance $h_i$ in series with a **voltage-controlled voltage source** (VCVS) of value $h_r V_2$.
- **Output port** contains a **current-controlled current source** (CCCS) of value $h_f I_1$ in parallel with an admittance $h_o$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use h-parameters?**
> 1. They are **easy to measure** (short/open at ports).
> 2. They are **readily available** from transistor manufacturer datasheets.
> 3. They give **accurate results** at low-to-mid audio frequencies.
> 4. The equivalent circuit uses **independent dependent sources** that clearly separate input and output effects.

---

## 2.2 Approximations Used in KTU Numerical Problems

To make manual calculations feasible, the KTU examination routinely uses the following **simplified h-model assumptions**:

| Approximation | Justification | Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h_{re} \approx 0$ | Internal feedback is negligible ($\sim 10^{-4}$) | Input loop is purely $h_{ie}$ |
| $h_{oe} \approx 0$ | Output admittance is negligible ($1/h_{oe} \gg R_L$) | Output is purely a current source $h_{fe}I_b$ |
| $1 + h_{oe} R_L \approx 1$ | Since $h_{oe} R_L$ is very small | Current gain simplifies to $-h_{fe}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU question papers, always **read the problem statement first**. If the problem explicitly gives the value of $h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$, you **must use them**. The approximations are only valid when the problem says *"neglect $h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$"*.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This!)

The following table is the **complete cheat sheet** for calculating gains and impedances of CE, CB, and CC amplifiers using the h-parameter model. All quantities below use $R_L$ = load resistance and $R_S$ = source resistance.

### Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier

| Quantity | Exact Formula (with $h_{re}, h_{oe}$) | Simplified Formula ($h_{re} = h_{oe} = 0$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Current Gain $A_I$** | $A_I = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$ | $A_I = -h_{fe}$ |
| **Voltage Gain $A_V$** | $A_V = \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie} + \Delta h \cdot R_L}$ | $A_V = \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie}}$ |
| **Input Impedance $Z_i$** | $Z_i = h_{ie} + h_{re} A_V R_L$ | $Z_i = h_{ie}$ |
| **Output Impedance $Z_o$** | $Z_o = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe} + \dfrac{1}{R_L}}$ | $Z_o = \infty$ |
| **Power Gain $A_P$** | $A_P = A_V \cdot A_I$ | $A_P = \dfrac{h_{fe}^2 R_L}{h_{ie}}$ |

> Where **$\Delta h = h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}$** (the h-parameter determinant).

### Common-Base (CB) Amplifier

| Quantity | Simplified Formula (most common in KTU) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Current Gain $A_I$** | $A_I = h_{fb} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + h_{ob} R_L} \approx h_{fb}$ |
| **Voltage Gain $A_V$** | $A_V = \dfrac{h_{fb} R_L}{h_{ib}}$ |
| **Input Impedance $Z_i$** | $Z_i \approx h_{ib}$ (very low, typically $20\text{–}50\text{ }\Omega$) |
| **Output Impedance $Z_o$** | $Z_o \approx \dfrac{1}{h_{ob}}$ (very high) |

### Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower

| Quantity | Simplified Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Current Gain $A_I$** | $A_I = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_E} \approx 1 + h_{fe}$ |
| **Voltage Gain $A_V$** | $A_V \approx 1$ (slightly less than unity) |
| **Input Impedance $Z_i$** | $Z_i \approx h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_E$ (very high) |
| **Output Impedance $Z_o$** | $Z_o \approx \dfrac{R_S + h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}$ (very low) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The h-parameter model is the **industry standard for BJT small-signal analysis** because:
> - It directly uses datasheet quantities — no need to extract $r_\pi, g_m, r_o$ from a vendor-specific SPICE model.
> - It separates *reverse* and *forward* effects, which is critical in **RF amplifier design** where feedback (Miller effect) is non-negligible.
> - It is the **foundation for the re-model and hybrid-π model**, both of which are derived from h-parameters.
> - It is the **only model specified by IEEE Standard 218** for BJT characterisation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Two-Port h-Parameter Model Derivation

Consider a linear two-port network. We choose to express **input voltage $V_1$** and **output current $I_2$** as the dependent variables, in terms of **input current $I_1$** and **output voltage $V_2$** (the independent variables).

### Step 1: Express $V_1$ as a function of $I_1$ and $V_2$

By the principle of superposition for a linear network, $V_1$ depends linearly on both $I_1$ and $V_2$:

$$
V_1 = (\text{constant}_1) I_1 + (\text{constant}_2) V_2
$$

Let the two constants be $h_{11}$ and $h_{12}$:

$$
V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2
$$

### Step 2: Express $I_2$ as a function of $I_1$ and $V_2$

Similarly, by superposition:

$$
I_2 = (\text{constant}_3) I_1 + (\text{constant}_4) V_2
$$

Let the constants be $h_{21}$ and $h_{22}$:

$$
I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2
$$

### Step 3: Define each h-parameter

Setting the independent variables to zero in turn gives the **definition of each h-parameter**:

$$
h_{11} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{V_2 = 0} \quad \text{(input impedance, output port shorted)}
$$

$$
h_{12} = \left. \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0} \quad \text{(reverse voltage ratio, input port open)}
$$

$$
h_{21} = \left. \frac{I_2}{I_1} \right|_{V_2 = 0} \quad \text{(forward current gain, output port shorted)}
$$

$$
h_{22} = \left. \frac{I_2}{V_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0} \quad \text{(output admittance, input port open)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CE Amplifier Formulas (with Full Analysis)

Consider a **Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier** with load $R_L$ connected at the output. The h-parameter equivalent circuit is:

- Input side: Input voltage $V_1$ drives the impedance $h_{ie}$ in series with a VCVS $h_{re} V_2$.
- Output side: A CCCS $h_{fe} I_1$ feeds into the parallel combination of $1/h_{oe}$ and $R_L$.

### Derivation 1: Current Gain $A_I$

The current gain is defined as:

$$
A_I = \frac{I_L}{I_1} = \frac{I_2}{I_1}
$$

From the output node KCL, the current $I_2$ flowing out of the network divides between $1/h_{oe}$ and $R_L$:

$$
I_2 = -I_L = \frac{-V_2}{R_L}
$$

(We use the sign convention that $I_2$ flows *into* the port, so the load current $I_L$ is $-I_2$.)

The h-parameter equation gives:

$$
I_2 = h_{fe} I_1 + h_{oe} V_2
$$

Substituting $V_2 = -I_2 R_L$:

$$
I_2 = h_{fe} I_1 + h_{oe} (-I_2 R_L)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
I_2 + h_{oe} R_L I_2 = h_{fe} I_1
$$

$$
I_2 (1 + h_{oe} R_L) = h_{fe} I_1
$$

$$
\boxed{A_I = \frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}}
$$

When the problem neglects $h_{oe}$:

$$
A_I \approx h_{fe}
$$

### Derivation 2: Voltage Gain $A_V$

The voltage gain is defined as:

$$
A_V = \frac{V_2}{V_1}
$$

We already have $V_2 = -I_2 R_L$. Using the input loop KVL:

$$
V_1 = h_{ie} I_1 + h_{re} V_2
$$

We know $I_1 = I_2 / h_{fe}$ (ignoring $h_{oe}$ for simplicity, otherwise $I_1 = I_2(1 + h_{oe}R_L)/h_{fe}$).

Substituting:

$$
V_1 = h_{ie} \cdot \frac{I_2}{h_{fe}} + h_{re} V_2
$$

Multiplying both sides by $h_{fe}$:

$$
h_{fe} V_1 = h_{ie} I_2 + h_{re} h_{fe} V_2
$$

Since $I_2 = -V_2 / R_L$:

$$
h_{fe} V_1 = -\frac{h_{ie} V_2}{R_L} + h_{re} h_{fe} V_2
$$

$$
h_{fe} V_1 = V_2 \left( h_{re} h_{fe} - \frac{h_{ie}}{R_L} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{1}{h_{fe}} \left( h_{re} h_{fe} - \frac{h_{ie}}{R_L} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = h_{re} - \frac{h_{ie}}{h_{fe} R_L}
$$

Inverting:

$$
\boxed{A_V = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie} - h_{re} h_{fe} R_L} = \frac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie} + \Delta h \cdot R_L}}
$$

where the negative sign confirms **CE is an inverting amplifier**, and $\Delta h = h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}$ is the h-parameter determinant.

When neglecting $h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$:

$$
A_V \approx \frac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie}}
$$

### Derivation 3: Input Impedance $Z_i$

The input impedance is defined as $V_1 / I_1$ looking into the input port (with $R_L$ connected):

$$
Z_i = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{h_{ie} I_1 + h_{re} V_2}{I_1} = h_{ie} + h_{re} \frac{V_2}{I_1}
$$

We have $V_2/I_1 = A_V \cdot (V_1 / I_1) = A_V Z_i$. Substituting back:

$$
Z_i = h_{ie} + h_{re} A_V Z_i
$$

$$
Z_i (1 - h_{re} A_V) = h_{ie}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_i = \frac{h_{ie}}{1 - h_{re} A_V} = h_{ie} + h_{re} A_V R_L}
$$

> When $h_{re} \approx 0$: $\quad Z_i \approx h_{ie}$

### Derivation 4: Output Impedance $Z_o$

The output impedance is found by setting the source $V_1 = 0$ (i.e. $I_1 = 0$ for an ideal source), removing $R_L$, and looking into the output port:

With $I_1 = 0$, the CCCS $h_{fe} I_1 = 0$ becomes an **open circuit**. The only thing left at the output is the admittance $h_{oe}$:

$$
Z_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}}
$$

When $R_L$ is reconnected, the effective load seen by the next stage is:

$$
Z_{o,\text{eff}} = R_L \Vert \frac{1}{h_{oe}} = \frac{R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}
$$

> When $h_{oe} \approx 0$: $\quad Z_o \to \infty$

### Derivation 5: Power Gain $A_P$

$$
A_P = A_V \cdot A_I
$$

Substituting the simplified formulas:

$$
A_P = \left( \frac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie}} \right) \cdot h_{fe} = \frac{h_{fe}^2 R_L}{h_{ie}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Matrix — Special Cases

### Special Case A: $R_S$ (Source Resistance) is Non-Zero

The **overall voltage gain** $A_{VS}$ from source to load is reduced by the voltage divider at the input:

$$
A_{VS} = A_V \cdot \frac{Z_i}{Z_i + R_S}
$$

### Special Case B: CE with Emitter Bypass Capacitor

When the emitter resistor $R_E$ is **fully bypassed** by a capacitor, the CE formulas apply directly with $R_L$ being the AC load at the collector.

When the emitter resistor is **unbypassed** ($R_E$ fully present in the AC model), the input resistance becomes:

$$
Z_i = h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_E
$$

And the voltage gain becomes:

$$
A_V = \frac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_E}
$$

> This trade-off — *gain decreases, but input impedance and linearity improve* — is the **central design tension** in BJT amplifier design and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Conversion Between Configurations (Critical for KTU)

Given CE parameters, the CB and CC parameters can be obtained using **approximate conversion formulas** (valid when $h_{oe} R_L$ is small):

$$
h_{ib} \approx \frac{h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}
$$

$$
h_{fb} \approx \frac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{fe}}
$$

$$
h_{rb} \approx \frac{h_{ie} h_{oe}}{1 + h_{fe}} - h_{re}
$$

$$
h_{ob} \approx \frac{h_{oe}}{1 + h_{fe}}
$$

$$
h_{ic} \approx h_{ie}
$$

$$
h_{fc} \approx -(1 + h_{fe})
$$

$$
h_{rc} \approx 1
$$

$$
h_{oc} \approx h_{oe}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Note the **sign convention**: $h_{fb}$ and $h_{fc}$ are **negative** in many textbooks. The student must check the sign convention used in their KTU-recommended textbook (Boylestad, Millman Halkias, or Salivahanan) and apply it consistently.
