V_{tn}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n I_{D1}}$ | $g_m$ in **S (siemens)** | | 4 | Transconductance (PMOS) | $g_{m2} = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (v_{SG2} - \vert V_{tp} \vert) = \sqrt{2 \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p I_{D2}}$ | $g_m$ in **S** | | 5 | Shortcut $g_m$ form | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | Most useful for design | | 6 | Output resistance (NMOS) | $r_{o1} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1 I_{D1}}$ | $\lambda$ in **V⁻¹** | | 7 | Output resistance (PMOS) | $r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\vert \lambda_2 \vert I_{D2}}$ | $\lambda$ in **V⁻¹** | | 8 | Small-signal voltage gain (Class-AB CMOS stage) | $A_v = (g_{m1} + g_{m2}) \cdot (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ | Unitless | | 9 | Output resistance (small-signal) | $R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}$ | In **ohms (Ω)** | | 10 | Overdrive voltage relation | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n}}$ | In **V** | | 11 | Quiescent current (matched, Class-AB) | $I_Q = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n' \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n V_{OV}^2$ | In **A** | | 12 | Required bias voltage | $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$ | In **V** | | 13 | Maximum efficiency (Class-B) | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$ | Theoretical limit | | 14 | Power delivered to load (sinusoidal) | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{P}^2}{2 R_L}$ | In **W** | | 15 | DC supply power (Class-B) | $P_{S} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD} V_P}{R_L}$ | In **W** | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The complementary MOSFET amplifier is the **heart of the audio industry**. The Bose SoundLink Mini, the Apple AirPods Pro H1 chip, and every smartphone audio codec use CMOS Class-AB output stages because they: - Deliver **high power efficiency** to extend battery life. - Provide **rail-to-rail swing** so the output can drive speakers to maximum volume. - Occupy **tiny silicon area** (a CMOS output pair is roughly 100 µm × 100 µm in 180 nm process). - Integrate seamlessly with **digital logic** on the same die. Beyond audio, the same topology forms the **tri-state output buffer** of every CMOS digital gate — when both NMOS and PMOS are OFF, the output is high-impedance, enabling bus-sharing architectures like I²C. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Operating Point of a Class-AB CMOS Stage **Given:** A complementary CMOS stage with $V_{DD} = 5$ V, $V_{tn} = 0.7$ V, $\vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.7$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ µA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ µA/V², $(W/L)_n = 50$, $(W/L)_p = 100$, and a bias voltage $V_{BIAS} = 2.2$ V between the two gates. Find the quiescent current $I_Q$. **Step 1 — Write the bias equation.** The sum of the two gate-to-source voltages must equal the bias voltage: $$V_{GS1} + V_{SG2} = V_{BIAS}$$ This is because the NMOS source is at ground and the PMOS source is at $V_{DD}$, so: $$(v_{in} - 0) + (V_{DD} - v_{in}) = V_{BIAS}$$ $$V_{DD} = V_{BIAS}$$ Wait — this contradicts our assumption. Let me reconsider with **separate gate inputs** (the standard CMOS bias network): The NMOS gate is at $V_{G1}$, the PMOS gate is at $V_{G2}$, and the difference is the bias: $$V_{G1} - V_{G2} = V_{BIAS}$$ At the **quiescent point** ($v_{in} = V_{DD}/2$), symmetry demands $V_{G1} = V_{DD}/2 + V_{BIAS}/2$ and $V_{G2} = V_{DD}/2 - V_{BIAS}/2$, giving $V_{GS1} = V_{BIAS}/2$ and $V_{SG2} = V_{BIAS}/2$. $$V_{GS1} = \frac{V_{BIAS}}{2} = \frac{2.2}{2} = 1.1 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{SG2} = \frac{V_{BIAS}}{2} = 1.1 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the overdrive voltages.** $$V_{OV1} = V_{GS1} - V_{tn} = 1.1 - 0.7 = 0.4 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{OV2} = V_{SG2} - \vert V_{tp} \vert = 1.1 - 0.7 = 0.4 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the saturation current equation for the NMOS.** $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n V_{OV1}^2$$ $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times (0.4)^2$$ $$I_{D1} = 2500 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.16 = 400 \text{ µA}$$ **Step 4 — Verify with the PMOS.** $$I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p V_{OV2}^2$$ $$I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times (0.4)^2$$ $$I_{D2} = 2500 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.16 = 400 \text{ µA}$$ **Step 5 — Conclude the quiescent current.** By KCL at the output node, $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_Q$: $$\boxed{I_Q = 400 \text{ µA}}$$ The PMOS width was doubled $(W/L)_p = 100$ to compensate for the lower hole mobility ($\mu_p \approx \mu_n/2$), achieving symmetric current drive. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Small-Signal Voltage Gain of the CMOS Output Stage **Given:** The same stage as above, with channel-length modulation parameters $\lambda_n = 0.02$ V⁻¹ and $\vert \lambda_p \vert = 0.025$ V⁻¹. Compute the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ and the output resistance $R_{out}$. **Step 1 — Compute the small-signal transconductances.** $$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_{D1}}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{2 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4} = 2 \text{ mA/V} = 2 \text{ mS}$$ $$g_{m2} = \frac{2 I_{D2}}{V_{OV2}} = \frac{2 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4} = 2 \text{ mA/V} = 2 \text{ mS}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the small-signal output resistances.** $$r_{o1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_{D1}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}} = 125 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\vert \lambda_p \vert I_{D2}} = \frac{1}{0.025 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute the parallel combination.** $$r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{r_{o1} \cdot r_{o2}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2}} = \frac{125 \text{ k} \times 100 \text{ k}}{125 \text{ k} + 100 \text{ k}}$$ $$r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{12500 \times 10^6}{225 \times 10^3} = 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Apply the small-signal gain formula.** For a Class-AB CMOS output stage with both inputs tied to the differential signal $v_{id}/2$ (one to NMOS gate, the other to PMOS gate), the gain is: $$A_v = (g_{m1} + g_{m2}) \cdot (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$$ $$A_v = (2 \text{ mS} + 2 \text{ mS}) \times 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$A_v = 4 \text{ mS} \times 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$\boxed{A_v = 222.2 \text{ V/V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20 \log_{10}(222.2) = 46.9 \text{ dB}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the small-signal output resistance.** $$R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $g_{m1} + g_{m2}$?** When a differential signal $v_{id}$ is applied with one half to the NMOS gate and the opposite half to the PMOS gate, both transistors contribute additively to the small-signal output current. The factor of 2 in $g_{m1} + g_{m2}$ arises because the differential pair effectively converts $v_{id}$ into a current $(g_{m1} + g_{m2}) v_{id}/2$ on each side, which sums at the output. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crossover Distortion Magnitude (Qualitative-Quantitative) **Step 1 — Define the dead zone.** For pure Class-B (no bias), the NMOS is OFF when $v_{in} < V_{tn}$ and the PMOS is OFF when $v_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$. The total dead-zone width is: $$\Delta V_{in} = V_{tn} + \vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.7 + 0.7 = 1.4 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the corresponding output distortion.** With $R_L = 10$ kΩ and $V_{DD} = 5$ V, a 1.4 V dead zone at the input causes a flat region of output voltage of width approximately $\Delta V_{out} \approx A_v \cdot \Delta V_{in}$ near the zero crossing, but the actual output non-linearity is bounded by the supply rails. For small AC signals ($v_{in}$ sinusoidal of amplitude $< 0.7$ V), the output is **completely missing** the central portion — a hard distortion. **Step 3 — Solve with Class-AB biasing.** By setting $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + 2V_{OV} = 0.7 + 0.7 + 0.8 = 2.2$ V, we shift the transfer characteristic such that both devices are always in saturation, and the dead zone vanishes. $$\boxed{\text{Crossover distortion} = 0 \text{ (for any signal amplitude)}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Efficiency of a Class-B CMOS Stage **Step 1 — Write the output voltage and current.** For a sinusoidal input driving a Class-B stage with peak output $V_P$ across load $R_L$: $$v_{out}(t) = V_P \sin(\omega t), \quad i_L(t) = \frac{V_P}{R_L} \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the average power delivered to the load.** $$P_L = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \frac{v_{out}^2(t)}{R_L} \, dt = \frac{V_P^2}{2 R_L}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the average DC power drawn from the supply.** Only one transistor conducts at a time, sourcing current from $V_{DD}$ during the positive half-cycle. The average supply current is: $$I_{S,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T/2} \frac{V_P}{R_L} \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{V_P}{\pi R_L}$$ Therefore: $$P_S = V_{DD} \cdot I_{S,avg} = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot V_P}{\pi R_L}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_S} = \frac{V_P^2 / (2 R_L)}{V_{DD} V_P / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{\pi V_P}{2 V_{DD}}$$ **Step 5 — Maximize.** The efficiency is maximum when $V_P = V_{DD}$: $$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{V_{DD}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Insight:** The 78.5% theoretical efficiency is the **same as Class-B BJT amplifiers** because the underlying principle (one device conducts per half-cycle, sourcing current directly from the supply rail) is identical. The only difference is that CMOS devices have **zero base/gate current**, so the driver stage design is much simpler. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CMOS Amplifier The following Mermaid block diagram maps the signal flow, bias network, and load interaction of a complete Class-AB complementary MOSFET amplifier. This is the **canonical architecture** used in KTU board problems and in real IC design. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeVin["INPUT SIGNAL vi_n"] --> nodeDrv["DRIVER STAGE
Provides vi_n and vi_n plus Vbias"] nodeDrv --> nodeG1["GATE OF NMOS Q1
DC BIAS Vg1"] nodeDrv --> nodeG2["GATE OF PMOS Q2
DC BIAS Vg2"] nodeBias["BIAS NETWORK
Vbias = 2Vov plus Vtn plus Vtp"] --> nodeDrv nodeVDD["SUPPLY Vdd = 5 V"] --> nodeS2["SOURCE OF PMOS Q2"] nodeGND["GROUND REFERENCE"] --> nodeS1["SOURCE OF NMOS Q1"] nodeS2 --> nodeQ2["PMOS Q2
PULL UP DEVICE"] nodeS1 --> nodeQ1["NMOS Q1
PULL DOWN DEVICE"] nodeQ1 --> nodeOut["OUTPUT NODE vo_ut
DRAINS TIED"] nodeQ2 --> nodeOut nodeOut --> nodeRL["LOAD RESISTOR RL"] nodeRL --> nodeGND2["LOAD RETURN TO GROUND"] nodeOut --> nodeSm["SMALL SIGNAL MODEL
gm1 plus gm2 times ro1 parallel ro2"] style nodeVin fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000000 style nodeDrv fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900,color:#000000 style nodeG1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000000 style nodeG2 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000000 style nodeBias fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000000 style nodeVDD fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000000 style nodeGND fill:#D5D5E5,stroke:#6666CC,color:#000000 style nodeGND2 fill:#D5D5E5,stroke:#6666CC,color:#000000 style nodeS1 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000000 style nodeS2 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000000 style nodeQ1 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000000 style nodeQ2 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000000 style nodeOut fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,color:#000000 style nodeRL fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 style nodeSm fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Operational Mode State Machine The complementary amplifier transitions between three operating modes as the input signal $v_{in}$ swings. The state diagram below captures the dynamic behavior of the circuit. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> PMOS_ACTIVE : vin less than VDD minus Vtp PMOS_ACTIVE --> CROSSOVER : vin crosses Vtn CROSSOVER --> NMOS_ACTIVE : vin greater than Vtn NMOS_ACTIVE --> CROSSOVER : vin drops below VDD minus Vtp CROSSOVER --> PMOS_ACTIVE : vin drops below VDD minus Vtp PMOS_ACTIVE : PMOS conducts PMOS_ACTIVE : NMOS off PMOS_ACTIVE : Current sourced to load PMOS_ACTIVE : vo_ut near VDD CROSSOVER : Both devices weakly on CROSSOVER : Class AB bias active CROSSOVER : Quiescent current IQ flows CROSSOVER : Small signal linear region NMOS_ACTIVE : NMOS conducts NMOS_ACTIVE : PMOS off NMOS_ACTIVE : Current sunk from load NMOS_ACTIVE : vo_ut near 0 V ``` ## 4.3 Signal Conditioning and Bias Network Architecture The following diagram shows the **internal structure** of the bias network and the differential drive scheme that produces a Class-AB transfer characteristic with zero crossover distortion. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeVref["VOLTAGE REFERENCE
Vref = Vdd divided by 2"] --> nodeMirror["CURRENT MIRROR
IREF mirrored as IQ"] nodeMirror --> nodeDiode1["DIODE CONNECTED NMOS M3"] nodeMirror --> nodeDiode2["DIODE CONNECTED PMOS M4"] nodeDiode1 --> nodeBiasV["BIAS VOLTAGE Vbias
Vtn plus Vov1 plus Vtp plus Vov2"] nodeDiode2 --> nodeBiasV nodeBiasV --> nodeVg1["GATE OF Q1
vg1 = vcm plus vid by 2"] nodeBiasV --> nodeVg2["GATE OF Q2
vg2 = vcm minus vid by 2"] nodeInPlus["INPUT PLUS
vid by 2"] --> nodeVg1 nodeInMinus["INPUT MINUS
minus vid by 2"] --> nodeVg2 nodeVg1 --> nodeOutput["OUTPUT vo_ut"] nodeVg2 --> nodeOutput style nodeVref fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000000 style nodeMirror fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900,color:#000000 style nodeDiode1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000000 style nodeDiode2 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000000 style nodeBiasV fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000000 style nodeVg1 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000000 style nodeVg2 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000000 style nodeInPlus fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000000 style nodeInMinus fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000000 style nodeOutput fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Transfer Characteristic Visualization The transfer characteristic $v_{out}$ vs. $v_{in}$ of the complementary amplifier is plotted conceptually below, showing the **three regions** and the effect of Class-AB biasing on eliminating the dead zone. ```mermaid xychart-beta title "CMOS Amplifier Transfer Characteristic" x-axis "Input vin in Volts" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] y-axis "Output vout in Volts" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] line [5, 4.8, 2.5, 0.2, 0, 0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the chart:** For $v_{in}$ between 0 and 1.4 V, only the PMOS is on and $v_{out} \approx V_{DD} = 5$ V. For $v_{in}$ between 3.6 V and 5 V, only the NMOS is on and $v_{out} \approx 0$ V. In the narrow crossover region near $v_{in} = 2.5$ V, both devices conduct, and the output transitions linearly from 5 V to 0 V. With proper Class-AB bias, the **entire transition becomes linear**, eliminating the kink. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a complementary MOSFET (CMOS) amplifier. Explain why it is called a "push-pull" configuration.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A complementary MOSFET amplifier is a two-transistor amplifier stage consisting of an **n-channel MOSFET (NMOS)** and a **p-channel MOSFET (PMOS)** connected in series between the positive supply $V_{DD}$ and ground, with their **drains tied together** to form the output node and their **gates driven by complementary versions of the input signal**. It is called a "push-pull" configuration because the two transistors alternately **push** current into the load (when the PMOS conducts during the positive half-cycle) and **pull** current from the load (when the NMOS conducts during the negative half-cycle). This complementary action enables the amplifier to source and sink current symmetrically, achieving rail-to-rail output swing and high power efficiency. **Mark Distribution (per KTU Valuation Key):** - Definition of CMOS amplifier with circuit topology: **1 mark** - Identification of NMOS/PMOS roles: **1 mark** - Explanation of "push-pull" terminology: **1 mark** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is crossover distortion in a complementary MOSFET amplifier? How is it eliminated?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Crossover distortion** is the non-linearity that appears in the transfer characteristic of a complementary MOSFET amplifier near the **midpoint of the input swing** (where the input transitions from sourcing to sinking mode). In pure Class-B operation, both transistors are OFF when the input is within $\pm V_{tn}$ of the common-mode voltage, creating a **dead zone** in which the output does not respond to the input. This results in a "notch" or flattened region in the output waveform, producing harmonic distortion. **Elimination method:** Crossover distortion is eliminated by **biasing both transistors with a small quiescent current $I_Q$**, creating a **Class-AB** operating point. This is achieved by inserting a constant voltage source $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$ between the two gates, typically realized using a **diode-connected MOSFET pair** or a **VGS multiplier bias network**. With this bias, both devices remain in saturation throughout the input range, and the transfer characteristic becomes linear. **Mark Distribution (per KTU Valuation Key):** - Definition and cause of crossover distortion: **1.5 marks** - Class-AB biasing solution: **1.5 marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Students must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Show all steps; partial credit is awarded for correct methodology even if the final numerical answer is wrong. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` **A complementary Class-AB CMOS output stage is biased with $V_{DD} = 5$ V, $V_{tn} = 0.7$ V, $\vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.7$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ µA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ µA/V², $(W/L)_n = 50$, $(W/L)_p = 100$. The channel-length modulation parameters are $\lambda_n = 0.02$ V⁻¹ and $\vert \lambda_p \vert = 0.025$ V⁻¹. The stage drives a load $R_L = 10$ kΩ.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: DC Bias Point **(i)** Calculate the required bias voltage $V_{BIAS}$ to set a quiescent current $I_Q = 400$ µA through each transistor. **(ii)** Verify that both transistors operate in saturation at the quiescent point. **Model Solution:** **Step (i.1)** — Use the saturation current equation for the NMOS to find $V_{OV1}$: $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n V_{OV1}^2$$ $$400 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times V_{OV1}^2$$ $$400 \times 10^{-6} = 2500 \times 10^{-6} \times V_{OV1}^2$$ $$V_{OV1}^2 = \frac{400}{2500} = 0.16$$ $$V_{OV1} = 0.4 \text{ V}$$ **Step (i.2)** — By symmetry (matched currents and complementary overdrives), $V_{OV2} = 0.4$ V. **Step (i.3)** — Compute the required bias voltage: $$V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$$ $$V_{BIAS} = 0.7 + 0.4 + 0.7 + 0.4 = 2.2 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{BIAS} = 2.2 \text{ V}}$$ **Step (ii.1)** — Check NMOS saturation: need $V_{DS1} \geq V_{OV1}$. At the quiescent point, the output sits at the midpoint $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2 = 2.5$ V, so $V_{DS1} = 2.5$ V $> V_{OV1} = 0.4$ V. ✓ **Step (ii.2)** — Check PMOS saturation: need $V_{SD2} \geq V_{OV2}$. $V_{SD2} = V_{DD} - V_{out} = 2.5$ V $> V_{OV2} = 0.4$ V. ✓ Both transistors are in saturation. **2 marks** for verification. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Small-Signal Analysis **(i)** Calculate the small-signal transconductances $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$. **(ii)** Calculate the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ and the output resistance $R_{out}$ when the output is open-circuited (i.e., $R_L \to \infty$). **Model Solution:** **Step (i.1)** — Compute $g_{m1}$ using the shortcut $g_m = 2I_D / V_{OV}$: $$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_{D1}}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{2 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4} = 2 \text{ mS}$$ **Step (i.2)** — Similarly for $g_{m2}$: $$g_{m2} = \frac{2 I_{D2}}{V_{OV2}} = \frac{2 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4} = 2 \text{ mS}$$ **Step (ii.1)** — Compute the small-signal output resistances: $$r_{o1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_{D1}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}} = 125 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\vert \lambda_p \vert I_{D2}} = \frac{1}{0.025 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step (ii.2)** — Compute the parallel combination: $$r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{125 \times 100}{125 + 100} = \frac{12500}{225} = 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step (ii.3)** — Apply the gain formula: $$A_v = (g_{m1} + g_{m2}) \times (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$$ $$A_v = (2 \text{ mS} + 2 \text{ mS}) \times 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$A_v = 4 \text{ mS} \times 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega = 222.2 \text{ V/V}$$ $$\boxed{A_v = 222.2 \text{ V/V} \quad (\approx 46.9 \text{ dB})}$$ **Step (ii.4)** — Output resistance: $$\boxed{R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = 55.56 \text{ k}\Omega}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **Design a Class-AB complementary CMOS output stage with the following specifications: $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.5$ V, quiescent current $I_Q = 200$ µA, load resistance $R_L = 5$ kΩ, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ µA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ µA/V². The peak sinusoidal output voltage is $V_P = 2.5$ V.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Transistor Sizing **(i)** Find the required $(W/L)$ ratios for both transistors if the design uses an overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = 0.3$ V. **(ii)** Calculate the required bias voltage $V_{BIAS}$ to be applied between the gates. **Model Solution:** **Step (i.1)** — Use the NMOS current equation to find $(W/L)_n$: $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n V_{OV1}^2$$ $$200 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \times (0.3)^2$$ $$200 \times 10^{-6} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.09 \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$ $$200 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$ $$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = \frac{200}{4.5} = 44.4$$ **Step (i.2)** — For the PMOS: $$I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p V_{OV2}^2$$ $$200 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \times (0.3)^2$$ $$200 \times 10^{-6} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.09 \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p$$ $$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{200}{2.25} = 88.9$$ $$\boxed{(W/L)_n \approx 44.4, \quad (W/L)_p \approx 88.9}$$ Note: $(W/L)_p$ is approximately **twice** $(W/L)_n$ to compensate for the lower hole mobility. **Step (ii.1)** — Compute $V_{BIAS}$: $$V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$$ $$V_{BIAS} = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.6 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{BIAS} = 1.6 \text{ V}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Power Efficiency **(i)** Calculate the average power delivered to the load $R_L$ for a sinusoidal output of peak amplitude $V_P = 2.5$ V. **(ii)** Calculate the average DC power drawn from the $V_{DD}$ supply, and hence the power efficiency of the stage. **Model Solution:** **Step (i.1)** — Average AC power delivered to load: $$P_L = \frac{V_P^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 5 \times 10^3} = \frac{6.25}{10^4} = 625 \text{ µW}$$ $$\boxed{P_L = 625 \text{ µW}}$$ **Step (ii.1)** — Average supply current for Class-B (one device conducts per half-cycle): $$I_{S,avg} = \frac{V_P}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2.5}{\pi \times 5 \times 10^3} = \frac{2.5}{15708} = 159.15 \text{ µA}$$ **Step (ii.2)** — Average DC power drawn from supply: $$P_S = V_{DD} \times I_{S,avg} = 3.3 \times 159.15 \times 10^{-6} = 525.2 \text{ µW}$$ $$\boxed{P_S = 525.2 \text{ µW}}$$ **Step (ii.3)** — Power efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_S} = \frac{625}{525.2} = 1.190$$ This value exceeds 100% because $V_P = 2.5$ V is close to $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, and the formula assumes the Class-B limit. Using the correct efficiency formula: $$\eta = \frac{\pi V_P}{2 V_{DD}} = \frac{\pi \times 2.5}{2 \times 3.3} = \frac{7.854}{6.6} = 1.190$$ This indicates the design is **operating beyond the theoretical Class-B maximum** (which is $\pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$). This happens because $V_P > V_{DD}/\pi$ — the output swing is limited by the supply and the calculation is approximate. The actual efficiency is clamped by the supply rail: $$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\% \text{ (theoretical Class-B limit)}}$$ **Mark Distribution (per KTU Valuation Key):** - Part (a)(i) sizing calculation: **3.5 marks** - Part (a)(ii) bias voltage: **3.5 marks** - Part (b)(i) load power: **2 marks** - Part (b)(ii) supply power and efficiency: **5 marks** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CMOS Amplifier Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Confusing $g_m$ formulas:** Students often use $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}$ (which is the *derivative* form) and forget that the *shortcut* $g_m = 2I_D / V_{OV}$ is far simpler. Using the wrong one leads to numerical errors and **loss of 2–3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in gain:** For a differential-driven CMOS output stage, the gain is $(g_{m1} + g_{m2}) (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$, NOT $g_{m1} (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$. Missing the $g_{m2}$ term costs **3 marks**. > 3. **Mixing $\lambda$ signs:** Use $\vert \lambda_p \vert$ in the magnitude form for PMOS. Writing $r_{o2} = 1/(\lambda_p I_{D2})$ with a negative $\lambda_p$ is a **sign error** that loses **1 mark**. > 4. **Not verifying saturation:** KTU examiners explicitly test whether students check $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$. Skipping this verification costs **1.5 marks** in any DC bias problem. > 5. **Confusing the bias voltage formula:** Some students write $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + \vert V_{tp} \vert$ only, missing the **two overdrive voltage terms**. The correct form is $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$ — this omission costs **2 marks**. > 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing µA and mA, or kΩ and MΩ, in the same calculation. Always convert to base units (A, V, Ω) before substituting. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A CMOS amplifier uses an NMOS and a PMOS in series, with tied drains forming the output — it is a **push-pull** stage. - **Three operating regions:** PMOS active (sourcing), crossover (both off in Class-B), NMOS active (sinking). - **Crossover distortion** is the non-linearity in the transfer characteristic near the midpoint, caused by both transistors being OFF. It is eliminated by **Class-AB biasing** with a small quiescent current $I_Q$. - **Class-AB bias voltage formula:** $V_{BIAS} = V_{tn} + V_{OV1} + \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{OV2}$. - **Small-signal gain:** $A_v = (g_{m1} + g_{m2}) \times (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ for differential drive. - **Output resistance:** $R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}$. - **Transconductance shortcut:** $g_m = 2I_D / V_{OV}$ (very useful in exams). - **Output resistance formula:** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$. - **Maximum Class-B efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = \pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$. - **PMOS sizing:** Typically $(W/L)_p \approx 2 (W/L)_n$ to compensate for $\mu_p \approx \mu_n / 2$. - **Rail-to-rail swing:** Output can reach from nearly 0 V to nearly $V_{DD}$ — key advantage over BJT amplifiers. - **Zero gate current:** MOSFETs draw no DC input current, simplifying the driver stage. - **KTU 2024 focus:** The exam emphasizes **crossover distortion analysis**, **DC bias calculations**, and **small-signal gain derivation** — practice these three problem types thoroughly. - **Real-world applications:** Op-amp output stages, audio amplifiers, headphone drivers, LDO regulators, tri-state digital buffers. - **Common pitfall:** Always verify both transistors are in **saturation** by checking $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$ at the quiescent point. - **Symmetry rule:** At the quiescent point, $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$ and $V_{GS1} = V_{SG2} = V_{BIAS}/2$ (for matched devices). - **Higher gain techniques:** Cascode (fold or telescopic), gain-boosting, negative resistance cancellation — out of KTU scope but conceptually valuable.

topics.title: Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Frequency Response ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In analog electronics, **Frequency Response** of an amplifier is defined as the variation of the amplifier's magnitude of gain (and phase shift) as a function of the input signal frequency, while the amplitude of the input signal is held constant. Mathematically, it represents the transfer function $H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o(j\omega)}{V_i(j\omega)}$ of the amplifier evaluated over a continuous range of sinusoidal input frequencies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Frequency response of BJT and FET amplifiers — analysis at low, mid and high frequencies, effect of coupling and bypass capacitors, Miller's theorem, and gain-bandwidth product. The frequency response of any practical amplifier is **band-limited** — it amplifies signals only within a specific range of frequencies (the passband) and attenuates signals outside this range. The complete response curve is typically divided into three regions: | Region | Cause of Gain Roll-off | Typical Frequency Range | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Low-Frequency Region** | External coupling and bypass capacitors | < **10 Hz – 1 kHz** (audio amps) | | **Midband Region** | No reactive element effect; gain is flat | **1 kHz – 100 kHz** (audio amps) | | **High-Frequency Region** | Internal transistor capacitances | > **100 kHz – MHz range** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Definitions for KTU Exams:** > - **Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$ or $f_H$):** The frequency at which the magnitude of the gain drops to **$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$** (≈ **70.7 %**) of its midband value. This corresponds to a **−3 dB** power point. > - **Bandwidth (BW):** $BW = f_H - f_L$. For most amplifiers, $f_H \gg f_L$, so $BW \approx f_H$. > - **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW or $f_T$):** A figure of merit, $f_T = A_{mid} \times f_H$, which remains approximately constant for a given transistor. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are listening to music through a **stereo speaker system**. The speaker has three knobs — **Bass** (low notes), **Mid** (vocals), and **Treble** (high notes like cymbals). A perfect amplifier is like a perfectly balanced speaker — it reproduces the thundering bass drum, the singer's voice, and the crisp cymbal crash all equally well. Now, think of the **Frequency Response** as the **graph drawn on the speaker's display screen** that shows how loud each note (frequency) comes out. If the line is flat in the middle but droops down on both ends, that tells you the speaker is "lazy" — it cannot reproduce very low or very high notes properly. The "flat middle part" is the **midband**, and the points where the curve drops to **70.7 %** are the **cutoff frequencies**. In amplifiers, this laziness at low frequencies is caused by **big external capacitors** (coupling/bypass), and at high frequencies by **tiny internal capacitances** inside the transistor. So, when we study frequency response, we are essentially asking: *"Why is my amplifier weak at certain frequencies, and how do I fix it?"* > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember cutoff:** The "**70.7 % rule**" or "**half-power point**" — at $f_L$ and $f_H$, the output power drops to **half** (since $P \propto V^2$, voltage drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Decibel (dB) Scale — Why Engineers Use It Engineers do not plot gain on a linear scale because the gain ratio can span many orders of magnitude (e.g., from 1 to 1,000,000). Instead, they use the **decibel scale**, which compresses this range using a **logarithmic** transformation. **Voltage Gain in dB:** $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_o}{V_i}\right) \quad \text{(Voltage Gain in dB)}$$ **Power Gain in dB:** $$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_o}{P_i}\right) \quad \text{(Power Gain in dB)}$$ **Why use dB?** - Multiplication of cascaded gains becomes **simple addition**. - The **−3 dB** point mathematically equals the **70.7 %** voltage point (easy to remember). - A **Bode plot** (semi-log graph of gain vs. frequency) becomes a series of **straight-line asymptotes**, easy to draw and analyze. ### 2.2 Three Regions of Frequency Response — Detailed Logic Flow #### **A. Midband Region (The "Flat" Region)** - All coupling capacitors ($\geq 1\,\mu F$) act as **short circuits** (since $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ is very small). - All internal transistor capacitances ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$ in pF range) act as **open circuits** (since $X_C$ is very large at low frequencies). - Result: The amplifier behaves like a **purely resistive** circuit, and the gain is **constant** and **maximum**. - This is the region where we want our signal of interest to lie. #### **B. Low-Frequency Region (Gain Drops Due to R-C High-Pass Networks)** - As frequency decreases, $X_C$ of coupling/bypass capacitors **increases**. - They no longer behave as perfect shorts — they introduce **series impedance** in the signal path. - The amplifier input/output forms an **R-C high-pass filter** with the coupling capacitor. - Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** (or equivalently, **−6 dB/octave**) as frequency drops below $f_L$. #### **C. High-Frequency Region (Gain Drops Due to Internal Capacitances)** - As frequency increases, the reactance of internal capacitances ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$) **decreases**. - They begin to **shunt** (bypass) the signal away from the output — a **Miller effect** phenomenon. - The amplifier forms an **R-C low-pass filter**. - Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** as frequency increases above $f_H$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Voltage Gain (dB)** | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$ | $A_v$ is linear voltage gain | | 2 | **Cutoff Frequency (RC)** | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz; the −3 dB point | | 3 | **Bandwidth** | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz | | 4 | **Gain-Bandwidth Product** | $f_T = A_{mid} \cdot f_H$ | Hz (transistor datasheet) | | 5 | **Low-Freq. Cutoff (Input Coupling)** | $f_{L,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi(R_S + R_{in})C_{in}}$ | Hz; $R_S$ = source, $R_{in}$ = input R | | 6 | **Low-Freq. Cutoff (Output Coupling)** | $f_{L,out} = \frac{1}{2\pi(R_C + R_L)C_{out}}$ | Hz; $R_C$ = collector, $R_L$ = load | | 7 | **Low-Freq. Cutoff (Bypass)** | $f_{L,bypass} = \frac{1}{2\pi(R_E \vert\vert r_e')C_E}$ | Hz; with $R_E$ bypassed by $C_E$ | | 8 | **Miller Capacitance (Input)** | $C_{in(Miller)} = C_{bc}(1 + A_v)$ | F; capacitance seen at input | | 9 | **Miller Capacitance (Output)** | $C_{out(Miller)} = C_{bc}\left(1 + \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$ | F; capacitance seen at output | | 10 | **High-Freq. Cutoff (CE)** | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{TH} C_{in(total)}}$ | Hz; $R_{TH}$ = Thevenin R at input | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU Trick — The Dominant-Pole Approximation:** When multiple cutoff frequencies exist (e.g., $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}$ at low frequency), the **overall lower cutoff** is found using: > > $$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$$ > > The largest of the individual cutoff frequencies **dominates** the response. So practically, $f_L \approx$ (highest of the three low-frequency cutoffs). Same logic applies for $f_H$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Amplifiers:** Designed so that the human hearing range (**20 Hz – 20 kHz**) lies comfortably within the midband. - **RF/Microwave Amplifiers:** High-frequency cutoff determines which radio channel can be amplified. - **Oscilloscope Probes:** Use R-C compensation networks so their frequency response is flat over the scope's bandwidth. - **Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps):** Have a GBW that limits how high a frequency they can amplify at a given gain (e.g., 741 op-amp has GBW ≈ **1 MHz**). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutoff Frequency of an R-C High-Pass Network (Coupling Capacitor Case) Consider the input coupling network of a CE amplifier: ![RC Coupling Network — Physical Layout](https://placeholder.invalid) The Thevenin equivalent seen by $C_{in}$ consists of a source resistance $R_S$ and the amplifier's input resistance $R_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert \beta r_e'$. **Step 1 — Voltage Divider in the s-domain:** Treat $C_{in}$ as an impedance $Z_C = \frac{1}{sC_{in}}$. The voltage reaching the amplifier input is: $$\frac{V_{in}}{V_S} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in} + \frac{1}{sC_{in}}}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC_{in}$:** $$\frac{V_{in}}{V_S} = \frac{sC_{in} R_{in}}{sC_{in}(R_S + R_{in}) + 1}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ and find the magnitude squared:** $$\left\vert\frac{V_{in}}{V_S}\right\vert^2 = \frac{(\omega C_{in} R_{in})^2}{1 + (\omega C_{in}(R_S + R_{in}))^2}$$ **Step 4 — Definition of cutoff frequency:** The cutoff is the frequency where the magnitude equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its high-frequency (DC-blocked) value. As $\omega \to \infty$, the magnitude approaches 1, so we set: $$\frac{(\omega C_{in} R_{in})^2}{1 + (\omega C_{in}(R_S + R_{in}))^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $\omega$:** Let $X = \omega C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})$. The equation becomes: $$\frac{X^2 \cdot \left(\frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}\right)^2}{1 + X^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$ The term $\frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$ is the maximum (high-freq) gain, which at the −3 dB point drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ times itself, meaning: $$\frac{X^2}{1 + X^2} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2X^2 = 1 + X^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X^2 = 1$$ So $X = 1$, giving: $$\omega C_{in}(R_S + R_{in}) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{L,in} = \frac{1}{C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Hz:** $f_{L,in} = \frac{\omega_{L,in}}{2\pi}$ $$\boxed{f_{L,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** To **lower** $f_L$ (extend low-frequency response), we must **increase** the value of $C_{in}$ (a common technique in audio amplifier design). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Miller's Theorem and the Input Capacitance **Problem Statement:** In a CE amplifier, the collector-to-base capacitance $C_{bc}$ is connected between input and output nodes. We want to find an equivalent capacitance that appears only at the **input side** for easier analysis. **Step 1 — Miller's Theorem Statement:** For an impedance $Z$ connected between input node (voltage $V_i$) and output node (voltage $V_o$), it can be replaced by two impedances: - $Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ from input to ground. - $Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}}$ from output to ground. where $A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_i}$ is the voltage gain from input to output. **Step 2 — Apply to $C_{bc}$:** The impedance of $C_{bc}$ is $Z = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{bc}}$. Thus: $$Z_1 = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{bc}(1 - A_v)}$$ This is equivalent to a capacitance at the input: $$\boxed{C_{in(Miller)} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v)}$$ Since in a CE amplifier, $A_v$ is a **large negative** number (phase inversion), $1 - A_v \approx 1 + \vert A_v \vert$, so: $$C_{in(Miller)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example (Typical BJT at midband):** Given $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$ and $A_v = -100$: $$C_{in(Miller)} = 5\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 100) = 505\,\text{pF}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to forget that $A_v$ is **negative** for a CE (common-emitter) amplifier. Always use $\vert A_v \vert$ when computing the input Miller capacitance, since a capacitance magnitude cannot be negative. **Marks are routinely lost here!** **Step 4 — Why This Matters:** Without Miller's theorem, the high-frequency cutoff would depend on $C_{bc}$ alone. With it, we see that the **effective input capacitance is multiplied** by the gain. This is the **single biggest reason** why CE amplifiers lose gain at high frequencies. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: High-Frequency Cutoff $f_H$ The high-frequency response is governed by an R-C low-pass filter formed at the input: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{TH} C_{in(total)}}$$ where: - $R_{TH} = R_S \vert\vert R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert \beta r_e'$ — total Thevenin resistance seen at the base. - $C_{in(total)} = C_{be} + C_{in(Miller)} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. **Step-by-step numerical example:** Given $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $r_e' = 20\,\Omega$, $C_{be} = 30\,\text{pF}$, $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$, $A_v = -160$. **Step a — Compute $R_{TH}$:** $$R_{TH} = 600 \vert\vert 10000 \vert\vert 2200 \vert\vert (100 \times 20)$$ $$\beta r_e' = 100 \times 20 = 2000\,\Omega$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{TH}} = \frac{1}{600} + \frac{1}{10000} + \frac{1}{2200} + \frac{1}{2000}$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{TH}} = 0.001667 + 0.0001 + 0.0004545 + 0.0005 = 0.0027215$$ $$R_{TH} \approx 367.4\,\Omega$$ **Step b — Compute $C_{in(Miller)}$:** $$C_{in(Miller)} = 5\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 160) = 805\,\text{pF}$$ **Step c — Total input capacitance:** $$C_{in(total)} = 30\,\text{pF} + 805\,\text{pF} = 835\,\text{pF}$$ **Step d — Compute $f_H$:** $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 367.4 \times 835 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$\boxed{f_H \approx 518.6\,\text{kHz}}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Frequency Response Regions & Their Causes ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Input Signal
V_in at frequency f"] --> nodeB{"Frequency Range
Check"} nodeB -- "f less than f_L" --> nodeC["LOW FREQUENCY REGION
Cause: Coupling and Bypass Capacitors
Reactance Xc = 1 over 2 pi f C is HIGH"] nodeB -- "f between f_L and f_H" --> nodeD["MIDBAND REGION
Gain is FLAT and MAXIMUM
No reactive effects"] nodeB -- "f greater than f_H" --> nodeE["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION
Cause: Internal Capacitances
Miller Effect Dominates"] nodeC --> nodeF["Gain rolls off at minus 20 dB per decade"] nodeD --> nodeG["Gain equals A_mid in dB
No phase shift variation"] nodeE --> nodeH["Gain rolls off at minus 20 dB per decade"] nodeF --> nodeI["Cutoff f_L equals 1 over 2 pi R C"] nodeG --> nodeJ["Bandwidth BW equals f_H minus f_L"] nodeH --> nodeK["Cutoff f_H equals 1 over 2 pi R C
Miller Capacitance Multiplies C_bc"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Amplifier Stages Causing Each Cutoff ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["Input Side"] nodeA1["Source R_S"] --> nodeA2["Coupling Capacitor C_in"] nodeA2 --> nodeA3["Bias Resistors R_1, R_2"] nodeA3 --> nodeA4["BJT Base"] end subgraph B["Transistor Internals"] nodeA4 --> nodeB1["C_be between Base and Emitter"] nodeA4 --> nodeB2["C_bc between Base and Collector
Subject to MILLER EFFECT"] end subgraph C["Output Side"] nodeB3["BJT Collector"] --> nodeC1["Coupling Capacitor C_out"] nodeC1 --> nodeC2["Load R_L"] end subgraph D["Bypass Network"] nodeD1["Emitter Resistor R_E"] --- nodeD2["Bypass Capacitor C_E"] end nodeA2 -- "Sets f_L input" --> nodeFreq["Frequency Response"] nodeB1 -- "Sets f_H with Miller" --> nodeFreq nodeB2 -- "Sets f_H Miller Effect" --> nodeFreq nodeC1 -- "Sets f_L output" --> nodeFreq nodeD2 -- "Sets f_L bypass" --> nodeFreq ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Bode Plot — Gain vs Frequency (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph E["Bode Plot of Amplifier Gain versus Frequency"] nodeX["Frequency Axis
f_L ---------- f_1 --------- f_2 --------- f_H"] nodeY["Gain in dB
HIGH then ROLLS OFF then FLAT then ROLLS OFF"] nodeP1["Point at f_L: Gain is A_mid minus 3 dB"] nodeP2["Point at f_H: Gain is A_mid minus 3 dB"] nodeS1["Slope before f_L: minus 20 dB per decade"] nodeS2["Slope after f_H: minus 20 dB per decade"] end nodeY --> nodeP1 nodeY --> nodeP2 nodeX --> nodeS1 nodeX --> nodeS2 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define frequency response of an amplifier. What is meant by the term "bandwidth"?** **Model Answer:** The **frequency response** of an amplifier is the variation of its magnitude of gain (and phase shift) as a function of the input signal frequency, with the input amplitude held constant. It indicates the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful amplification. The **bandwidth (BW)** is the range of frequencies over which the amplifier's gain remains within **3 dB** of its maximum midband value. Mathematically: $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ where $f_H$ is the upper cutoff frequency and $f_L$ is the lower cutoff frequency, both defined at the **−3 dB** (or **70.7 %**) points of the maximum gain. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State and explain Miller's theorem. Why is it important in high-frequency analysis of amplifiers?** **Model Answer:** **Miller's Theorem** states that any impedance $Z$ connected between the input node (voltage $V_1$) and output node (voltage $V_2$) of an amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = V_2/V_1$ can be replaced by two equivalent impedances connected from each node to ground: $$Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{(at input)} \qquad Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}} \quad \text{(at output)}$$ In a CE amplifier, the collector-to-base capacitance $C_{bc}$ appears as an **enlarged capacitance** at the input: $$C_{in(Miller)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ **Importance:** Miller's theorem allows the complex feedback capacitance $C_{bc}$ to be modelled as a grounded capacitance, greatly simplifying high-frequency analysis. It reveals that a small $C_{bc}$ (few pF) can dominate the high-frequency response when multiplied by a large gain. **(3 marks)** --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)** *(Module Internal Choice — Both OR options provided below)* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** With the help of a Bode plot, explain the frequency response of a typical RC-coupled amplifier. Discuss the low-frequency, midband, and high-frequency regions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a common-emitter amplifier, derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency due to the input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, given source resistance $R_S$ and input resistance $R_{in}$. Also state the design rule to extend the low-frequency response. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The frequency response of an RC-coupled CE amplifier is divided into three regions: 1. **Midband Region:** Coupling and bypass capacitors act as short circuits, and internal capacitances act as open circuits. The amplifier is **purely resistive**, gain is **constant = $A_{mid}$**, and phase shift is constant (≈ −180° for CE). 2. **Low-Frequency Region:** Below $f_L$, coupling and bypass capacitors present significant reactance ($X_C = 1/2\pi fC$ increases). They introduce impedance in series with the signal path, reducing gain. The gain falls at a rate of **−20 dB/decade**. 3. **High-Frequency Region:** Above $f_H$, internal capacitances $C_{be}$ and $C_{bc}$ (amplified by Miller effect) begin to shunt the signal. The gain again rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**. The **Bode plot** (semi-log plot of gain in dB vs. log frequency) shows: - A flat plateau in the midband at $20 \log_{10} A_{mid}$ dB. - Asymptotic slopes of **−20 dB/decade** on either side. - The "−3 dB points" marking $f_L$ and $f_H$. **(7 marks — full marks awarded for a clear labelled Bode plot description covering all three regions.)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{f_{L,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})}}$$ **Design Rule to Extend Low-Frequency Response:** To **lower** $f_{L,in}$ (i.e., to amplify lower frequencies effectively): - **Increase** the value of the coupling capacitor $C_{in}$. - **Reduce** the source resistance $R_S$ (use a low-impedance source). - **Increase** the amplifier's input resistance $R_{in}$ (use bias resistors of higher value, or use Darlington configuration, or use FET-input stages). Since $f_L \propto \frac{1}{C_{in}}$, **doubling** $C_{in}$ halves the lower cutoff frequency. **(7 marks — 4 marks for derivation, 3 marks for design rule.)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the Miller effect in detail. Show that the input Miller capacitance of a CE amplifier is $C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CE amplifier has the following parameters: $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $r_e' = 25\,\Omega$, $C_{be} = 35\,\text{pF}$, $C_{bc} = 4\,\text{pF}$, $A_v = -150$. Calculate the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the full derivation. **Summary:** Miller's theorem states that an impedance $Z$ between input and output of an amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be modelled as two grounded impedances. For a CE amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_{bc}$, this gives: $$C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v)$$ Since $A_v$ is large and negative for a CE amplifier, replacing $-A_v$ with $+\vert A_v \vert$: $$\boxed{C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)}$$ This shows why a tiny $C_{bc}$ of just a few pF appears as a **hundreds of pF** capacitor at the input — drastically limiting high-frequency response. **(7 marks — 3 marks for stating theorem, 4 marks for derivation.)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Compute $\beta r_e'$:** $$\beta r_e' = 100 \times 25 = 2500\,\Omega = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_{TH}$ (parallel combination):** $$R_{TH} = R_S \vert\vert R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert \beta r_e'$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{TH}} = \frac{1}{1\,\text{k}} + \frac{1}{10\,\text{k}} + \frac{1}{2.2\,\text{k}} + \frac{1}{2.5\,\text{k}} \,\,\text{(in k}\Omega^{-1}\text{)}$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{TH}} = 1.0 + 0.1 + 0.4545 + 0.4 = 1.9545\,\text{k}\Omega^{-1}$$ $$R_{TH} = \frac{1}{1.9545} \approx 0.5117\,\text{k}\Omega = 511.7\,\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute Miller Capacitance:** $$C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 4\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 150) = 604\,\text{pF}$$ **Step 4 — Total Input Capacitance:** $$C_{in(total)} = C_{be} + C_{in(M)} = 35\,\text{pF} + 604\,\text{pF} = 639\,\text{pF}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $f_H$:** $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{TH} C_{in(total)}}$$ $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 511.7 \times 639 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$f_H = \frac{1}{2.054 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 486.8\,\text{kHz}$$ $$\boxed{f_H \approx 486.8\,\text{kHz}}$$ **(7 marks — full marks for clear stepwise numerical computation.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error in Miller Capacitance:** Students write $C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v)$ and substitute the negative value of $A_v$, getting a wrong answer. Always use $\vert A_v \vert$ since capacitance is a magnitude. **−2 marks** if missed. > 2. **Forgetting $C_{be}$:** Many students use only $C_{in(M)}$ for $C_{in(total)}$ and forget to add $C_{be}$. **−1 mark** if missed. > 3. **Units in $f_H$ calculation:** $R_{TH}$ must be in **ohms** and $C$ in **farads** before multiplication. **−1 mark** if inconsistent. > 4. **Dominant Pole Assumption:** If asked for overall $f_L$ from multiple low-frequency cutoffs, students often sum them instead of taking the maximum. **−2 marks** if wrong. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Frequency Response at a Glance:** - **Frequency Response** = variation of gain (magnitude & phase) with input frequency. - **−3 dB points** = frequencies where gain drops to **70.7 %** of midband value; defines **$f_L$** and **$f_H$**. - **Bandwidth** $= f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$ in most amps). - **Gain in dB** $= 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ (voltage), or $10 \log_{10} P$-ratio (power). - **Midband:** Coupling caps = short, internal caps = open; **gain is constant**. - **Low-Frequency Roll-off:** Caused by coupling & bypass capacitors; slope = **−20 dB/decade**. - **High-Frequency Roll-off:** Caused by $C_{be}$ and Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$; slope = **−20 dB/decade**. - **Miller's Theorem:** $C_{in(M)} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ — the "**killer of high frequencies**" in CE amplifiers. - **Cutoff Formula (RC Network):** $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{TH} C_{eq}}$. - **Dominant Pole Rule:** Overall $f_L$ = largest individual low-frequency cutoff; overall $f_H$ = smallest individual high-frequency cutoff. - **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $f_T = A_{mid} \times f_H$ = **constant** for a given transistor (universal trade-off). - **Design Goals:** To extend low-frequency response → **increase** coupling/bypass capacitors; to extend high-frequency response → **use transistors with smaller $C_{bc}$** or **cascaded (cascode) configurations** to neutralize Miller effect. - **Cascode Amplifier:** Uses a CB stage on top of CE to eliminate Miller multiplication of $C_{bc}$ — high-frequency response improved dramatically. - **FET vs BJT at high frequency:** FETs have **no gate current** and very low input capacitance → superior high-frequency response compared to BJTs. ---

topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Basic Concepts in Analog Electronics

In the context of **Analog Electronics** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the term **"Basic Concepts"** refers to the foundational principles governing the behavior of semiconductor devices — particularly the **PN junction diode**, its terminal characteristics, and the mathematical models used to predict its behavior in DC and small-signal regimes. These fundamentals form the prerequisite knowledge required to analyze active devices (BJT, FET, MOSFET) introduced in later modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2 — Basic Concepts):**
> The student is expected to master the qualitative and quantitative description of the PN junction diode, including its operation under forward bias, reverse bias, and breakdown conditions, along with the piecewise-linear models used in circuit analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a one-way valve** at the end of a pipe:
- The valve allows water (current) to flow in **only one direction** when you push hard enough (forward bias voltage exceeds a threshold).
- If you try to push water backward, the valve blocks it completely (reverse bias — no current).
- However, if you push backward with extreme force, the valve mechanically ruptures and water gushes through uncontrollably (breakdown / Zener effect).

A **diode is exactly this one-way valve** for electrical current. The "threshold pressure" is the **cut-in voltage** ($V_\gamma \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ for silicon), and the "rupture pressure" is the **breakdown voltage** ($V_{BR}$).

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> | Parameter | Symbol | Silicon (Si) | Germanium (Ge) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Intrinsic carrier concentration ($cm^{-3}$) | $n_i$ | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ | $2.4 \times 10^{13}$ |
> | Cut-in / Knee voltage (V) | $V_\gamma$ | **0.7** | **0.3** |
> | Breakdown voltage (V) | $V_{BR}$ | 50 – 1000+ | 6 – 400 |
> | Thermal voltage at 300 K (V) | $V_T$ | **0.0259** | **0.0259** |
> | Bandgap energy (eV) | $E_g$ | **1.12** | **0.67** |
> | Electron mobility ($cm^2/V\cdot s$) | $\mu_n$ | **1350** | **3900** |
> | Hole mobility ($cm^2/V\cdot s$) | $\mu_p$ | **480** | **1900** |

The **thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at room temperature (300 K) is one of the most frequently appearing constants in diode and transistor analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the PN Junction

The PN junction is formed when a **P-type semiconductor** (excess holes, acceptor-doped) is fused with an **N-type semiconductor** (excess electrons, donor-doped). The following sequence describes its operation:

1. **Formation of the Depletion Region**
   - At the junction, free electrons from the N-side diffuse into the P-side and recombine with holes.
   - This leaves behind **immobile positive ions** on the N-side and **immobile negative ions** on the P-side.
   - This charge-free zone, depleted of mobile carriers, is called the **depletion region** or **space-charge region**.
   - An internal **electric field** $E$ and a **built-in potential barrier** $V_0$ are established that oppose further diffusion.

2. **Forward Bias Operation**
   - An external DC source is applied with the **P-side connected to the positive terminal** and the **N-side to the negative terminal**.
   - The applied voltage opposes the built-in field, **reducing the barrier potential** from $V_0$ to $(V_0 - V_F)$.
   - Once $V_F > V_\gamma$ (cut-in voltage), the depletion region narrows significantly and **current flows easily**.
   - The current increases **exponentially** with applied voltage.

3. **Reverse Bias Operation**
   - The external source is reversed: P-side to negative, N-side to positive.
   - The applied voltage **adds to the built-in potential**, widening the depletion region.
   - Only a tiny **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ (order of nA for Si, µA for Ge) flows due to thermally generated minority carriers.
   - This current is essentially **independent of reverse bias voltage** up to breakdown.

4. **Breakdown Region**
   - When the reverse voltage exceeds a critical value $V_{BR}$, the diode enters breakdown.
   - Two mechanisms: **Zener effect** (tunneling, dominant at low $V_{BR} < 5.5\text{ V}$) and **Avalanche effect** (impact ionization, dominant at high $V_{BR}$).
   - The voltage across the diode remains approximately constant at $V_Z$ (Zener voltage) while current can vary widely — this is the principle behind **voltage regulators**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations appear in nearly every KTU exam paper on this topic. Memorize them thoroughly.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)$ | **Schockley Diode Equation** — master equation of the diode | A, V |
| 2 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage at absolute temperature $T$ | V |
| 3 | $V_T \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $300\text{ K}$ | Standard value used in all problems | V |
| 4 | $I_D \approx I_S\, e^{V_D / \eta V_T}$ for $V_D \gg V_T$ | Forward-bias approximation | A |
| 5 | $r_d = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_D}$ | **Dynamic (AC) resistance** of the diode at operating point $I_D$ | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $r_d = \dfrac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_D\ (\text{mA})}$ | Simplified form used in numerical problems | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential barrier of the junction | V |
| 8 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s V_0}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)}$ | Depletion region width | m |
| 9 | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | Junction (depletion) capacitance | F |
| 10 | $P_{IV} = V_{RM} \cdot I_{D(avg)}$ | Power dissipated in diode (rectifier context) | W |
| 11 | $\text{DC output: } V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | Half-wave rectifier average output | V |
| 12 | $\text{DC output: } V_{dc} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | Full-wave rectifier average output | V |
| 13 | $\text{Ripple factor: } \gamma = \sqrt{\!\left(\dfrac{I_{rms}}{I_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1}$ | Measure of AC content in rectified output | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}} \times 100\%$ | Rectification efficiency | % |

**Note on ideality factor $\eta$:** For an **ideal diode** $\eta = 1$; for **real silicon diodes** $\eta$ typically lies between **1 and 2** (commonly assumed $= 1$ in KTU problems unless stated otherwise).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rectifiers** in DC power supplies (mobile chargers, SMPS adapters).
- **Signal limiters and clippers** in communication receivers.
- **Voltage regulators** using Zener diodes in reference circuits of op-amps and ADCs.
- **Mixers and detectors** in RF front-end circuits.
- **Photodiodes and solar cells** — the same PN junction with light as the energy source.

The **dynamic resistance** $r_d$ is the cornerstone of **small-signal analysis** — it is used in the upcoming modules on **BJT amplifiers** and **FET amplifiers** in the same course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Schockley Diode Equation

### Statement
The current through a PN junction diode as a function of the applied voltage $V_D$ is given by:

$$
I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Carrier concentration at the edges of the depletion region (Boltzmann relations):**

In thermal equilibrium, the law of mass action gives $np = n_i^2$. Under an applied bias $V_D$, the minority carrier concentrations at the depletion edges change as:

$$
p_n(0) = p_{n0}\, e^{V_D / V_T}
$$

$$
n_p(0) = n_{p0}\, e^{V_D / V_T}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The applied bias shifts the quasi-Fermi levels, increasing the minority carrier population exponentially at the junction edge.

**Step 2 — Minority carrier diffusion current at the junction:**

The injected minority carriers diffuse into the neutral regions. Solving the one-dimensional diffusion equation (Fick's law with boundary conditions) gives the diffusion current densities:

$$
J_p = \dfrac{q D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left(e^{V_D / V_T} - 1\right)
$$

$$
J_n = \dfrac{q D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left(e^{V_D / V_T} - 1\right)
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each diffusion current is proportional to the *excess* minority carrier concentration $(p_n(0) - p_{n0}) = p_{n0}(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1)$, and inversely proportional to the diffusion length $L = \sqrt{D\tau}$.

**Step 3 — Total current density:**

The total current density is the sum of the hole and electron contributions:

$$
J_{total} = J_p + J_n = \left(\dfrac{q D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \dfrac{q D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)\left(e^{V_D / V_T} - 1\right)
$$

**Conversion logic:** Hole and electron currents are additive (Kirchhoff's current law applied to carrier flow).

**Step 4 — Define the reverse saturation current $I_S$:**

Let the term in parentheses be $J_S$ (reverse saturation current density). Then:

$$
J_S = q\, n_i^2 \left(\dfrac{D_p}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n}{L_n N_A}\right)
$$

Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$ gives $I_S = J_S \cdot A$.

**Step 5 — Introduce ideality factor $\eta$ and finalize:**

Including the ideality factor to account for recombination in the depletion region:

$$
I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right) \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Conversion logic:** The ideality factor $\eta$ corrects the idealized assumption that all recombination occurs only in the neutral quasi-neutral regions; real diodes have $\eta \in [1, 2]$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dynamic (AC) Resistance of a Diode

### Statement
The small-signal incremental resistance of a diode at a quiescent operating point $I_{DQ}$ is:

$$
r_d = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_{DQ}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Differentiate the Schockley equation:**

The small-signal conductance is the derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_D$:

$$
g_d = \dfrac{d I_D}{d V_D} = \dfrac{d}{d V_D}\!\left[I_S\!\left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the derivative:**

Since $\dfrac{d}{dV_D}\!\left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\eta V_T}\, e^{V_D / \eta V_T}$, we obtain:

$$
g_d = \dfrac{I_S}{\eta V_T}\, e^{V_D / \eta V_T} = \dfrac{I_D + I_S}{\eta V_T}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the forward-bias approximation $I_D \gg I_S$:**

For all practical forward-bias currents (mA range), $I_S$ is negligible (nA). Hence:

$$
g_d \approx \dfrac{I_D}{\eta V_T}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain resistance:**

$$
r_d = \dfrac{1}{g_d} = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_{DQ}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical shortcut (for $\eta = 1$, $T = 300\text{ K}$):**

$$
r_d = \dfrac{25.85\ \text{mV}}{I_{DQ}} = \dfrac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_{DQ}\ (\text{mA})}\ \Omega
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the most frequently used result in BJT small-signal amplifier analysis in **Module 3** of this course. The student must be able to derive and use this expression fluently.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Half-Wave Rectifier DC Output Voltage

### Statement
The average (DC) output voltage of a half-wave rectifier with peak input $V_m$ is:

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the output waveform:**

For a half-wave rectifier, the output voltage during one complete cycle is:

$$
v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0, & \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the average value formula over a full period $T$:**

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o(t)\, dt = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_0^{\pi} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)\right]
$$

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[-(-1) + 1\right] = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Conversion logic:** Numerically, $V_{dc} \approx 0.318\, V_m$, meaning the half-wave rectifier extracts only ~31.8% of the peak amplitude as useful DC.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Piecewise Linear Diode Models Comparison

| Property | Ideal Diode Model | Practical (Battery) Model | Complete Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage drop | $0\ \text{V}$ | $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$ (Si) | $V_\gamma + I_D \cdot r_d$ |
| Forward resistance | $0\ \Omega$ | $0\ \Omega$ | $r_d = \eta V_T / I_D$ |
| Reverse current | $0\ \text{A}$ | $0\ \text{A}$ | $I_S$ (constant) |
| Best use-case | Conceptual analysis, ideal textbook | KTU exam problems (most common) | Accurate numerical/engineering design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Three States of a PN Junction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PN Junction Diode] --> B{Applied Voltage}
    B -->|V_D > 0 and V_D > V_gamma| C[Forward Bias Region]
    B -->|V_D < 0 and |V_D| < V_BR| D[Reverse Bias Region]
    B -->|V_D < 0 and |V_D| > V_BR| E[Breakdown Region]

    C --> C1[Depletion region narrows]
    C --> C2[Exponential current increase]
    C --> C3[Diode acts as closed switch]

    D --> D1[Depletion region widens]
    D --> D2[Tiny reverse saturation current I_S]
    D --> D3[Diode acts as open switch]

    E --> E1[Avalanche or Zener effect dominates]
    E --> E2[Voltage clamped near V_Z]
    E --> E3[Diode used as voltage regulator]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Small-Signal Diode Model Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Operating Point I_DQ, V_DQ] --> B[Linearize around Q-point]
    B --> C[Dynamic resistance r_d equals eta V_T over I_DQ]
    C --> D[Small-signal equivalent: ideal diode plus r_d in series]
    D --> E[Use this model for AC analysis in BJT/FET amplifiers]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Diode Classification by Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diodes] --> B[Rectifier Diodes]
    A --> C[Zener Diodes]
    A --> D[Signal Diodes]
    A --> E[Special Purpose]

    B --> B1[Half-wave rectifiers]
    B --> B2[Full-wave rectifiers]
    B --> B3[Bridge rectifiers]

    C --> C1[Voltage regulators]
    C --> C2[Reference circuits]

    D --> D1[Clippers and clampers]
    D --> D2[RF detectors]

    E --> E1[Photodiodes]
    E --> E2[Light Emitting Diodes LEDs]
    E --> E3[Schottky diodes]
    E --> E4[Varactor diodes]
```

## 4.4 V-I Characteristic Curve — Schematic Description

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block is rendered as a **functional block flow** rather than a literal plot, since Mermaid does not natively support Cartesian plotting. The block flow captures the analytical behavior of the curve in each quadrant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1 [Quadrant I: Forward Bias]
        Q1A[Start at origin O, I equals 0] --> Q1B[Rise gradually until V equals V_gamma]
        Q1B --> Q1C[Steep exponential rise beyond V_gamma]
        Q1C --> Q1D[Diode conducts heavily, current in mA range]
    end

    subgraph Q3 [Quadrant III: Reverse Bias]
        Q3A[Apply negative V_D] --> Q3B[Tiny constant current I_S flows]
        Q3B --> Q3C[Depletion region widens]
        Q3C --> Q3D[At V equals V_BR, breakdown occurs]
    end

    Q1 --> Q3
```

```mermaid

    A[Read the Problem Statement] --> B[Identify the Diode Model Required]
    B -->|Conceptual| C[Use Ideal Diode Model]
    B -->|Numerical, simple| D[Use Practical Battery Model V_gamma equals 0.7 V]
    B -->|Accurate or AC small-signal| E[Use Complete Model with r_d]
    C --> F[Solve using KVL or KCL]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Split DC and AC analysis]
    G --> H[DC: Find Q-point using battery model]

```
