# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term **cut-in voltage** of a PN junction diode. How does it differ for silicon and germanium diodes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cut-in voltage** (also called **knee voltage** or **threshold voltage**) of a PN junction diode is the minimum forward bias voltage that must be applied across the diode before it starts conducting appreciable forward current. Below this voltage, the diode current is negligibly small (in the order of $\mu$A), and above it, the current rises sharply and exponentially.

- For a **silicon diode**, $V_\gamma \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$ at 300 K.
- For a **germanium diode**, $V_\gamma \approx \mathbf{0.3\ V}$ at 300 K.

The difference arises because silicon has a **larger bandgap** ($E_g = 1.12\text{ eV}$) than germanium ($E_g = 0.67\text{ eV}$), requiring a higher applied voltage to overcome the built-in potential barrier.

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for silicon value, 1 mark for germanium value and reason.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between the **static resistance** and **dynamic resistance** of a diode. Which one is used in small-signal AC analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Static Resistance ($R_D$) | Dynamic Resistance ($r_d$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of DC voltage to DC current at the operating point | Inverse slope of the V-I curve at the Q-point |
| Formula | $R_D = V_D / I_D$ | $r_d = \eta V_T / I_{DQ}$ |
| Used in | DC analysis, load-line construction | **Small-signal AC analysis** |

In small-signal AC analysis, the diode is biased at a quiescent point $(V_{DQ}, I_{DQ})$ and the small AC variations are modeled using **$r_d$**, because for small signals the non-linear diode curve is approximated as linear with slope $1/r_d$.

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark each for static and dynamic definitions, 1 mark for AC use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in the reverse saturation term $(-1)$ of the Schockley equation — this costs 1 full mark.
> 2. In small-signal analysis, students use the **DC current value** directly in mA without converting — ensure you use $I_{DQ}$ in **amperes** when computing $r_d = \eta V_T / I_{DQ}$.
> 3. Confusing the **knee voltage symbol** — KTU uses $V_\gamma$ (gamma) or $V_k$ or $V_{cut-in}$; all three are accepted but stick to $V_\gamma$ for consistency.
> 4. **Units in final answer:** Always express the answer with units (V, A, $\Omega$, W) — missing units is a **0.5 mark deduction** per answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** This question carries internal choice. Attempt **any one** of the two parts.

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat V-I characteristic curve, explain the operation of a PN junction diode under forward and reverse bias. Define the terms **knee voltage** and **reverse saturation current**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Forward Bias Region:**

When the P-side of the diode is connected to the positive terminal and the N-side to the negative terminal of a DC source, the diode is said to be in forward bias.

- The applied voltage opposes the built-in potential $V_0$, reducing the effective barrier to $(V_0 - V_F)$.
- The depletion region narrows.
- For $V_F < V_\gamma$, the diode current is very small.
- For $V_F \geq V_\gamma$, the diode conducts strongly, and the current increases **exponentially** according to the Schockley equation.

**2. Reverse Bias Region:**

When the polarity is reversed (P-side to negative, N-side to positive):

- The applied voltage adds to $V_0$, widening the depletion region.
- Only a very small **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ (typically nA for Si) flows due to thermally generated minority carriers.
- $I_S$ is **independent** of the magnitude of reverse voltage up to breakdown.

**3. Definitions:**

- **Knee voltage ($V_\gamma$):** The minimum forward voltage at which the diode starts conducting appreciable current. For Si, $V_\gamma \approx 0.7\text{ V}$.
- **Reverse saturation current ($I_S$):** The small, constant current that flows under reverse bias, arising from minority carrier drift across the junction. It is temperature-dependent (doubles every 10 °C rise).

**4. V-I Curve Sketch (described):**

- **Forward region (1st quadrant):** Starts at origin, rises slowly until $V_\gamma$, then shoots up almost vertically.
- **Reverse region (3rd quadrant):** Almost flat along the negative voltage axis at $I = -I_S$.
- **Breakdown point:** At $V = -V_{BR}$, the curve bends sharply downward, indicating a large current increase at nearly constant voltage.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for forward bias explanation, 2 marks for reverse bias, 1.5 marks each for the two definitions, 0.5 mark for curve description.

#### (b) [7 Marks] For a silicon diode with reverse saturation current $I_S = 10\ \text{nA}$ at 300 K, find the diode current when the applied voltage is:
(i) $V_D = 0.5\ \text{V}$ (forward), and
(ii) $V_D = -0.5\ \text{V}$ (reverse).
Take $\eta = 1$ and $V_T = 25.85\ \text{mV}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_S = 10 \times 10^{-9}\text{ A} = 10^{-8}\text{ A}$, $V_T = 25.85 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}$, $\eta = 1$.

**Schockley Equation:**

$$
I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)
$$

**Part (i):** $V_D = +0.5\text{ V}$

Compute the exponent:

$$
\dfrac{V_D}{\eta V_T} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 \times 0.02585} = 19.342
$$

Compute $e^{19.342}$:

$$
e^{19.342} \approx 2.476 \times 10^{8}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_D = 10^{-8} \times (2.476 \times 10^{8} - 1) \approx 10^{-8} \times 2.476 \times 10^{8} = 2.476\ \text{A}
$$

**Result:** $I_D \approx \mathbf{2.48\ A}$ — a very large current, as expected for $V_D > V_\gamma$.

**Part (ii):** $V_D = -0.5\text{ V}$

Compute the exponent:

$$
\dfrac{V_D}{\eta V_T} = \dfrac{-0.5}{0.02585} = -19.342
$$

Compute $e^{-19.342}$:

$$
e^{-19.342} \approx 4.04 \times 10^{-9}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_D = 10^{-8} \times (4.04 \times 10^{-9} - 1) \approx 10^{-8} \times (-0.99999) \approx -10^{-8}\text{ A}
$$

**Result:** $I_D \approx \mathbf{-10\ \text{nA}} = -I_S$ — the diode current equals the negative of the reverse saturation current, confirming the diode blocks DC current in reverse.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for setting up the equation, 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 1 mark for correct units and physical interpretation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** This question carries internal choice. Attempt **any one** of the two parts.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the **dynamic (AC) resistance** of a diode starting from the Schockley equation. A silicon diode operates at a forward current of $I_{DQ} = 5\ \text{mA}$. Find its dynamic resistance at 300 K. ($\eta = 1$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$.)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the Schockley equation:

$$
I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)
$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $V_D$ to obtain the small-signal conductance:

$$
g_d = \dfrac{d I_D}{d V_D} = \dfrac{I_S}{\eta V_T}\, e^{V_D / \eta V_T} = \dfrac{I_D + I_S}{\eta V_T}
$$

For forward-bias operation, $I_D \gg I_S$, so $I_S$ is dropped:

$$
g_d \approx \dfrac{I_{DQ}}{\eta V_T}
$$

Inverting for resistance:

$$
\boxed{\,r_d = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_{DQ}}\,}
$$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

Given $I_{DQ} = 5\text{ mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}$:

$$
r_d = \dfrac{1 \times 26 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{26}{5} = 5.2\ \Omega
$$

**Result:** $r_d = \mathbf{5.2\ \Omega}$.

**Interpretation:** At higher forward current, the diode's AC resistance decreases — useful when designing low-impedance signal paths.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave rectifier** with a resistive load. Derive the expressions for:
(i) DC output voltage $V_{dc}$,
(ii) RMS output voltage $V_{rms}$,
(iii) Rectification efficiency $\eta$.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Operation (2 marks):**

A half-wave rectifier consists of a transformer, a single diode, and a load resistor $R_L$ all in series. During the **positive half-cycle** of the AC input, the diode is forward-biased and conducts, delivering current to $R_L$. During the **negative half-cycle**, the diode is reverse-biased and blocks current, so the output across $R_L$ is zero.

**Output Waveform:** $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$, and $v_o = 0$ for $\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$.

**(i) DC Output Voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V_m
$$

**(ii) RMS Output Voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} (V_m \sin\theta)^2\, d\theta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2}} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}
$$

**(iii) Rectification Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} = \dfrac{(V_m/\pi)^2}{(V_m/2)^2} = \dfrac{4}{\pi^2} \approx 0.406 = 40.6\%
$$

**Result:** The maximum rectification efficiency of a half-wave rectifier is **40.6%** — this is a standard KTU result.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for circuit and waveform, 1.5 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 2 marks for part (iii).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Basic Concepts**

- **Schockley Diode Equation** $I_D = I_S\!\left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)$ — the **master equation**. The $(-1)$ is essential.
- **Thermal voltage** $V_T = 25.85\text{ mV}$ at 300 K — memorize this value.
- **Cut-in voltage:** $0.7\text{ V}$ for Si, $0.3\text{ V}$ for Ge — these are the most-tested numerical values in KTU papers.
- **Dynamic resistance** $r_d = \eta V_T / I_{DQ}$ — directly applicable in BJT and FET small-signal analysis later in the course.
- **Reverse saturation current** $I_S$ doubles for every **10 °C** rise in temperature; it is in nA range for Si.
- **Diode piecewise models:** Ideal (zero drop), Practical ($V_\gamma$ drop), Complete ($V_\gamma + I_D r_d$). Use the **practical model** for most KTU numerical problems.
- **Half-wave rectifier:** $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$, $V_{rms} = V_m/2$, efficiency $= 40.6\%$.
- **Full-wave rectifier:** $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$, $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$, efficiency $= 81.2\%$.
- **Ripple factor:** $\gamma = \sqrt{(I_{rms}/I_{dc})^2 - 1}$; equals 1.21 for half-wave, 0.482 for full-wave.
- **PIV rating:** Peak Inverse Voltage the diode must withstand — equal to $V_m$ for half-wave, $2V_m$ for bridge full-wave.
- **Built-in potential** $V_0 = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — increases with doping and decreases with temperature.
- **Depletion width** $W \propto \sqrt{V_0 + V_R}$ — widens under reverse bias.
- **Junction capacitance** $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$ — decreases with increasing reverse bias (basis of varactor diodes).
- **Zener diode:** Operates in reverse breakdown region; maintains nearly constant $V_Z$ — used as a **voltage regulator**.
- **Always state assumptions** (e.g., $\eta = 1$, $T = 300\text{ K}$, $I_S \ll I_D$) explicitly in your exam answers — examiners reward this practice with bonus marks.
- **Units are mandatory** in every numerical answer; missing units = **0.5 mark deduction** per answer as per KTU valuation norms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BJT Amplifier Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BJT Amplifier Frequency Response** is defined as the variation of the magnitude and phase of the amplifier's voltage gain (or current gain) as a function of the input signal frequency, while the amplitude of the input sinusoidal signal is held constant.

In any practical BJT amplifier, the **small-signal voltage gain** $A_v(s)$ is not a constant with respect to frequency. It is a **rational function of the complex frequency variable $s = j\omega$**, whose magnitude rolls off at low and high frequencies, while remaining approximately constant in the **midband region**.

For a single-stage common-emitter (CE) amplifier, the generalized gain expression takes the canonical form:

$$A_v(s) = A_{v(mid)} \cdot \frac{s\,\tau_s}{(1 + s\,\tau_s)} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + s\,\tau_1)(1 + s\,\tau_2)\ldots}$$

Where:
- $A_{v(mid)}$ = **midband voltage gain** (constant, frequency-independent)
- $\tau_s$ = **short-circuit time constant** associated with the input coupling network
- $\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots$ = **open-circuit time constants** corresponding to internal transistor capacitances

### Three Distinct Frequency Regions

| Region | Frequency Range | Dominant Reactive Elements | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Frequency Region** | $f < f_{L}$ | Coupling ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) and Bypass ($C_E$) capacitors | Gain falls off at $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per pole |
| **Midband Region** | $f_{L} \le f \le f_{H}$ | All capacitors treated as short or open | Gain is flat, $A_v = A_{v(mid)}$ |
| **High-Frequency Region** | $f > f_{H}$ | Internal BJT capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) | Gain falls off at $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per pole |

Where $f_{L}$ and $f_{H}$ are the **lower** and **upper 3-dB cutoff frequencies**, respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Ear and a Stretched Rubber Band**
>
> Imagine a microphone connected to a loudspeaker through a chain of electronic "translators" (the amplifier). Each translator in the chain is like a rubber band.
>
> - At **very low frequencies** (a deep bass drum), the giant coupling capacitors act like *stiff springs* that **refuse to oscillate slowly** — they block the slow signal, just as a stiff spring blocks slow motion. Result: **bass is muted**.
> - At **mid frequencies** (human voice), the rubber bands (capacitors) are loose and free, and the system translates the signal faithfully. Result: **gain is flat**.
> - At **very high frequencies** (a cymbal crash), the tiny parasitic capacitances inside the transistor behave like *short circuits* to the fast signal, **shunting the high-frequency content to ground**. Result: **treble is muted**.
>
> The plot of gain versus frequency therefore looks like a **flat plateau (midband) sandwiched between two sloping shoulders (low and high)** — a shape the academic world calls a **band-pass response**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q = \textbf{25.85 mV} \approx 26\,\text{mV}$
- **Cutoff frequency definition (–3 dB point):** $|A_v(f_c)| = \dfrac{A_{v(mid)}}{\sqrt{2}}$
- **Decade:** A factor of **10** in frequency.
- **Octave:** A factor of **2** in frequency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Frequency Response Exist? — The Physics

A BJT amplifier contains **two fundamentally different classes of capacitors**:

1. **External (Coupling / Bypass) Capacitors** — typically in the range $\mu\text{F}$ to tens of $\mu\text{F}$. They form high-pass RC networks with bias resistors.
2. **Internal (Parasitic) Capacitances** — typically in the range of pF. They consist of:
   - $C_\pi$ — Base–Emitter diffusion + junction capacitance
   - $C_\mu$ — Base–Collector reverse-biased junction capacitance (Miller capacitance)

At **low frequencies**, the external capacitors have *high impedance* ($Z_C = 1/j\omega C$) and steal signal current, causing attenuation.

At **high frequencies**, the internal capacitances have *low impedance* and shunt the signal away from the load, again causing attenuation.

## 2.2 The Low-Frequency Response — Pole-Zero Logic

For a typical CE amplifier with **input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$**, **output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$**, and **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$**, three low-frequency poles are introduced. Using the **Short-Circuit Time Constant (SCTC) method**:

$$f_L = f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3} - \text{higher order terms}$$

Or more precisely, the dominant pole approximation:

$$f_L \approx f_{L(dom)} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$$

The standard textbook expressions (using the Miller and SCTC approach) are:

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_s + R_{in}) C_{in}}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{e}' C_E}$$

Where:
- $R_s$ = source resistance
- $R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert r_\pi$ (input resistance seen by $C_{in}$)
- $R_e' = R_E \Vert \left(\dfrac{R_s' + r_\pi}{\beta_0 + 1}\right)$ (resistance seen by $C_E$, with $R_s' = R_s \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2$)

## 2.3 The High-Frequency Response — Hybrid-π Model

In the high-frequency region, the BJT is replaced by the **small-signal hybrid-π equivalent** containing $r_\pi$, $g_m v_{be}$, and the two parasitic capacitors $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$.

The voltage gain (CE configuration) becomes:

$$A_v(s) = -g_m (R_C \Vert R_L) \cdot \frac{1 - s C_\mu / g_m}{1 + s[R_\sigma(C_\pi + C_\mu) + R_L' C_\mu]}$$

Where $R_\sigma = R_s' \Vert r_\pi$ and $R_L' = R_C \Vert R_L$.

This gives:
- **One dominant pole:** $\omega_H = \dfrac{1}{[R_\sigma(C_\pi + C_\mu(1+g_m R_L'))] + R_L' C_\mu}$

Using **Miller's Theorem**, the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ is reflected to the input as:

$$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v') \approx C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$$

And to the output as:

$$C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v'}\right) \approx C_\mu$$

The high-frequency pole simplifies to:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_\sigma (C_\pi + C_{M1})}$$

## 2.4 Miller's Theorem (Recap for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller's Theorem:** An impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with voltage gain $K = V_o/V_i$ can be split into:
> $$Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - K} \quad \text{(at the input node)}$$
> $$Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - 1/K} \quad \text{(at the output node)}$$

This is critical for solving $C_\mu$ effects, which would otherwise be a "floating" capacitor making nodal analysis intractable.

## 2.5 Bandwidth and Gain-Bandwidth Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is an invariant figure-of-merit for a single-pole amplifier:
> $$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert \cdot f_H = \frac{g_m R_L'}{2\pi R_\sigma C_{in}'} \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$
> where $C_{in}' = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{25.85\,mV}$ at 300 K | V |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | Siemens (A/V) |
| 3 | Input Resistance (hybrid-π) | $r_\pi = \beta_0 / g_m$ | Ohms |
| 4 | Lower Cutoff (SCTC sum) | $f_L \approx \sum f_{L_k}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Input Coupling Pole | $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_s + R_{in})C_{in}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Output Coupling Pole | $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L)C_{out}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Emitter Bypass Pole | $f_{L3} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_e' C_E}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Miller Input Cap | $C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$ | Farads |
| 9 | Miller Output Cap | $C_{M2} = C_\mu$ | Farads |
| 10 | Upper Cutoff (CE) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_\sigma (C_\pi + C_{M1})}$ | Hz |
| 11 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v(mid)}\vert \cdot f_H$ | Hz |
| 12 | Decibel Gain | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$ | dB |
| 13 | High-Freq Gain Roll-off | $-20\log_{10}\sqrt{1+(f/f_H)^2}$ | dB |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio pre-amplifiers** must have $f_L \le 20\,\text{Hz}$ and $f_H \ge 20\,\text{kHz}$ — the *audio band*.
- **RF amplifiers** are designed with $f_H$ deep into the MHz/GHz range using **neutralization** techniques to cancel the Miller effect.
- **Operational amplifiers** internally cascade gain stages; the dominant pole of the *first* stage dictates the closed-loop bandwidth.
- **PCB designers** must minimize stray capacitances because $C_{M1}$ multiplied by $g_m R_L'$ can reduce $f_H$ by orders of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Low-Frequency Pole from Emitter Bypass Capacitor

We will derive the lower 3-dB frequency contribution of the bypass capacitor $C_E$ in a CE amplifier with partial bypass (resistor $R_{Ef}$ in series with $C_E$).

### Step 1: Draw the small-signal equivalent with $C_E$ included

Thevenin resistance seen looking out of the emitter terminal is found by setting $v_s = 0$ and applying a test voltage $v_x$ at the emitter.

### Step 2: The emitter node sees two parallel paths to ground

- Path 1: Through $R_E \Vert$ (anything external at the emitter)
- Path 2: Through the transistor's internal emitter resistance

### Step 3: Internal resistance looking into the emitter

For a BJT with base driven by $R_s' = R_s \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2$:

$$R_{in,e} = \frac{R_s' + r_\pi}{\beta_0 + 1}$$

### Step 4: Total resistance seen by $C_E$

$$R_{e}' = R_E \Vert R_{in,e} = R_E \Vert \frac{R_s' + r_\pi}{\beta_0 + 1}$$

### Step 5: Pole frequency

The pole is set by $R_{e}'$ and $C_E$:

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{e}' C_E}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \left(R_E \Vert \dfrac{R_s' + r_\pi}{\beta_0 + 1}\right) C_E}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: High-Frequency Cutoff Using Miller's Theorem

### Step 1: Write the small-signal hybrid-π circuit

The CE amplifier in the high-frequency region has the following elements:
- Source: $v_s$ with resistance $R_s$
- Bias network: $R_1 \Vert R_2 = R_B$
- Transistor: $r_\pi$ between base and emitter, $g_m v_{be}$ current source from collector to emitter
- Parasitics: $C_\pi$ from base to emitter, $C_\mu$ from base to collector
- Load: $R_C \Vert R_L = R_L'$

### Step 2: Thevenize the source and bias network

$$R_s' = R_s \Vert R_B = \frac{R_s R_B}{R_s + R_B}$$

### Step 3: Determine the gain from base to collector

Assuming $C_\mu$ is initially a high-impedance element at the frequency of interest (will be justified later), the gain is:

$$A_v' = \frac{v_o}{v_{be}} = -g_m R_L'$$

### Step 4: Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_\mu$

$C_\mu$ is between base ($v_{be}$) and collector ($-g_m R_L' \cdot v_{be}$). The Miller equivalent input capacitance is:

$$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v') = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$$

Miller equivalent output capacitance:

$$C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v'}\right) \approx C_\mu$$

### Step 5: Sum all capacitances at the input node

$$C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')$$

### Step 6: Identify the input time constant

The total resistance at the base node is $R_s' \Vert r_\pi$:

$$R_{in}' = R_s' \Vert r_\pi = \frac{R_s' r_\pi}{R_s' + r_\pi}$$

### Step 7: Pole frequency

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{R_{in}' \cdot C_{in,tot}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_s' \Vert r_\pi) [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')]}$$

### Step 8: Simplification

Since $R_s' \Vert r_\pi \le r_\pi$, this can be approximated in design analysis. The dominant limitation is the **Miller-multiplied $C_\mu$**, which is why **CC (emitter follower) and CB (common-base) configurations have superior high-frequency response** — the Miller gain in those configurations is approximately 1.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (Typical KTU Style):**
> Given: $R_s = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta_0 = 100$, $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, $C_\pi = 10\,\text{pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\,\text{pF}$. Compute $f_H$.

### Step 1: Compute $g_m$

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{25.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 38.68\,\text{mA/V}$$

### Step 2: Compute $r_\pi$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{100}{38.68 \times 10^{-3}} = 2585\,\Omega \approx 2.585\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 3: Compute $R_B$ and $R_s'$

$$R_B = R_1 \Vert R_2 = \frac{100 \times 50}{150} = 33.33\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_s' = R_s \Vert R_B = \frac{1 \times 33.33}{34.33} = 0.971\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 4: Compute $R_L'$

$$R_L' = R_C \Vert R_L = \frac{5 \times 10}{15} = 3.333\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 5: Compute $R_{in}'$

$$R_{in}' = R_s' \Vert r_\pi = \frac{0.971 \times 2.585}{0.971 + 2.585} = \frac{2.510}{3.556} = 0.706\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 6: Compute Miller input capacitance

$$C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L') = 2 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 + 38.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 3333)$$

$$g_m R_L' = 38.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.333 \times 10^{3} = 128.93$$

$$C_{M1} = 2 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 + 128.93) = 2 \times 10^{-12} \times 129.93 = 259.86\,\text{pF}$$

### Step 7: Total input capacitance

$$C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = 10 + 259.86 = 269.86\,\text{pF}$$

### Step 8: Upper cutoff frequency

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in}' C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 706 \times 269.86 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.905 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{1.197 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 835\,\text{kHz}$$

### Step 9: Midband gain (for completeness)

$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m R_L' = -128.93$$

$$\text{GBW} = 128.93 \times 835 \times 10^{3} \approx 107.6\,\text{MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Miller effect multiplied $C_\mu$ from 2 pF to 260 pF — a **130× amplification**, which is the central reason the CE amplifier has limited high-frequency response.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Configuration Comparison

| Property | **Common-Emitter (CE)** | **Common-Base (CB)** | **Common-Collector (CC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Miller Gain | $-g_m R_L'$ (large) | $\approx 1$ (small) | $\approx 1$ (small) |
| $f_H$ | Lowest | **Highest** | High |
| $f_L$ | Higher (bypass cap) | Lower (no bypass) | Lowest |
| Midband Gain | High | High | $\approx 1$ (current gain) |
| Input Impedance | Medium | **Lowest** | **Highest** |
| Output Impedance | High | Highest | Lowest |
| Use Case | Voltage amplifier | RF/High-freq | Buffer/Impedance match |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Q1. Define the term "Miller Capacitance" with respect to BJT high-frequency analysis. Why is it a bottleneck for CE amplifiers? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Miller Capacitance refers to the equivalent input capacitance that results when a feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ (between base and collector) is reflected to the input side using Miller's Theorem. For a CE stage with gain $A_v' = -g_m R_L'$, the effective input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$$

It becomes a bottleneck because the gain $g_m R_L'$ can be large (e.g., 100), so a tiny $C_\mu$ of 2 pF appears as 200 pF at the input, drastically reducing $f_H$. This is the **principal reason CE amplifiers have limited bandwidth** compared to CB and CC configurations.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

### Q2. What is the significance of the "Short-Circuit Time Constant (SCTC)" method in low-frequency analysis? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The SCTC method, developed by Middlebrook, simplifies the calculation of the lower 3-dB frequency $f_L$ in amplifiers with multiple coupling/bypass capacitors. Instead of deriving the full third-order transfer function and then taking the 3-dB frequency, one can:

1. Short all other capacitors and find the resistance seen by each capacitor individually.
2. Compute each individual pole $f_{Lk} = 1/(2\pi R_k C_k)$.
3. Approximate the overall $f_L \approx \sum f_{Lk}$.

This yields a result accurate to within ~10% of the true $f_L$, making it a high-yield tool for hand calculations in KTU exams.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert $R_s$ into the base-side equivalent $R_s' = R_s \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2$ when computing the high-frequency pole.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $C_\mu$ *without* Miller multiplication — examiners explicitly test for this.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up low-frequency and high-frequency equations (e.g., using $C_\mu$ in the low-frequency region).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to express final answers in **Hz** and to clearly state the assumption of "single-pole approximation."

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Based on High-Frequency Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Q. (a)** For a CE amplifier with $R_s = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 68\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 33\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta_0 = 150$, $I_C = 1.5\,\text{mA}$, $C_\pi = 15\,\text{pF}$, and $C_\mu = 3\,\text{pF}$, determine:
- (i) The midband voltage gain $A_{v(mid)}$
- (ii) The upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$
- (iii) The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $g_m = I_C / V_T = 1.5\,\text{mA} / 25.85\,\text{mV} = 58.03\,\text{mA/V}$

**Step 2:** $r_\pi = \beta_0 / g_m = 150 / (58.03 \times 10^{-3}) = 2585\,\Omega = 2.585\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 3:** $R_B = R_1 \Vert R_2 = (68 \times 33)/(68+33) = 22.11\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 4:** $R_s' = R_s \Vert R_B = (1 \times 22.11)/(1+22.11) = 0.956\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 5:** $R_L' = R_C \Vert R_L = (4.7 \times 10)/(14.7) = 3.197\,\text{k}\Omega$

**(i) Midband Gain:**
$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m R_L' = -58.03 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.197 \times 10^3 = -185.5$$

**Step 6:** $R_{in}' = R_s' \Vert r_\pi = (0.956 \times 2.585)/(0.956 + 2.585) = 0.697\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 7:** Miller input capacitance:
$$C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L') = 3 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 + 185.5) = 559.5\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 8:** $C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = 15 + 559.5 = 574.5\,\text{pF}$

**(ii) Upper Cutoff Frequency:**
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in}' C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 697 \times 574.5 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 397\,\text{kHz}$$

**(iii) GBW:**
$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v(mid)}\vert \times f_H = 185.5 \times 397 \times 10^3 \approx 73.6\,\text{MHz}$$

---

#### **Q. (b)** With the help of Miller's theorem and the hybrid-π model, derive an expression for the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier. Explain why the CE amplifier has poorer high-frequency response compared to the common-base configuration. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to Section 3.2 above. The key derivation is:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_s' \Vert r_\pi)\left[C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')\right]}$$

**Reason for CB superiority:** In a common-base configuration, the input is applied to the emitter and output is taken at the collector. The voltage gain $A_v' \approx g_m R_L'$, but $C_\mu$ is connected from collector to **ground** (since the base is AC-grounded), not as a feedback element. Therefore, the Miller multiplication factor $(1 + g_m R_L')$ does **not** apply, and $f_H$ is typically 10–100 times higher than CE for the same transistor.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Based on Low-Frequency Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Q. (a)** For a CE amplifier with $R_s = 600\,\Omega$, $R_1 = 60\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta_0 = 120$, $C_{in} = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_{out} = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\,\mu\text{F}$, and $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, estimate the lower 3-dB frequency using the SCTC method. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $g_m = 1\,\text{mA} / 25.85\,\text{mV} = 38.68\,\text{mA/V}$

**Step 2:** $r_\pi = 120 / (38.68 \times 10^{-3}) = 3102\,\Omega = 3.10\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 3:** $R_B = (60 \times 30)/(90) = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 4:** $R_{in} = R_B \Vert r_\pi = (20 \times 3.10)/(23.10) = 2.684\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Pole 1 (Input Coupling):**
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_s + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 2684) \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 48.4\,\text{Hz}$$

**Pole 2 (Output Coupling):**
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 15{,}000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 10.6\,\text{Hz}$$

**Pole 3 (Emitter Bypass):**

$R_s' = R_s \Vert R_B = (0.6 \times 20)/20.6 = 0.5826\,\text{k}\Omega$

$R_{in,e} = (R_s' + r_\pi)/(\beta_0 + 1) = (0.5826 + 3.10)/121 = 0.0304\,\text{k}\Omega = 30.4\,\Omega$

$R_e' = R_E \Vert R_{in,e} = (1500 \times 30.4)/1530.4 = 29.8\,\Omega$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_e' C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 29.8 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 53.4\,\text{Hz}$$

**Total Lower Cutoff (SCTC Approximation):**
$$\boxed{f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3} = 48.4 + 10.6 + 53.4 = 112.4\,\text{Hz}}$$

---

#### **Q. (b)** Sketch the Bode magnitude plot for the BJT amplifier's frequency response, clearly labeling the midband gain, the lower 3-dB frequency $f_L$, the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$, and the slopes in each region. Explain what determines each corner frequency. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The Bode plot consists of three straight-line segments:

1. **Low-frequency asymptote** ($f < f_L$): Rises with slope **+20 dB/decade**, intersecting the midband gain line at $f = f_L$.

2. **Midband plateau** ($f_L \le f \le f_H$): Flat horizontal line at magnitude $20\log_{10}\vert A_{v(mid)}\vert$ in dB.

3. **High-frequency asymptote** ($f > f_H$): Falls with slope **−20 dB/decade** (or steeper for multiple poles), intersecting the midband line at $f = f_H$.

**Determinants of Corner Frequencies:**
- $f_L$ is determined by the **external coupling and bypass capacitors** ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$) and the source/bias resistances. Larger capacitors → lower $f_L$.
- $f_H$ is determined by the **internal parasitic capacitances** ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) and the small-signal source resistance. Larger capacitances or higher source resistance → lower $f_H$.

**Reference Sketch (ASCII):**

```
Gain (dB)
   |              ___________________
   |             /                   \
   |            /                     \
Av(mid) dB ----+-----------------------+------
   |          /                         \
   |         /                           \  (-20 dB/decade)
   |        /                             \
   |       /                               \
   |      /                                 \
   |     /                                   \
   |    /  (+20 dB/decade)                    \
   |   /                                       \
   |__/_________|__________________________|__________ log f
              fL                              fH
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ☐ **Three regions of frequency response:** Low (coupling/bypass caps), Midband (flat), High (internal parasitics).
- ☐ **Low-frequency poles** are determined by $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$ via the SCTC method: $f_L \approx \sum f_{Lk}$.
- ☐ **Emitter bypass pole is the dominant low-frequency pole** in most CE designs (because $R_e'$ is small, $C_E$ has to be huge to compensate).
- ☐ **High-frequency response** uses the **hybrid-π model** with $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$.
- ☐ **Miller's Theorem** is the key to handling $C_\mu$: $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')$.
- ☐ **CE amplifiers are bandwidth-limited** by the Miller effect, which can multiply a 2 pF $C_\mu$ by a factor of 100+.
- ☐ **CB and CC amplifiers** do not suffer from Miller multiplication → much higher $f_H$.
- ☐ **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is a constant for a given transistor: GBW $\approx g_m / (2\pi C_\mu)$ for CB.
- ☐ **Slopes on Bode plot:** +20 dB/decade below $f_L$, flat in midband, −20 dB/decade above $f_H$ (for a single pole).
- ☐ **3-dB point** definition: $|A_v(f_c)| = A_{v(mid)}/\sqrt{2}$, equivalently power is halved.
- ☐ **Always use $V_T = 25.85\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K** in $g_m$ and $r_\pi$ calculations.
- ☐ **Always Thevenize** $R_s$ with the bias network ($R_1 \Vert R_2$) for high-frequency calculations.
- ☐ **Units check:** $\Omega \times \text{F} = \text{seconds}$; $\text{Hz} = 1/\text{seconds}$ → final $f_H$ in Hz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FET Amplifier Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FET Amplifier Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**FET Amplifier Frequency Response** describes the variation of the amplifier's gain magnitude and phase as a function of the input signal frequency. It is a complete AC characterization of Field Effect Transistor (FET) amplifier circuits (Common Source, Common Drain, Common Gate) plotted typically as a Bode plot.

The frequency response is conventionally divided into three distinct operational bands:

1. **Low-Frequency Band** (below lower cutoff $f_L$): The gain is attenuated by the reactance of **external coupling capacitors** ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) and the **source-bypass capacitor** ($C_S$).
2. **Mid-Frequency Band** (between $f_L$ and $f_H$): All coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **AC short circuits**, while internal FET capacitances behave as **AC open circuits**. The gain is purely resistive and constant at $A_{mid}$.
3. **High-Frequency Band** (above upper cutoff $f_H$): The gain is attenuated by the internal junction capacitances of the FET, namely **$C_{gs}$ (gate-to-source)**, **$C_{gd}$ (gate-to-drain)**, and **$C_{ds}$ (drain-to-source)**.

The **Bandwidth ($BW$)** of the amplifier is defined as:

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_H \gg f_L)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must master the application of **Miller's Theorem** to convert the feedback capacitance $C_{gd}$ into equivalent input/output shunt capacitances, since this is the most heavily tested concept in the frequency response module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a FET amplifier as a **home audio speaker system**:

- At very low frequencies (**bass region**): The heavy coupling capacitors act like a **closed door** — they "refuse" to let slow signals through, similar to how a heavy door resists being pushed open slowly. The bass you hear is muffled.
- At mid frequencies (**vocal region**): The capacitors become **invisible wires**, and the speaker works perfectly. This is the **"sweet spot"** where music sounds natural.
- At very high frequencies (**treble region**): The microscopic internal capacitances inside the FET become tiny **leaky valves** that shunt high-frequency signals to ground before amplification. Treble becomes muffled too.

The frequency response curve is therefore a **"bandpass" curve** — like a hill — where the flat top represents faithful amplification, and the two sloped sides represent signal loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A perfect amplifier would have $f_L = 0$ Hz and $f_H = \infty$ Hz. Real FETs are limited by **physics** — coupling capacitors are large discrete components, and junction capacitances are unavoidable due to the depletion regions inside the FET.

## 1.3 Standard FET Capacitance Parameters (Bold Constants)

The internal capacitances of a FET (JFET or MOSFET) are specified in datasheets using standardized notation:

- **$C_{iss}$ (Input Capacitance)** = $C_{gs} + C_{gd}$ (with output shorted to AC ground)
- **$C_{oss}$ (Output Capacitance)** = $C_{ds} + C_{gd}$ (with input shorted to AC ground)
- **$C_{rss}$ (Reverse Transfer Capacitance)** = $C_{gd}$ (the Miller capacitance)

These parameters are critical for high-frequency design and are typically in the range of **1 pF to 50 pF** for small-signal FETs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three FET Internal Capacitances

The FET is a three-terminal device, and each pair of terminals has an associated parasitic capacitance formed by the reverse-biased PN junctions (in JFET) or the oxide layer and depletion region (in MOSFET):

- **$C_{gs}$**: Gate-to-Source capacitance — input side
- **$C_{gd}$**: Gate-to-Drain capacitance — feedback (Miller) capacitance
- **$C_{ds}$**: Drain-to-Source capacitance — output side

The physical **why**: When a FET operates in the saturation (active) region, the gate-to-channel junction is reverse-biased. A reverse-biased junction behaves like a **parallel-plate capacitor** whose plates are separated by a depletion region. The width of this depletion region is **frequency-dependent** (via charge storage), which is why these capacitances vary with operating point.

## 2.2 Low-Frequency Response Analysis

At low frequencies, the **mid-band gain** is established by the AC small-signal model. As frequency decreases, the reactance of the **coupling and bypass capacitors** increases:

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$$

When $X_C$ becomes comparable to the associated resistance, the voltage divider effect of the capacitor attenuates the signal.

For a **Common Source (CS) amplifier** with a source-bypass capacitor $C_S$, the dominant low-frequency pole is typically introduced by the source network:

$$f_{L(S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' C_S}$$

where $R_S' = R_S \parallel \left(\frac{1}{g_m}\right) = \frac{R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$.

For the input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$:

$$f_{L(in)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_{in}') C_{in}}$$

## 2.3 Miller's Theorem — The Critical Tool

**Miller's Theorem** is the cornerstone of high-frequency FET amplifier analysis. It states that an impedance $Z$ connected between input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ can be **split into two equivalent impedances**:

- **Miller Input Impedance**: $Z_{in(M)} = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- **Miller Output Impedance**: $Z_{out(M)} = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$

For a **capacitive** feedback element $Z = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{gd}}$, applied to a CS amplifier with gain $A_v = -g_m R_D'$:

$$C_{gd(in)} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

$$C_{gd(out)} = C_{gd}\left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_{gd} \quad (\text{for } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Miller's Theorem matters:** The term $C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$ is called the **Miller Capacitance**. It can become **hundreds of times larger** than $C_{gd}$ alone, drastically reducing the high-frequency cutoff. This is the single most important reason FET amplifiers have limited bandwidth.

## 2.4 High-Frequency Response Analysis

After applying Miller's theorem, the high-frequency response is determined by the **RC low-pass filter** formed at the input:

$$C_{in(total)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd(in)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

The **upper cutoff frequency** for the CS amplifier is:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(total)}}$$

where $R_{eq(in)} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$ (signal source resistance in parallel with gate bias resistance).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Mid-band Voltage Gain (CS) | $A_v = -g_m R_D'$ | Negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion |
| Miller Input Capacitance | $C_{M(in)} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$ | Boosted feedback capacitance at input |
| Total Input Capacitance | $C_{in(T)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$ | Dominant pole capacitance |
| Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(T)}}$ | High-frequency −3 dB point |
| Lower Cutoff (Source) | $f_{L(S)} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_S}{2\pi R_S C_S}$ | Bypass capacitor pole |
| Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Useful frequency range |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = \dfrac{g_m R_D'}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(T)}}$ | Constant for a given FET/circuit |
| Common Drain (CD) Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'}$ | Unity-gain buffer approximation |
| Common Gate (CG) Gain | $A_v = g_m R_D'$ | Non-inverting, high BW |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FET amplifier frequency response is fundamental in the design of:

- **RF Front-End Amplifiers** in radio receivers (e.g., 88–108 MHz FM band)
- **Wideband Oscilloscope Probes** requiring flat response from DC to GHz
- **Audio Preamplifiers** where $f_L$ must be $< 20$ Hz and $f_H > 20$ kHz
- **High-Speed Data Acquisition Systems** in biomedical instrumentation
- **Antenna Preamplifiers** in satellite communication systems

A deep understanding of bandwidth limitations allows engineers to select appropriate FETs (e.g., GaAs FETs for microwave applications) and design **compensation networks** to extend the response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Upper Cutoff Frequency of a Common Source FET Amplifier

**Problem Statement:** Derive the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ for a Common Source (CS) JFET amplifier with the following specifications:
- $g_m = 3$ mS (transconductance)
- $R_D = 4.7$ kΩ (drain resistor)
- $R_{sig} = 1$ kΩ (source resistance)
- $R_G = 1$ MΩ (gate bias resistor)
- $C_{gs} = 4$ pF, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, $C_{ds} = 1$ pF

### Step 1: Determine the AC Drain Resistance

Assuming a current-source biasing arrangement (or that the bypass capacitor fully bypasses $R_S$ at mid-band), the AC drain resistance is $R_D' = R_D \parallel r_d$. For simplicity, neglect $r_d$ (assuming $r_d \to \infty$):

$$R_D' = R_D = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 2: Compute the Mid-Band Voltage Gain

$$A_v = -g_m R_D' = -(3 \times 10^{-3})(4.7 \times 10^3) = -14.1$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** characteristic of CS amplifiers.

### Step 3: Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{gd}$

The feedback capacitance $C_{gd}$ is split into a Miller input capacitance:

$$C_{gd(in)} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 - (-14.1)) = 2 \text{ pF} \times 15.1 = 30.2 \text{ pF}$$

And a Miller output capacitance (much smaller, often neglected):

$$C_{gd(out)} = C_{gd}\left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}\right) = 2 \text{ pF} \times \left(\frac{-15.1}{-14.1}\right) \approx 2.14 \text{ pF}$$

### Step 4: Compute Total Input Capacitance

$$C_{in(T)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd(in)} = 4 \text{ pF} + 30.2 \text{ pF} = 34.2 \text{ pF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The original $C_{gs} = 4$ pF is now negligible compared to the Miller-boosted $C_{gd(in)} = 30.2$ pF. This shows why **Miller effect dominates** FET high-frequency response.

### Step 5: Compute the Equivalent Input Resistance

$$R_{eq(in)} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G = \frac{R_{sig} \cdot R_G}{R_{sig} + R_G}$$

$$R_{eq(in)} = \frac{(1 \text{ k}\Omega)(1000 \text{ k}\Omega)}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + 1000 \text{ k}\Omega} \approx 999 \text{ }\Omega \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Upper Cutoff Frequency

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(T)}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (1 \times 10^3)(34.2 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.42 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2.149 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{f_H \approx 4.65 \text{ MHz}}$$

### Step 7: Calculate the Gain-Bandwidth Product

$$GBP = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = 14.1 \times 4.65 \times 10^6 \approx 65.6 \text{ MHz}$$

This is a useful **figure of merit** — for a given FET and circuit, the product of mid-band gain and bandwidth is approximately constant.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lower Cutoff Frequency Due to Source Bypass Capacitor

For a CS amplifier with an unbypassed source resistor $R_S$, the gain is reduced. Adding a bypass capacitor $C_S$ in parallel with $R_S$ restores the gain at high frequencies. The lower cutoff frequency introduced by $C_S$ is derived as follows:

### Step 1: Express the Impedance of the Source Network

The total source impedance is the parallel combination of $R_S$ and $C_S$:

$$Z_S = R_S \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_S} = \frac{R_S}{1 + j\omega R_S C_S}$$

### Step 2: Determine the AC Source Resistance "Seen" by the FET

The effective source degeneration resistance, accounting for the loading of the FET's transconductance, is:

$$Z_S' = Z_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{Z_S / g_m}{Z_S + 1/g_m}$$

### Step 3: Find the Frequency at Which $Z_S$ Equals $1/g_m$

The pole occurs when the magnitude of $Z_S$ equals $1/g_m$:

$$\left\vert Z_S \right\vert = \frac{R_S}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_S C_S)^2}} = \frac{1}{g_m}$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Cutoff Frequency

$$1 + (\omega R_S C_S)^2 = (g_m R_S)^2$$

$$(\omega_L R_S C_S)^2 = (g_m R_S)^2 - 1$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{\sqrt{(g_m R_S)^2 - 1}}{R_S C_S}$$

For the typical case $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$f_L \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_S}$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis: CS vs CD vs CG Frequency Response

| **Parameter** | **Common Source (CS)** | **Common Drain (CD)** | **Common Gate (CG)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain | $-g_m R_D'$ (high, inverting) | $\dfrac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'}$ (≈1, non-inv.) | $g_m R_D'$ (high, non-inv.) |
| Input Impedance | Very High ($R_G$) | Very High ($R_G$) | Low ($1/g_m$) |
| $C_{gd}$ Miller Effect | **Severe** (multiplied by gain) | Negligible (gain ≈ 1) | **None** (gate is grounded) |
| Bandwidth | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Typical Use | General-purpose amp | Buffer/Impedance matcher | RF/UHF amplifier |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: FET Amplifier Frequency Response Partitioning

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Bode Plot of FET Amplifier Gain

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B -->|f less than fL| C[LOW FREQUENCY BAND]
    B -->|fL to fH| D[MID BAND Constant Gain Amid]
    B -->|f greater than fH| E[HIGH FREQUENCY BAND]

    C --> F[Gain rises at plus 20 dB per decade slope]
    D --> G[Gain is flat at Amid in dB]
    E --> H[Gain falls at minus 20 dB per decade slope]

    subgraph CUTOFFS [CUTOFF FREQUENCY POINTS]
        I[Lower Cutoff fL minus 3 dB point]
        J[Upper Cutoff fH minus 3 dB point]
    end

    F --> I
    G --> I
    G --> J
    H --> J

    style C fill:#4472c4,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Common Source Amplifier AC Equivalent with Capacitances

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT_NET [INPUT COUPLING NETWORK]
        A1[Vsig with Rsiq] --> A2[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
        A2 --> A3[Gate Node]
    end

    subgraph FET_MODEL [FET SMALL SIGNAL MODEL]
        A3 -->|Cgs to source| A4[Source Node AC Ground]
        A3 -->|Cgd to drain| A5[Drain Node]
        A4 --> A6[Current Source gm Vgs]
        A6 --> A5
        A5 -->|Cds to source| A4
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_NET [OUTPUT COUPLING NETWORK]
        A5 --> A7[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
        A5 -.->|RD| A4
        A7 --> A8[Load Resistor RL]
    end

    subgraph MILLER_APPLIED [MILLER TRANSFORM APPLIED]
        A3 -->|Cin total equals Cgs plus Cgd times 1 plus Av| A4
        A5 -->|Cout total equals Cds plus Cgd| A4
    end

    style A3 fill:#ff8c00,color:#000000
    style A5 fill:#ff8c00,color:#000000
    style A6 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Miller's Theorem as applied to FET amplifier analysis. Why is the Miller capacitance $C_{gd}$ particularly significant in determining the high-frequency response of a Common Source amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

Miller's Theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between the input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two equivalent shunt impedances:

- **Miller Input Impedance:** $Z_{in(M)} = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- **Miller Output Impedance:** $Z_{out(M)} = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$

For the FET's gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ in a CS amplifier with gain $A_v = -g_m R_D'$, the Miller input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{gd(in)} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

This is significant because **$g_m R_D'$ is typically large (10 to 100)**, so the Miller-boosted capacitance at the input node can be **tens to hundreds of times larger** than the physical $C_{gd}$. This multiplied capacitance directly determines the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')]}$$

Consequently, $C_{gd}$ becomes the **dominant factor limiting bandwidth**, even though its physical value is small.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the high-frequency response of Common Source (CS) and Common Gate (CG) FET amplifiers. Which configuration offers the widest bandwidth and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Common Source (CS)** | **Common Gate (CG)** |
|---|---|---|
| Input Capacitance Affected | **Severely** by Miller effect on $C_{gd}$ | **Not affected** (gate is AC grounded) |
| Voltage Gain | $-g_m R_D'$ (inverting, high) | $g_m R_D'$ (non-inverting, high) |
| Bandwidth | **Lowest** (Miller capacitance limits $f_H$) | **Highest** (no Miller multiplication) |
| Input Impedance | Very high (gate is input) | Very low ($= 1/g_m$) |

The **Common Gate (CG) configuration offers the widest bandwidth** because:
1. The gate is connected directly to AC ground, so the input signal is applied to the source.
2. Since the gate is grounded, there is **no voltage gain from input (source) to the gate node**, so Miller's theorem produces a factor of unity — **no capacitance multiplication occurs**.
3. The dominant input capacitance is simply $C_{gs}$ (in parallel with $1/g_m$), which is much smaller than the Miller-boosted $C_{gd}$ in CS.

Therefore, $f_H^{CG} \gg f_H^{CS}$ for the same FET and load resistance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** With the help of a high-frequency equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ of a Common Source FET amplifier. Clearly state the role of Miller's theorem in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A JFET CS amplifier has the following parameters: $g_m = 2.5$ mS, $r_d = 50$ kΩ, $R_D = 5$ kΩ, $R_G = 1$ MΩ, $R_{sig} = 1$ kΩ, $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, $C_{ds} = 1$ pF. Calculate (i) the mid-band voltage gain, (ii) the Miller input capacitance, and (iii) the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Draw the high-frequency equivalent circuit** of the CS amplifier using the FET small-signal model. The model includes:
- Voltage-controlled current source $g_m V_{gs}$
- Output resistance $r_d$
- Three inter-terminal capacitances: $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{ds}$
- Source resistance $R_{sig}$ and gate bias resistor $R_G$
- Drain resistor $R_D$ and load $R_L$

**Step 2: Define the total drain resistance:**

$$R_D' = R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L$$

For the high-frequency analysis, the **mid-band gain** (assumed constant in the passband) is:

$$A_v = -g_m R_D'$$

**Step 3: Apply Miller's Theorem to the feedback capacitance $C_{gd}$:**

The impedance $Z_{gd} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{gd}}$ connects the input node ($V_i$) and output node ($V_o$).

$$C_{gd(in)} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

$$C_{gd(out)} = C_{gd}\left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_{gd} \quad (\text{for } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1)$$

**Step 4: Combine capacitances at the input node:**

$$C_{in(T)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd(in)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

**Step 5: Identify the dominant pole.** The input node forms a low-pass filter with the equivalent input resistance:

$$R_{eq(in)} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$$

**Step 6: Calculate $f_H$:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(T)}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')]}$$

**Role of Miller's theorem:** It transforms the **feedback** capacitance $C_{gd}$ into a **shunt (lumped)** capacitance at the input, enabling the use of simple RC low-pass filter analysis. Without Miller's theorem, the input and output would be coupled, making analysis intractable by inspection.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $g_m = 2.5$ mS, $r_d = 50$ kΩ, $R_D = 5$ kΩ, $R_G = 1$ MΩ, $R_{sig} = 1$ kΩ, $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, $C_{ds} = 1$ pF

**(i) Mid-band Voltage Gain:**

$$R_D' = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{5 \text{ k}\Omega \times 50 \text{ k}\Omega}{5 \text{ k}\Omega + 50 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{250}{55} \approx 4.545 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -g_m R_D' = -(2.5 \times 10^{-3})(4.545 \times 10^3) = -11.36$$

**(ii) Miller Input Capacitance:**

$$C_{gd(in)} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D') = 2 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 11.36) = 2 \text{ pF} \times 12.36 = 24.72 \text{ pF}$$

**Total input capacitance:**

$$C_{in(T)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd(in)} = 5 \text{ pF} + 24.72 \text{ pF} = 29.72 \text{ pF}$$

**(iii) Upper Cutoff Frequency:**

$$R_{eq(in)} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega \times 1000 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + 1000 \text{ k}\Omega} \approx 999 \text{ }\Omega \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} C_{in(T)}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (1 \times 10^3) \times (29.72 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.972 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{1.867 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{f_H \approx 5.35 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the low-frequency response of a CS FET amplifier. Derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency due to the source-bypass capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CS FET amplifier has $g_m = 4$ mS, $R_S = 1$ kΩ, $R_{sig} = 600$ Ω, $R_G = 1$ MΩ. The coupling capacitor is $C_{in} = 0.1$ μF, and the source-bypass capacitor is $C_S = 100$ μF. Determine (i) the input pole frequency $f_{L(in)}$ and (ii) the source-bypass pole frequency $f_{L(S)}$. Comment on which pole dominates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Identify the three low-frequency poles** in a typical CS amplifier:
- Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$
- Output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$
- Source-bypass capacitor $C_S$

**Step 2: Effect on gain.** As frequency decreases, the reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ of these capacitors increases, attenuating the signal through the voltage divider effect. The gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** per pole.

**Step 3: Source-bypass capacitor analysis.** The CS amplifier typically has a source resistor $R_S$ for bias stability. At low frequencies, $R_S$ introduces **negative feedback**, reducing the gain from $-g_m R_D'$ to:

$$A_v(low) = \frac{-g_m R_D'}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

When the bypass capacitor $C_S$ is added in parallel with $R_S$, at high frequencies $C_S$ becomes a short circuit, fully bypassing $R_S$ and restoring the maximum gain.

**Step 4: Derive $f_{L(S)}$.** The total impedance at the source node is:

$$Z_S = R_S \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_S} = \frac{R_S}{1 + j\omega R_S C_S}$$

The pole occurs when $|Z_S| = \dfrac{1}{g_m}$:

$$\frac{R_S}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_S C_S)^2}} = \frac{1}{g_m}$$

Squaring and solving:

$$1 + (\omega R_S C_S)^2 = (g_m R_S)^2$$

$$(\omega_L R_S C_S)^2 = (g_m R_S)^2 - 1$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{\sqrt{(g_m R_S)^2 - 1}}{R_S C_S}$$

For $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$\boxed{f_{L(S)} \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_S}}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $g_m = 4$ mS, $R_S = 1$ kΩ, $R_{sig} = 600$ Ω, $R_G = 1$ MΩ, $C_{in} = 0.1$ μF, $C_S = 100$ μF

**(i) Input pole frequency $f_{L(in)}$:**

$$R_{in}' = R_{sig} \parallel R_G = \frac{600 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{600 + 1{,}000{,}000} \approx 599.6 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_{L(in)} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in}' C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 599.6 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{L(in)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.996 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{3.767 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{L(in)} \approx 2655 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.66 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Source-bypass pole frequency $f_{L(S)}$:**

Since $g_m R_S = (4 \times 10^{-3})(1 \times 10^3) = 4 \gg 1$, use the simplified formula:

$$f_{L(S)} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_S} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{L(S)} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{6.283 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.37 \text{ Hz}$$

Using the exact formula:

$$f_{L(S)} = \frac{\sqrt{(g_m R_S)^2 - 1}}{2\pi R_S C_S} = \frac{\sqrt{16 - 1}}{2\pi \times 10^3 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.873}{0.6283}$$

$$\boxed{f_{L(S)} \approx 6.16 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Comment on dominant pole:** The **input coupling capacitor pole** $f_{L(in)} \approx 2.66$ kHz is the **dominant low-frequency pole** because it is approximately **430 times higher** than the source-bypass pole. The overall lower cutoff frequency is determined by the highest pole:

$$f_L \approx f_{L(in)} = 2.66 \text{ kHz}$$

This illustrates that the source-bypass capacitor is very effective at low frequencies because of the $g_m$ multiplication effect in the denominator.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Miller's Theorem:** A very common mistake is using $C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ with $A_v$ positive. Always remember that for a CS amplifier, $A_v$ is **negative**, so $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. Students often lose **2-3 marks** here.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the parallel combination:** When calculating $R_{eq(in)} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$, students sometimes simply use $R_{sig}$ alone. For a FET, $R_G$ is in the MΩ range, so this is approximately $R_{sig}$, but in exact derivations, the parallel formula is required.
>
> 3. **Unit confusion in capacitance:** A frequent error is using capacitance in μF (microfarads) instead of pF (picofarads) when computing $f_H$. Since $f_H$ involves very small capacitances, always convert: **1 pF = 10⁻¹² F**.
>
> 4. **Neglecting $r_d$:** In some derivations, $r_d$ (drain-to-source resistance of the FET) is significant. The total drain resistance is $R_D' = R_D \parallel r_d$, not just $R_D$ alone. Failing to include $r_d$ leads to an overestimated gain and incorrect $f_H$.
>
> 5. **Confusing $C_{iss}$, $C_{oss}$, $C_{rss}$:** Datasheet parameters $C_{iss}$, $C_{oss}$, and $C_{rss}$ are **not** the same as $C_{gs}$, $C_{ds}$, and $C_{gd}$ directly. They are **short-circuit** measurements. Use the relationships carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **FET has three internal capacitances**: $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{ds}$ — all in the picofarad range.
- [x] **Miller's Theorem** transforms $C_{gd}$ into a **larger input capacitance** $C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$, which dominates the high-frequency response.
- [x] **Low-frequency response** is governed by **external coupling and bypass capacitors** (typically in μF range).
- [x] **High-frequency response** is governed by **internal FET capacitances** (typically in pF range).
- [x] **Common Source (CS)**: Highest gain but lowest bandwidth due to Miller effect.
- [x] **Common Drain (CD)**: Unity-gain buffer; moderate bandwidth; no Miller effect (gain ≈ 1).
- [x] **Common Gate (CG)**: Highest bandwidth; low input impedance; no Miller effect.
- [x] **Upper cutoff formula:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq(in)} [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')]}$
- [x] **Lower cutoff (source bypass):** $f_{L(S)} \approx \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_S}$ when $g_m R_S \gg 1$.
- [x] **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is approximately constant for a given FET configuration.
- [x] **Datasheet symbols**: $C_{iss} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}$, $C_{oss} = C_{ds} + C_{gd}$, $C_{rss} = C_{gd}$.
- [x] **Phase shift:** CS amplifier produces 180° phase inversion at mid-band; CD and CG are non-inverting.
- [x] **Bode plot slopes:** +20 dB/decade below $f_L$, flat in mid-band, −20 dB/decade above $f_H$.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** RF amplifiers, audio preamps, wideband buffers, oscilloscope front-ends.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback and Oscillator Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback and Oscillator Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Feedback?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Feedback** in an electronic amplifier is the process of returning a portion of the output signal back to the input, where it is combined with the original input signal. This returned signal (called the *feedback signal*) alters the net input, thereby modifying the amplifier's overall gain and performance characteristics.

There are two fundamental types of feedback in analog electronics:

- **Negative Feedback (NFB):** The feedback signal is *out of phase* (180°) with the input. It **reduces** the overall gain but **improves** stability, bandwidth, input impedance, and reduces distortion/noise.
- **Positive Feedback (PFB):** The feedback signal is *in phase* (0°) with the input. It **increases** gain and is the foundation of **oscillator circuits**, which generate continuous sinusoidal waveforms without any external AC input.

## 1.2 The Block Diagram of a Feedback System

The classical closed-loop feedback topology consists of two fundamental blocks:

- **Amplifier Block (A):** The basic open-loop amplifier with voltage gain $A$.
- **Feedback Network (β):** A passive network (resistors, capacitors, or inductors) that samples the output and produces a feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$.

The **summing junction** (Σ) subtracts $V_f$ from the input $V_s$ (for NFB) to produce the error signal $V_{\epsilon} = V_s - V_f$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Steering Wheel of a Car**
> Imagine driving a car. The *steering wheel* is the input, the *car's direction* is the output, and your *eyes* continuously sense the output (the road) and feed it back to your brain (the input) to correct the path.
> - If you over-steer (too much "feedback"), the car wobbles (oscillation!).
> - If you gently correct (small negative feedback), the drive is smooth and stable.
> - If your brain ignores the road entirely and adds more steering (positive feedback), the car swerves wildly out of control — just like a runaway oscillator!

In amplifiers:
- **Negative feedback** = *gentle correction* → stable, accurate, high-fidelity amplifiers.
- **Positive feedback** = *runaway amplification* → oscillators (Barkhausen criterion satisfied).

## 1.4 The Four Feedback Topologies

The classification depends on **what is sampled** at the output and **how it is mixed** at the input.

| Sampling Quantity | Mixing Quantity | Topology Name | Key Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output Voltage | Series (voltage) | **Voltage-Series** | ↑ $R_i$, ↓ $R_o$ (Voltage Amp) |
| Output Voltage | Shunt (current) | **Voltage-Shunt** | ↓ $R_i$, ↓ $R_o$ (Transresistance) |
| Output Current | Series (voltage) | **Current-Series** | ↑ $R_i$, ↑ $R_o$ (Transconductance) |
| Output Current | Shunt (current) | **Current-Shunt** | ↓ $R_i$, ↑ $R_o$ (Current Amp) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Convention:** "Series" mixing means the feedback voltage appears in series with the source (R increases). "Shunt" mixing means the feedback current is in parallel (R decreases). Voltage sampling reduces $R_o$; Current sampling increases $R_o$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Feedback Equation

The closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier is given by the **Abernathy Equation**:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Open-loop gain of the amplifier
- $\beta$ = Reverse transmission factor (feedback fraction)
- $A\beta$ = **Loop Gain** (return ratio)
- $1 + A\beta$ = **Return Difference** (desensitivity factor)

### 2.1.1 The Two Critical Conditions

1. **If $|1 + A\beta| \gg 1$:** $A_f \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ — Gain is determined *solely* by the feedback network, not the active device.
2. **If $|1 + A\beta| < 0$:** The amplifier becomes an **oscillator** (Barkhausen condition).

## 2.2 Effects of Negative Feedback (Top 7 — KTU Hot List)

- **Gain Reduction:** $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$
- **Gain Stability:** $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$
- **Bandwidth Increase:** $(BW)_f = (BW)(1+A\beta)$
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $A \cdot BW = A_f \cdot (BW)_f$ (constant for a single-pole system)
- **Input Resistance (Series mixing):** $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$
- **Input Resistance (Shunt mixing):** $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$
- **Output Resistance (Voltage sampling):** $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$
- **Output Resistance (Current sampling):** $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$
- **Noise Reduction:** Output noise voltage is reduced by $(1+A\beta)$.
- **Distortion Reduction:** Harmonic distortion reduces by factor $(1+A\beta)$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Without Feedback | With Negative Feedback | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Voltage Gain | $A$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW_f = BW(1+A\beta)$ | $\text{Hz}$ |
| 4 | Input Resistance (Series) | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Input Resistance (Shunt) | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Output Resistance (Voltage) | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Output Resistance (Current) | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Feedback Voltage | — | $V_f = \beta V_o$ | $\text{V}$ |
| 9 | Loop Gain | — | $T = A\beta$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Barkhausen Frequency | — | $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ | $\text{Hz}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hi-Fi Audio Amplifiers:** Negative feedback (typically 20–40 dB) is used to reduce Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) to < 0.01%.
- **Op-Amp Precision:** The IC 741 uses voltage-series NFB to achieve a near-ideal $R_i = 2\,\text{M}\Omega$ and $R_o < 75\,\Omega$.
- **Telecom Signal Generators:** LC oscillators (Colpitts, Hartley) generate carrier frequencies in the **MHz to GHz** range for AM/FFM transmitters.
- **Quartz Wristwatches & Microcontrollers:** Crystal oscillators provide a 32.768 kHz reference with frequency stability of $\pm 1\,\text{ppm}$ (parts per million).
- **Function Generators:** Wien Bridge oscillators are used in lab equipment to produce low-distortion sine waves from 1 Hz to 1 MHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Gain $A_f$

**Step 1: Define the feedback signal.**
The feedback network samples the output $V_o$ and produces:
$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff at the summing junction (Σ).**
For negative feedback, the error signal at the amplifier input is:
$$V_{\epsilon} = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 3: Apply the open-loop amplifier definition.**
The amplifier multiplies $V_{\epsilon}$ by its open-loop gain $A$:
$$V_o = A \cdot V_{\epsilon} = A(V_s - V_f)$$

**Step 4: Substitute the feedback relationship.**
$$V_o = A \cdot V_s - A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 5: Collect $V_o$ terms on the left side.**
$$V_o + A\beta V_o = A \cdot V_s$$

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 6: Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f = V_o/V_s$.**
$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-derived equation** in KTU analog electronics. Memorize it. For $A\beta \gg 1$, the gain becomes $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, depending only on the passive feedback network.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gain Stability with NFB

Starting from the basic feedback equation:
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $A$ (treating $\beta$ as a constant):
$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{(1+A\beta)(1) - A(\beta)}{(1+A\beta)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

Divide by $A_f$:
$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{dA}{(1+A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{(1+A\beta)}{A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

This shows that the percentage change in closed-loop gain is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: RC Phase-Shift Oscillator Frequency

The RC phase-shift oscillator uses a single BJT inverting amplifier ($A$ is inverting, $A = -A$) with a three-stage RC ladder providing 180° phase shift.

The standard ladder network consists of three equal sections: $R$ and $C$. The oscillation frequency is derived by setting the imaginary part of the loop gain to zero. The classical result is:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$

And the **minimum gain required** (Barkhausen magnitude condition) is:

$$\boxed{A_{min} = 29}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency

The Wien Bridge uses a non-inverting amplifier ($\angle A = 0°$) with a lead-lag RC network. The feedback network is a series RC in parallel with a parallel RC:

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} \quad ; \quad Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The feedback factor is:
$$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

After algebraic simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$), we obtain:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

For oscillation, the imaginary part must vanish:
$$\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC} \Rightarrow \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

The feedback factor at this frequency is:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{3}$$

Since $A_f = 1/\beta$ for oscillation, the amplifier must have:
$$\boxed{A_{min} = 3}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Colpitts Oscillator Frequency

The Colpitts oscillator uses a **capacitive voltage divider** ($C_1$ and $C_2$) with a single inductor $L$. The tank circuit resonant frequency is:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}}$$

where the equivalent capacitance of the series combination is:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

The condition for sustained oscillation requires the amplifier gain to overcome the feedback attenuation:
$$A_{min} = \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

## 3.6 Derivation: Hartley Oscillator Frequency

The Hartley uses an **inductive voltage divider** ($L_1$ and $L_2$) with a single capacitor $C$:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}}$$

where the equivalent inductance is:
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$

($M$ = mutual inductance between $L_1$ and $L_2$). The condition for oscillation:
$$A_{min} = \frac{L_1}{L_2}$$

## 3.7 Barkhausen Criterion (Master Equation)

For sustained sinusoidal oscillation, **two simultaneous conditions** must be met:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The loop gain magnitude must be exactly **unity**:
$$|A\beta| = 1$$

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of 360°)**:
$$\angle A + \angle \beta = 0° \text{ or } 360° \cdot n$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $|A\beta|$ is kept *slightly greater* than 1 to compensate for device non-idealities, and amplitude-limiting circuits (thermistor, diodes, JFET) are used to stabilize the output.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Negative Feedback Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vs[Input Vs] --> SUM{Summing Junction}
    SUM -->|V_eps| A[Amplifier Block A]
    A -->|V_o| Out[Output V_o]
    Out --> Load[Load RL]
    Out --> Beta[Feedback Network beta]
    Beta -->|V_f| SUM
    style SUM fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style A fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
    style Beta fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Four Feedback Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[Input Source] --> Mixer{Mixing Type}
    Mixer -->|Series| SeriesMix[Series Mixing]
    Mixer -->|Shunt| ShuntMix[Shunt Mixing]
    SeriesMix --> VSAmp[Voltage-Series Amplifier]
    ShuntMix --> VShAmp[Voltage-Shunt Amplifier]
    Amp[Vout] --> Sampler{Sampling Type}
    Sampler -->|Voltage Node| VSample[Voltage Sampling]
    Sampler -->|Current Loop| ISample[Current Sampling]
    VSample --> VSAmp
    VSample --> VShAmp
    ISample --> CSAmp[Current-Series Amplifier]
    ISample --> CShAmp[Current-Shunt Amplifier]
    style VSAmp fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333
    style VShAmp fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#333
    style CSAmp fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333
    style CShAmp fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: RC Phase-Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InAmp[Inverting Amplifier] -->|180 deg| RC1[RC Section 1 - 60 deg]
    RC1 --> RC2[RC Section 2 - 60 deg]
    RC2 --> RC3[RC Section 3 - 60 deg]
    RC3 --> FbBack[Feedback to Inverting Input]
    FbBack --> InAmp
    Out[Output Sine Wave f = 1 by 2 pi RC sqrt 6]
    InAmp --> Out
    style InAmp fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333
    style RC1 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
    style RC2 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
    style RC3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NonInv[Non-Inverting Amplifier A = 3] --> Out[Output Sine Wave]
    Out --> Zs[Series RC Branch Zs]
    Zs --> Node[Common Node]
    Node --> Zp[Parallel RC Branch Zp]
    Zp --> Gnd[Ground]
    Node -->|Beta = 1 by 3| NonInv
    Out -->|f = 1 by 2 pi RC| Final[Output]
    style NonInv fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
    style Zs fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style Zp fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Colpitts vs Hartley Oscillator Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Colpitts[Colpitts Oscillator] --> C1T[C1 Capacitor]
    Colpitts --> C2T[C2 Capacitor]
    Colpitts --> L_Inductor_Tank[L Inductor Tank]
    C1T --> Tank1[Tank Circuit f = 1 by 2 pi sqrt L times Ceq]
    C2T --> Tank1
    L_Inductor_Tank --> Tank1
    Hartley[Hartley Oscillator] --> L1T[L1 Inductor]
    Hartley --> L2T[L2 Inductor]
    Hartley --> C_Capacitor_Tank[C Capacitor Tank]
    L1T --> Tank2[Tank Circuit f = 1 by 2 pi sqrt Leq times C]
    L2T --> Tank2
    C_Capacitor_Tank --> Tank2

```
