# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the four basic feedback connection types. How are they classified based on sampling and mixing?**

**Model Answer:**

The four basic feedback connection types are determined by two design choices: the **output sampling** method (voltage or current) and the **input mixing** method (series or shunt).

The four topologies are:
1. **Voltage-Series Feedback:** Output voltage is sampled (shunt connection at output); feedback signal is mixed in series with the input source.
2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback:** Output voltage is sampled; feedback signal is mixed in shunt (parallel) with the input source.
3. **Current-Series Feedback:** Output current is sampled (series connection at output); feedback signal is mixed in series with the input.
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback:** Output current is sampled; feedback signal is mixed in shunt with the input.

Classification Rule: *Sampling name is listed first, mixing name is listed second.*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: For a Voltage-Series feedback amplifier, derive the expression for input resistance with feedback.**

**Model Answer:**

For a Voltage-Series feedback amplifier, the input resistance with feedback is given by:
$$R_{if} = R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

Where:
- $R_i$ is the input resistance of the basic amplifier (without feedback).
- $A$ is the open-loop voltage gain.
- $\beta$ is the feedback factor (voltage ratio, dimensionless).
- $(1 + A\beta)$ is the desensitivity factor or amount of feedback.

Since series mixing *increases* input resistance, the Voltage-Series feedback amplifier behaves as an excellent voltage amplifier with very high input impedance — ideal for cascading stages where loading must be minimized.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Both choices are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice 1]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the four types of feedback topologies in amplifiers. Discuss how each topology affects the input and output resistance of the amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop gain of $A = 1000$, input resistance $R_i = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, output resistance $R_o = 40 \Omega$, and feedback factor $\beta = 0.04$. It uses **Voltage-Series Feedback**. Calculate:
- (i) Closed-loop gain $A_f$
- (ii) Input resistance with feedback $R_{if}$
- (iii) Output resistance with feedback $R_{of}$
- (iv) Loop gain $A\beta$ **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The four basic feedback topologies are classified by their **sampling** (output) and **mixing** (input) configuration:

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt):** Samples output voltage (shunt at output), mixes in series at input. **Effect:** $R_{if}$ increases by $(1+A\beta)$, $R_{of}$ decreases by $(1+A\beta)$. Acts as an ideal voltage amplifier.

2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt):** Samples output voltage, mixes in shunt at input. **Effect:** $R_{if}$ decreases by $(1+A\beta)$, $R_{of}$ decreases by $(1+A\beta)$. Acts as a transresistance (V-to-I to V) amplifier.

3. **Current-Series Feedback (Series-Series):** Samples output current (series at output), mixes in series at input. **Effect:** $R_{if}$ increases by $(1+A\beta)$, $R_{of}$ increases by $(1+A\beta)$. Acts as a transconductance amplifier.

4. **Current-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Series):** Samples output current, mixes in shunt at input. **Effect:** $R_{if}$ decreases by $(1+A\beta)$, $R_{of}$ increases by $(1+A\beta)$. Acts as an ideal current amplifier.

**Summary table:**

| Topology | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voltage-Series | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |
| Current-Series | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |
| Current-Shunt | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1: Compute the loop gain $A\beta$.**
$$A\beta = 1000 \times 0.04 = 40$$

**Step 2: Compute the desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta)$.**
$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 40 = 41$$

**Step 3: Compute the closed-loop gain $A_f$.**
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1000}{41} \approx 24.39 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 4: Compute the input resistance with feedback (Series Mixing → multiplied).**
$$R_{if} = R_i \cdot (1+A\beta) = 2 \text{ k}\Omega \times 41 = 82 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute the output resistance with feedback (Voltage Sampling → divided).**
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{40}{41} \approx 0.976 \Omega$$

**Step 6: Final tabulated answer.**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Loop gain $A\beta$ | 40 (dimensionless) |
| Closed-loop gain $A_f$ | $\approx 24.39$ V/V |
| Input resistance $R_{if}$ | 82 k$\Omega$ |
| Output resistance $R_{of}$ | $\approx 0.976$ $\Omega$ |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Choice 2]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the general expression for closed-loop gain $A_f$ of a negative feedback amplifier. Show that the gain is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Current-Shunt Feedback** amplifier has the following parameters: open-loop current gain $A = 500$, input resistance $R_i = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, output resistance $R_o = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$, and feedback factor $\beta = 0.02$. Calculate:
- (i) Closed-loop gain $A_f$
- (ii) Input resistance with feedback $R_{if}$
- (iii) Output resistance with feedback $R_{of}$
- (iv) Comment on the stability of the amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The block diagram of a negative feedback amplifier has a basic amplifier (gain $A$) and a feedback network (gain $\beta$).

**Step 1:** The error signal at the input is:
$$x_i = x_s - x_f$$

**Step 2:** The feedback signal is proportional to the output:
$$x_f = \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 3:** The amplifier output is:
$$x_o = A \cdot x_i$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:
$$x_i = x_s - \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into Step 3:
$$x_o = A \cdot (x_s - \beta \cdot x_o) = A \cdot x_s - A\beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging:
$$x_o + A\beta \cdot x_o = A \cdot x_s$$

**Step 7:** Factoring $x_o$:
$$x_o \cdot (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot x_s$$

**Step 8:** Therefore:
$$A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$$

This shows the closed-loop gain is reduced by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$ compared to the open-loop gain. This reduction is the **price** paid for the benefits of feedback: improved stability, reduced distortion, wider bandwidth, and controlled input/output impedances.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1: Loop gain.**
$$A\beta = 500 \times 0.02 = 10$$

**Step 2: Desensitivity factor.**
$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 3: Closed-loop gain (current gain $A_f$ in A/A).**
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{500}{11} \approx 45.45 \text{ A/A}$$

**Step 4: Input resistance with feedback (Shunt Mixing → divided).**
$$R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega}{11} \approx 90.91 \Omega$$

**Step 5: Output resistance with feedback (Current Sampling → multiplied).**
$$R_{of} = R_o \cdot (1+A\beta) = 20 \text{ k}\Omega \times 11 = 220 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6: Comment on stability.**

The current-shunt feedback reduces the gain sensitivity by a factor of 11. The amplifier now exhibits:
- **Very low input resistance** ($\approx 90.91 \Omega$): suitable for current-source-driven inputs.
- **Very high output resistance** ($220 \text{ k}\Omega$): approaches an ideal current source.
- The gain has become less sensitive to variations in transistor parameters, temperature, and supply voltage. The reduction in gain is a trade-off, but the amplifier is far more **stable and predictable** in linear operation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Naming Convention Reversal:** Students frequently list the *input mixing* before the *output sampling* (e.g., writing "Series-Voltage" instead of "Voltage-Series"). KTU 2024 strict grading deducts **1 mark per occurrence**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Wrong Sign of Feedback:** When deriving $A_f$, students sometimes write $A_f = A/(1 - A\beta)$ assuming positive feedback. Always verify the **mixing junction sign**: negative feedback uses **subtraction**, yielding $(1+A\beta)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Confusing $A$ Units:** $A$ is *not always dimensionless*. For Voltage-Shunt it is a transresistance (Ohms), and for Current-Series it is a transconductance (Siemens). Writing $A_f$ with wrong units in a numerical answer costs full method marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Applying Wrong Resistance Formula:** Using the *multiplication* formula $(1+A\beta)$ for shunt mixing — or the *division* formula for series mixing — is a classic KTU error. **Memory tip:** "Series Sampling Rises the resistance, Shunt Sampling Shrinks it" — for both input and output sides.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Forgetting $R_s$ and $R_L$ Loading Effects:** In numerical problems, if the source resistance $R_s$ or load resistance $R_L$ is given, the effective $A$ and $\beta$ must include these. The KTU 2024 paper often includes such twist conditions to test deep understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] There are **exactly four** feedback topologies: Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt.
- [x] Naming order: **Sampling first, Mixing second**.
- [x] **Voltage sampling** is implemented by a **shunt (parallel)** connection at the output.
- [x] **Current sampling** is implemented by a **series** connection at the output.
- [x] **Series mixing** uses **KVL** at the input; **Shunt mixing** uses **KCL** at the input.
- [x] Master closed-loop gain equation: $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$.
- [x] Loop gain $A\beta$ is **always dimensionless** — choose the right $A$ and $\beta$ units to ensure this.
- [x] Series Mixing: $R_{if} = R_i \cdot (1+A\beta)$ — *increases* $R_i$.
- [x] Shunt Mixing: $R_{if} = R_i / (1+A\beta)$ — *decreases* $R_i$.
- [x] Voltage Sampling: $R_{of} = R_o / (1+A\beta)$ — *decreases* $R_o$ (approaches ideal voltage source).
- [x] Current Sampling: $R_{of} = R_o \cdot (1+A\beta)$ — *increases* $R_o$ (approaches ideal current source).
- [x] **Voltage-Series** = Non-inverting op-amp; **Voltage-Shunt** = Inverting op-amp / TIA input stage.
- [x] **Current-Series** = Transconductance amplifier (e.g., emitter degeneration in BJT).
- [x] **Current-Shunt** = Transimpedance amplifier (e.g., photodiode TIA).
- [x] The product $(1 + A\beta)$ is called the **desensitivity factor** or **amount of feedback**.
- [x] Negative feedback trades **gain** for **stability, bandwidth, linearity, and controlled impedance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Feedback Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Feedback Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Practical Feedback Circuits — KTU 2024 Scheme

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **practical feedback circuit** is a real, physically realizable amplifier stage in which a controlled portion of the output signal is returned to the input through a passive (or active) sampling-and-mixing network. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, practical feedback amplifiers are classified into **four canonical topologies** based on the **sampling method** at the output (voltage or current sampling) and the **mixing method** at the input (series or shunt mixing):

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback** (also called **Series-Shunt**) → acts as a **Voltage Amplifier**
2. **Current-Series Feedback** (also called **Series-Series**) → acts as a **Current Amplifier**
3. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** (also called **Shunt-Shunt**) → acts as a **Transresistance Amplifier**
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback** (also called **Shunt-Series**) → acts as a **Transconductance Amplifier**

The **feedback factor β** is a dimensionless (or dimensional) gain of the feedback network that samples the output $X_o$ and produces a feedback signal $X_f = \beta X_o$, which is then combined with the source signal $X_s$ to form the error (difference) signal $X_i = X_s \pm X_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module explicitly tests the student's ability to identify a given practical BJT/Op-Amp feedback circuit, classify it into one of the four topologies, and compute the **closed-loop gain**, **input resistance**, and **output resistance** with feedback.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of feedback like the **thermostat controlling a room's temperature**:

- The **thermostat (feedback network)** continuously *samples* the room's actual temperature.
- It *mixes* this sensed value with the desired temperature (set by you).
- Any *error* (difference) drives the heater to either heat more or less.

> In electronics: the **β network** samples the output, mixes it with the input, and any **error signal** $X_i$ drives the amplifier to correct the deviation — yielding highly predictable, stable performance.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Closed-Loop Gain**: $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$
- **Loop Gain**: $T = A\beta$
- **Amount of Feedback** (in dB): $20\log_{10}\vert 1+A\beta \vert$
- **Desensitivity Factor**: $1+A\beta$
- **Standard β Ranges**: $\beta \approx 0.01$ to $0.1$ in discrete BJT feedback stages

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition of $1+A\beta$:** When $A\beta \gg 1$ (called *deep feedback*), the closed-loop gain $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — the amplifier forgets its own imperfections and behaves only as the **β network** dictates, which is the *whole purpose* of feedback.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Topologies — Structural Logic

Each topology is uniquely identified by **how the output is sampled** and **how the input is mixed**:

| # | Topology | Sampling | Mixing | Idealized Source | Idealized Load | Closed-Loop Gain Unit |
|---|----------|----------|--------|------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Voltage-Series | Voltage (Shunt at o/p) | Series | Thévenin (Voltage) | — | Dimensionless $A_v$ |
| 2 | Current-Series | Current (Series at o/p) | Series | Thévenin (Voltage) | Norton | Dimensionless $A_i$ |
| 3 | Voltage-Shunt | Voltage (Shunt at o/p) | Shunt | Norton (Current) | — | Resistance $R_M$ (Ω) |
| 4 | Current-Shunt | Current (Series at o/p) | Shunt | Norton (Current) | Norton | Conductance $G_M$ (S) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Rule:** If the output is **shorted to ground** and the feedback signal **vanishes** → it is **voltage sampling**. If an output terminal is **opened** and the feedback signal **vanishes** → it is **current sampling**.

> **Mixing Rule:** If the feedback signal **sits in series** with the source in a KVL loop → **series mixing**. If it **sits in parallel** with the source in a KCL node → **shunt mixing**.

## 2.2 The General Closed-Loop Analysis Equations

For **any** of the four topologies, the standard signal-flow relations are:

$$X_s = X_i + X_f \quad \text{(KVL/KCL mixing equation)}$$

$$X_f = \beta X_o \quad \text{(feedback network equation)}$$

$$X_o = A X_i \quad \text{(basic amplifier equation)}$$

Combining these algebraically:

$$A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$$

The term $1+A\beta$ is called the **desensitivity factor** or **return difference**. It is the *single most important quantity* in KTU feedback problems.

## 2.3 Input and Output Resistance with Feedback

The presence of feedback **modifies** both the input and output resistances of the amplifier. The transformation depends on the topology:

| Topology | Input Resistance $R_{if}$ | Output Resistance $R_{of}$ |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Voltage-Series | $R_{if} = R_i (1+A\beta)$ | $R_{of} = R_o / (1+A\beta)$ |
| Current-Series | $R_{if} = R_i (1+A\beta)$ | $R_{of} = R_o (1+A\beta)$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | $R_{if} = R_i / (1+A\beta)$ | $R_{of} = R_o / (1+A\beta)$ |
| Current-Shunt | $R_{if} = R_i / (1+A\beta)$ | $R_{of} = R_o (1+A\beta)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:**
> - **Series mixing** → $R_{if}$ **increases** (by factor $1+A\beta$)
> - **Shunt mixing** → $R_{if}$ **decreases** (by factor $1+A\beta$)
> - **Voltage sampling** → $R_{of}$ **decreases** (better voltage regulator)
> - **Current sampling** → $R_{of}$ **increases** (better current regulator)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Topology | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | All topologies | Same as $A$ |
| 2 | Loop gain | $T = A\beta$ | All topologies | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Desensitivity | $D = 1+A\beta$ | All topologies | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Gain sensitivity | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}\cdot\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | All topologies | — |
| 5 | Upper cutoff (BW↑) | $f_{Hf} = f_H (1+A\beta)$ | Single-pole $A$ | Hz |
| 6 | Lower cutoff (BW↓) | $f_{Lf} = \dfrac{f_L}{1+A\beta}$ | Single-pole $A$ | Hz |
| 7 | Series-mixed $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ | Voltage/Current-Series | Ω |
| 8 | Shunt-mixed $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | Voltage/Current-Shunt | Ω |
| 9 | Voltage-sampled $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | Voltage-Series/Shunt | Ω |
| 10 | Current-sampled $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ | Current-Series/Shunt | Ω |
| 11 | Feedback in dB | $20\log_{10}\vert 1+A\beta \vert$ | All | dB |
| 12 | Noise reduction factor | $1/(1+A\beta)$ for input-referred | All | Dimensionless |

> **Note:** In the table above, the absolute value bars are written as `\vert` for safe markdown rendering. The actual notation in KTU answer sheets is $\vert 1+A\beta \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Practical feedback circuits form the **backbone of every commercial amplifier IC**:

- **Op-Amps (µA741, LM358, TL081):** Internally, the op-amp is a **cascade of voltage-series feedback stages** (emitter/collector degenerated differential pairs). The **high open-loop gain** ($10^5$ to $10^6$) combined with negative feedback yields the **virtual short / virtual open** behavior.
- **Audio Power Amplifiers (TDA series):** Use **voltage-shunt feedback** to set stable, low-impedance voltage gain.
- **Current Regulators / Voltage Regulators (LM317, 7805):** Are *literally* feedback circuits — the **voltage-sampling** at the output is compared with a **bandgap reference** (series mixing).
- **Wideband RF Amplifiers:** Use **shunt-shunt feedback** to achieve 50 Ω input/output matching over decades of bandwidth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain Equation

**Step 1 — Define the basic amplifier input-output relationship:**

$$X_o = A \cdot X_i$$

This says: the open-loop amplifier $A$ produces output $X_o$ from its *internal* input $X_i$ (the error signal).

**Step 2 — Define the feedback network behavior:**

$$X_f = \beta \cdot X_o$$

This says: the β-network samples the output and scales it by $\beta$ to produce the feedback signal.

**Step 3 — Define the mixing equation:**

$$X_i = X_s - X_f$$

This says: the error signal is the difference between source and feedback (negative feedback convention). For *positive* feedback, replace $-$ with $+$.

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$X_i = X_s - \beta \cdot X_o$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the new $X_i$ into Step 1:**

$$X_o = A (X_s - \beta X_o)$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$X_o = A \cdot X_s - A \beta \cdot X_o$$

**Step 7 — Bring the $X_o$ terms to the same side:**

$$X_o + A\beta X_o = A X_s$$

**Step 8 — Factor $X_o$ on the left:**

$$X_o (1 + A\beta) = A X_s$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f = X_o / X_s$:**

$$A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deep Feedback Limit ($A\beta \gg 1$):** When $A\beta \gg 1$, we get $A_f \approx 1/\beta$. The closed-loop gain depends **only on the passive feedback network**, not on the (temperature-sensitive, nonlinear) active device.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Input Resistance with Series Mixing

**Step 1 — Open-loop input resistance is $R_i$:** By definition,

$$X_i = \frac{V_s}{R_i + \text{series feedback}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_i = \frac{V_i}{I_i}$$

**Step 2 — With feedback closed, the voltage appearing across $R_i$ is now $X_i = X_s - \beta X_o$:**

$$I_i = \frac{X_i}{R_i} = \frac{X_s - \beta X_o}{R_i}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $X_o = A X_i = A(X_s - \beta X_o)$:** But we want a relation purely in terms of $X_s$ and $I_i$. Substitute $X_i = A(X_s - \beta X_o)$ into $X_o = A X_i$ to get $X_o = A(X_s - \beta X_o)$, then solve for $X_o$ in terms of $X_s$:

$$X_o = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} X_s = A_f X_s$$

**Step 4 — Now plug back to find $I_i$:**

$$I_i = \frac{X_s - \beta A_f X_s}{R_i} = \frac{X_s (1 - \beta A_f)}{R_i}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ and simplify $\beta A_f$:**

$$\beta A_f = \frac{\beta A}{1+A\beta}$$

$$1 - \beta A_f = \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}$$

**Step 6 — Therefore:**

$$I_i = \frac{X_s}{R_i(1+A\beta)}$$

**Step 7 — Effective input resistance with feedback:**

$$R_{if} = \frac{X_s}{I_i} = R_i (1+A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i (1+A\beta) \quad \text{(Series Mixing)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Output Resistance with Voltage Sampling

**Step 1 — Apply a test source $V_t$ at the output with input $X_s = 0$:** The output voltage is now $X_o = V_t$.

**Step 2 — The feedback network produces $X_f = \beta V_t$** which gets mixed to give $X_i = -\beta V_t$ (since $X_s = 0$).

**Step 3 — The amplifier's controlled source produces:**

$$V_{\text{ctrl}} = A X_i = -A\beta V_t$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the output node:** The current supplied by the test source $I_t$ flows through $R_o$ and into the controlled source:

$$I_t = \frac{V_t - V_{\text{ctrl}}}{R_o} = \frac{V_t - (-A\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \frac{V_t (1+A\beta)}{R_o}$$

**Step 5 — Output resistance with feedback:**

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} \quad \text{(Voltage Sampling)}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier

> **Problem:** A CE BJT amplifier with emitter degeneration resistor $R_E$ (un-bypassed) has $A = -200$, $R_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, and $\beta = 0.05$ (the feedback factor for a CE stage with $R_E$ is approximately $\beta \approx R_E / (R_C + R_E)$). Compute the closed-loop gain, input resistance, output resistance, and the amount of feedback in dB.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loop gain:**

$$A\beta = 200 \times 0.05 = 10$$

**Step 2 — Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{-200}{11} \approx -18.18$$

**Step 4 — Input resistance (series mixing):**

$$R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta) = 1000 \times 11 = 11\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Output resistance (voltage sampling):**

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{10000}{11} \approx 909.09\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Feedback in dB:**

$$20\log_{10}(1+A\beta) = 20\log_{10}(11) = 20 \times 1.0414 = 20.83\text{ dB}$$

**Step 7 — Gain stability check:** If $A$ drifts by ±20% (from $-200$ to $-240$ or $-160$):

$$A_{f,\text{new}} = \frac{-240}{1+240\times0.05} = \frac{-240}{13} \approx -18.46$$

$$\text{Drift in } A_f = \frac{-18.46 - (-18.18)}{-18.18} \times 100\% \approx 1.5\%$$

Thus a **20% drift in $A$** results in only **1.5% drift in $A_f$** — confirming the **desensitization** property of feedback.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Feedback Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sX[Source Xs] --> mixSum[Summing Junction]
    fbX[Feedback Xf] --> mixSum
    mixSum --> eiX[Error Xi]
    eiX --> ampA[Basic Amplifier A]
    ampA --> outX[Output Xo]
    outX --> loadR[Load RL]
    outX --> fbNet[Feedback Network beta]
    fbNet --> fbX
```

> **Interpretation:** Source signal enters, gets compared with feedback, error drives the basic amplifier, output is sampled by β-network, β-network returns scaled feedback to the input — a **closed loop**.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of the Four Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startAll[Feedback Amplifier] --> qSample{How is output sampled?}
    qSample -- Voltage Shunt --> vSamp
    qSample -- Current Series --> cSamp
    vSamp --> qMix1{How is input mixed?}
    qMix1 -- Series --> top1[Voltage Series VCVS]
    qMix1 -- Shunt --> top3[Voltage Shunt VCCS]
    cSamp --> qMix2{How is input mixed?}
    qMix2 -- Series --> top2[Current Series VCVS alt]
    qMix2 -- Shunt --> top4[Current Shunt CCCS alt]
    top1 --> desc1[Increases Ri Decreases Ro]
    top2 --> desc2[Increases Ri Increases Ro]
    top3 --> desc3[Decreases Ri Decreases Ro]
    top4 --> desc4[Decreases Ri Increases Ro]
```

> **Reading the chart:** The **mixing** decision determines $R_{if}$; the **sampling** decision determines $R_{of}$.

## 4.3 Signal Flow Graph of the Feedback Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1((Xs)) -- 1 --> b1((Sum))
    a2((Xf)) -- 1 --> b1
    b1 -- 1 --> c1((Xi))
    c1 -- A --> d1((Xo))
    d1 -- beta --> a2
    d1 -- 1 --> e1((Output))
```

> This is a **Mason's-rule graph**: forward path gain $= A$, loop gain $= A\beta$, return difference $= 1+A\beta$, hence $A_f = A / (1+A\beta)$.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: CE Amplifier with Emitter Degeneration (Voltage-Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vSrc[Vin] --> vIn[Ri base input]
    vIn --> ceStage[CE BJT A stage]
    ceStage --> vOutNode[Output node]
    vOutNode --> rLoad[RL]
    vOutNode --> fbRe[RE un bypassed]
    fbRe --> vIn
    fbRe -. feedback path .-> vFb[Xf = beta Vout]
```

> The un-bypassed $R_E$ creates **series mixing** at the input (feedback voltage in the emitter loop) and **voltage sampling** at the output — a textbook **voltage-series** topology.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Current-Series Feedback (Common-Emitter with Collector-to-Base Feedback Resistor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    iSrc[Is Norton source] --> iInNode[KCL node]
    iFbNode[If feedback] --> iInNode
    iInNode --> ceAmp[CE Transistor A]
    ceAmp --> iOutNode[Output collector node]
    iOutNode --> rLoad[RL]
    iOutNode --> senseR[Rsense in series with load]
    senseR --> fbNet[Feedback beta network]
    fbNet --> iFbNode
```

> **Identification tip:** The presence of a **sense resistor** in series with the load is the *visual hallmark* of **current sampling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four basic feedback topologies in amplifiers. How are they classified based on sampling and mixing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four basic feedback topologies in amplifiers are:

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback** — output is sampled by voltage (shunt connection at output) and input is mixed by series (KVL).
2. **Current-Series Feedback** — output is sampled by current (series element at output) and input is mixed by series.
3. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** — output is sampled by voltage and input is mixed by shunt (KCL).
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback** — output is sampled by current and input is mixed by shunt.

The classification is based on:
- **Sampling method at output:** voltage sampling (output node voltage) or current sampling (output branch current).
- **Mixing method at input:** series mixing (feedback signal appears in series with source) or shunt mixing (feedback signal appears in parallel with source).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the term "loop gain" in a feedback amplifier. State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The **loop gain** $T = A\beta$ is defined as the product of the open-loop gain $A$ of the basic amplifier and the feedback factor $\beta$ of the feedback network. It represents the gain experienced by a signal traversing the entire feedback loop exactly once.

**Significance:**
- It determines the **amount of feedback** applied: feedback in dB $= 20\log_{10}\vert 1+A\beta \vert$.
- It quantifies the **desensitization** of the closed-loop gain to variations in $A$.
- It governs the **bandwidth extension** and the **modification of input/output impedances**.
- When $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep feedback), the closed-loop gain becomes independent of $A$, depending only on $\beta$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier in terms of open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A voltage-series feedback amplifier has the following parameters: $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.02$, $R_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 20\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop gain $A_f$, input resistance $R_{if}$, output resistance $R_{of}$, and the amount of feedback in dB.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

Starting with the three governing equations of a negative feedback amplifier:

$$X_o = A \cdot X_i \quad \text{(Amplifier)}$$

$$X_f = \beta \cdot X_o \quad \text{(Feedback network)}$$

$$X_i = X_s - X_f \quad \text{(Mixing junction, KVL/KCL)}$$

Substituting $X_f$ from equation 2 into equation 3:

$$X_i = X_s - \beta X_o$$

Substituting this $X_i$ into equation 1:

$$X_o = A (X_s - \beta X_o)$$

Expanding and rearranging:

$$X_o + A\beta X_o = A X_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_o(1+A\beta) = A X_s$$

Therefore, the closed-loop gain is:

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}}$$

This derivation is fully **topology-agnostic** — it holds for voltage, current, transresistance, and transconductance amplifiers, with $A$ and $\beta$ carrying appropriate units.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:** $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.02$, $R_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 20\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute the loop gain:**

$$A\beta = 1000 \times 0.02 = 20$$

**Step 2 — Compute the desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 20 = 21$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1000}{21} \approx 47.62$$

**Step 4 — Input resistance (series mixing):**

$$R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta) = 2 \times 21 = 42\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Output resistance (voltage sampling):**

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{20}{21} \approx 0.952\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Feedback in dB:**

$$20\log_{10}(1+A\beta) = 20\log_{10}(21) = 20 \times 1.3222 \approx 26.44\text{ dB}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $A_f$ | $\approx 47.62$ |
| $R_{if}$ | $42\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_{of}$ | $\approx 952\text{ }\Omega$ |
| Feedback | $\approx 26.44\text{ dB}$ |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the four basic feedback topologies in amplifiers.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) For a current-shunt feedback amplifier, the open-loop parameters are $A = 5000\text{ S}$, $\beta = 0.005\text{ }\Omega$, $R_i = 500\text{ }\Omega$, $R_o = 40\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop gain, input resistance, output resistance, and bandwidth if the open-loop upper cutoff frequency is $f_H = 20\text{ kHz}$.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**The Four Topologies:**

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt):** The output is sampled by a **shunt (parallel) connection** at the output node, and the feedback signal is **mixed in series** with the source. The closed-loop gain $A_f$ is dimensionless. It **increases $R_{if}$** and **decreases $R_{of}$**, making it ideal for voltage amplification from a low-impedance source to a high-impedance load.

2. **Current-Series Feedback (Series-Series):** The output is sampled by a **series element** (sense resistor) in the output branch, and feedback is **mixed in series** at the input. $A_f$ is dimensionless. It **increases both $R_{if}$ and $R_{of}$** — ideal for current-mode amplification.

3. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt):** The output is sampled by a **shunt connection** and feedback is **mixed in shunt (KCL node)** at the input. $A_f$ has units of **resistance (Ω)** — the amplifier is a **transresistance** amplifier. It **decreases both $R_{if}$ and $R_{of}$** — ideal for current-input voltage-output signal processing (e.g., photodiode amplifiers).

4. **Current-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Series):** The output is sampled by a **series element** and feedback is **mixed in shunt**. $A_f$ has units of **conductance (S)** — the amplifier is a **transconductance** amplifier. It **decreases $R_{if}$** and **increases $R_{of}$** — ideal for voltage-input current-output stages (e.g., OTA circuits).

**Block Diagrams** (already provided in SECTION 4 of these notes).

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:** $A = 5000\text{ S}$, $\beta = 0.005\text{ }\Omega$, $R_i = 500\text{ }\Omega$, $R_o = 40\text{ k}\Omega$, $f_H = 20\text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Loop gain (dimensionless, despite units of $A$ and $\beta$):**

$$A\beta = 5000 \times 0.005 = 25 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

> The loop gain of a current-shunt amplifier is dimensionless because $A$ has units of Siemens (A/V) and $\beta$ has units of ohms (V/A) — they cancel.

**Step 2 — Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 25 = 26$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop transconductance gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{5000}{26} \approx 192.31\text{ S}$$

**Step 4 — Input resistance (shunt mixing — decreases):**

$$R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta} = \frac{500}{26} \approx 19.23\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Output resistance (current sampling — increases):**

$$R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta) = 40 \times 26 = 1040\text{ k}\Omega = 1.04\text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Bandwidth extension:**

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1+A\beta) = 20 \times 26 = 520\text{ kHz}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $A_f$ | $\approx 192.31\text{ S}$ |
| $R_{if}$ | $\approx 19.23\text{ }\Omega$ |
| $R_{of}$ | $1040\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $f_{Hf}$ | $520\text{ kHz}$ |

> **Gain-Bandwidth Product is conserved:** $A \times f_H = 5000 \times 20\text{ kHz} = 10^8\text{ S·Hz}$, and $A_f \times f_{Hf} = 192.31 \times 520\text{ kHz} \approx 10^8\text{ S·Hz}$ — **same**, confirming the Bode theorem.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in transresistance / transconductance amplifiers:** Students often write $A_f$ as a dimensionless number for current-shunt / voltage-shunt feedback. Always carry the units (Ω or S) in the final answer.
> 2. **Mixing vs. Sampling swapped in $R_{if}$ / $R_{of}$ formulas:** A frequent error is applying $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ to a shunt-mixed stage. Memorize: **Series → Multiply, Shunt → Divide**.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign in $X_i = X_s - X_f$:** The desensitivity factor $1+A\beta$ assumes **negative** feedback. Using $+A\beta$ reverses all conclusions.
> 4. **Not specifying the topology in numerical answers:** KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** if a numerical answer does not mention the topology it pertains to (e.g., "for series mixing...").
> 5. **Open-loop gain $A$ vs. closed-loop $A_f$:** When the problem says "with feedback" or "closed-loop," always use $A_f$, not $A$. The phrasing must be parsed carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **four topologies** are: Voltage-Series, Current-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Shunt.
- ✅ **Sampling Rule:** Short the output to test voltage sampling; open the output branch to test current sampling.
- ✅ **Mixing Rule:** If the feedback signal is in the same KVL loop as the source → **series**; if in the same KCL node → **shunt**.
- ✅ **Closed-loop gain** = $\dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ for *all* topologies; $\beta$ may carry units (Ω or S) for shunt stages.
- ✅ **Desensitivity factor** $1+A\beta$ controls gain stabilization, bandwidth extension, and impedance transformation.
- ✅ **Series mixing** → $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ (boosted); **Shunt mixing** → $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ (lowered).
- ✅ **Voltage sampling** → $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ (lowered); **Current sampling** → $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ (boosted).
- ✅ **Bandwidth** $f_{Hf} = f_H (1+A\beta)$; lower cutoff $f_{Lf} = f_L / (1+A\beta)$.
- ✅ **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is invariant under feedback (Bode's theorem).
- ✅ **Voltage-Series** ≈ Emitter/Source degeneration in BJTs/FETs.
- ✅ **Voltage-Shunt** ≈ Op-Amp with **T-network feedback** or transimpedance amplifier.
- ✅ **Current-Series** ≈ CB stage with feedback from collector or **cascode** configurations.
- ✅ **Current-Shunt** ≈ Op-Amp current booster or **OTA** stage.
- ✅ **Deep feedback** ($A\beta \gg 1$) ⇒ $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — the *design target* of every op-amp circuit.
- ✅ **Noise figure** improves for signals injected *after* the feedback network, degrades for signals *before* it.
- ✅ **Stability:** As $A\beta$ grows, the phase margin shrinks — the trade-off for excessive feedback is oscillation (Nyquist/Bode stability criterion).
- ✅ Always carry **units** in the final numerical answer — a 14-mark KTU question expects them.

> **End of Notes — Practical Feedback Circuits (Module 2, PBEET304, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oscillators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Oscillator** is an electronic circuit that generates a periodic, continuously varying output signal (sine wave, square wave, triangular wave, etc.) **without any external input signal**, deriving its energy solely from a **DC power supply**. It converts DC energy into AC energy at a specific frequency determined by the circuit components.

In KTU 2024 terminology, an oscillator is a **positive feedback amplifier** that satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** to produce sustained sinusoidal oscillations.

### Barkhausen Criterion

For sustained oscillations, the loop gain of a feedback system must satisfy two simultaneous conditions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion for Sustained Oscillations**
> 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be **unity**.
>    $$\vert A\beta\vert = 1$$
> 2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **0° or an integer multiple of 360°**.
>    $$\angle A\beta = 0^{\circ} \;\text{or}\; 2n\pi \text{ radians}, \; n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

Where:
- $A$ = Voltage gain of the amplifier (without feedback)
- $\beta$ = Feedback factor (transfer function of feedback network)

> [!NOTE]
> - For **oscillations to BUILD UP** from noise: $\vert A\beta\vert \geq 1$
> - For **sustained oscillations** (steady state): $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$
> - The condition $\vert A\beta\vert < 1$ causes **damped oscillations** that die out.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Push the Swing" Intuition

Imagine a child on a **swing** in a playground.

- **Without anyone pushing**, the swing slows down and eventually stops due to friction — this is a **damped system**, just like a passive LC circuit losing energy to resistance.
- **To keep the swing going forever**, someone (the "amplifier" or "energy source") must push the swing at **exactly the right moment** (correct **phase**) and with **just enough energy** to compensate for friction losses (correct **magnitude**).
- The swing has a **natural frequency** (its period of oscillation), and the person pushing must match it.
- If the push is too early or too late (wrong phase), the swing slows down.
- If the push is too weak (gain too low) or too strong (gain too high, leading to growing amplitude), the system fails to maintain a steady state.

**The oscillator is essentially the swing + the person pushing it in perfect synchronization.**

| Swing Concept | Oscillator Equivalent |
|---------------|----------------------|
| Swing's natural period | Frequency of oscillation $f_o$ |
| Friction (energy loss) | Resistance in tank circuit |
| Pusher's energy | DC supply via amplifying device |
| Timing of push (right moment) | Phase condition (0° or 360°) |
| Strength of push | Magnitude condition $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ |

---

## 1.3 Oscillator vs. Amplifier — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Amplifier | Oscillator |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| External input | **Required** | **Not required** |
| Purpose | Boosts an input signal | Generates a signal |
| Feedback | Negative feedback (typically) | **Positive feedback** |
| Frequency | Determined by input signal | Determined by **passive components** |
| Loop gain condition | $\vert A\beta\vert < 1$ (stability) | $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ (sustained) |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Sinusoidal Oscillators (KTU Syllabus)

KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically emphasizes the following types:

1. **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** — low frequency (audio range, **1 Hz to 100 kHz**)
2. **Wien Bridge Oscillator** — audio frequency range (**1 Hz to 1 MHz**)
3. **LC Oscillators:**
   - **Hartley Oscillator** — RF range (**100 kHz to 10 MHz**)
   - **Colpitts Oscillator** — RF range
4. **Crystal Oscillator** — high frequency, high stability (**1 kHz to 100 MHz+**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb in KTU examinations:**
> - Use **RC oscillators** for frequencies below **~1 MHz**.
> - Use **LC oscillators** for frequencies from **~1 MHz to 500 MHz**.
> - Use **Crystal oscillators** when **frequency stability** is the priority.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Block Diagram of a Sinusoidal Oscillator

An oscillator consists of two main blocks:
1. **Amplifier Stage** (active device — BJT/FET/op-amp) providing gain $A$ and introducing 180° phase shift (in CE configuration) or 0° (in op-amp inverting/non-inverting).
2. **Frequency-Determining Network (FDN)** — a passive RC or LC network that introduces the required 180° phase shift (for CE BJT) at the desired frequency of oscillation $f_o$.

The amplifier's 180° + network's 180° = total 360° (0°) → satisfies phase condition.

---

## 2.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Theoretical Breakdown

### Working Principle
- Uses a **BJT in CE configuration** (introduces 180° phase shift).
- The feedback network is a **ladder of 3 RC sections**, each providing 60° phase shift.
- Total phase shift in feedback path = **180°** → combined with amplifier's 180° = 360°.

### Logic Steps
1. **Why 3 sections?** A single RC network can produce a maximum phase shift of 90° (asymptotically, never reached). To get 180° with a practical network, **at least 3 RC sections** are cascaded. Each section contributes 60° at the oscillation frequency.
2. **Feedback factor** $\beta$ is calculated using mesh/nodal analysis of the ladder network.
3. The **Barkhausen criterion** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ is solved to obtain $f_o$.
4. The **magnitude condition** $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ gives the **minimum gain** required to sustain oscillations.

### Key Features
- Generates low-frequency sine waves (audio range).
- Uses a common-emitter BJT amplifier (180° inversion).
- The feedback network is a **C-R-C ladder** (or R-C-R).

---

## 2.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Theoretical Breakdown

### Working Principle
- Uses an **op-amp in non-inverting configuration** (0° phase shift, so amplifier contributes 0°).
- The feedback network must therefore contribute **0°** at $f_o$ (not 180°).
- The Wien bridge network consists of a **series RC** ($R_1, C_1$) arm and a **parallel RC** ($R_2, C_2$) arm.

### Logic Steps
1. Compute the feedback factor $\beta = \dfrac{Z_{\text{parallel}}}{Z_{\text{series}} + Z_{\text{parallel}}}$.
2. For zero phase shift, the imaginary part of $\beta$ must be zero → $\omega RC = 1/(\omega RC)$.
3. This yields the frequency of oscillation.
4. At this frequency, $\beta = 1/3$ → amplifier must have gain $\geq 3$ to compensate.

### Key Features
- Most popular **audio-frequency oscillator** (commercial function generators use this).
- Excellent **frequency stability**.
- Can be tuned over a wide range by using a **ganged variable capacitor** (or dual potentiometer) so that $R_1 = R_2$ and $C_1 = C_2$.

---

## 2.4 LC Oscillators — General Principle

LC oscillators use a **tank circuit** (parallel LC) as the frequency-determining network.
- The tank circuit has a natural resonant frequency $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{\text{eq}} C_{\text{eq}}}}$.
- At resonance, the tank circuit acts as a **pure resistive load** to the amplifier.

### 2.4.1 Hartley Oscillator
- Uses a **tapped inductor** ($L_1$ and $L_2$ in series, with mutual inductance $M$).
- Feedback is taken from the **junction of the two inductors** (or via a coupling capacitor).
- Frequency is set by the total inductance $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ and the single capacitor $C$.

### 2.4.2 Colpitts Oscillator
- Uses a **capacitive voltage divider** ($C_1$ and $C_2$ in series).
- Feedback is taken from the **junction of the two capacitors**.
- Frequency is set by the single inductor $L$ and the series equivalent capacitance $C_T = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.

---

## 2.5 Crystal Oscillator — Theoretical Breakdown

A **piezoelectric crystal** (commonly quartz) exhibits the **piezoelectric effect**:
- When an AC voltage is applied, the crystal **vibrates mechanically** at a precise natural frequency.
- Conversely, mechanical stress produces an electrical charge.

### Equivalent Circuit of a Quartz Crystal
- $L$ = large (represents crystal mass / inertia)
- $C$ = small (representing crystal stiffness)
- $R$ = small (representing friction / damping)
- $C_p$ (or $C_s$ in some notations) = shunt/holder capacitance, typically a few pF

### Two Resonant Frequencies
1. **Series Resonant Frequency** $f_s$: when $L$ resonates with $C$ (series LC). $X_L = X_C$.
2. **Parallel Resonant Frequency** $f_p$: when $L$ resonates with the series combination of $C$ and $C_p$ ($C$ and $C_p$ in series).

> [!NOTE]
> $f_s < f_p$. The crystal operates in the narrow band between $f_s$ and $f_p$, giving **extremely high Q** (Q in the order of **10,000 to 1,000,000**).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below lists **all exam-critical formulas**. Memorize these for KTU ESE.

| Oscillator Type | Frequency of Oscillation $f_o$ | Minimum Gain $\vert A\vert_{\min}$ | Frequency Range | Phase Shift Provided |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Barkhausen Criterion** | $\angle A\beta = 0^{\circ} \;\text{or}\; 360^{\circ}$ | $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ | Universal | Universal |
| **RC Phase Shift (BJT, 3-section ladder)** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\vert A\vert \geq 29$ | **1 Hz to 100 kHz** | 180° (network) + 180° (CE amp) = 360° |
| **Wien Bridge (op-amp)** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $\vert A\vert \geq 3$ | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** | 0° (network) + 0° (non-inv. op-amp) = 0° |
| **Hartley Oscillator** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ where $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | $\vert A\vert \geq \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ (approximate) | **100 kHz to 10 MHz** | 180° (LC tank) + 180° (CE amp) = 360° |
| **Colpitts Oscillator** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_T}}$ where $C_T = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | $\vert A\vert \geq \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ (approximate) | **1 MHz to 500 MHz** | 180° (LC tank) + 180° (CE amp) = 360° |
| **Crystal — Series Resonance** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | — | **1 kHz to 100 MHz+** | Series resonance: $X = 0$ (crystal looks like $R$) |
| **Crystal — Parallel Resonance** | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_p^{\text{eq}}}}$ where $C_p^{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}$ | — | (just above $f_s$) | Parallel resonance: $X = \infty$ (crystal looks open) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Wien Bridge**: Used in **audio function generators** (e.g., HP/Agilent 33120A) and **audio test equipment** due to clean, low-distortion sine wave output.
> - **RC Phase Shift**: Used in **tone generators**, **electronic music synthesizers**, and **instrumentation**.
> - **Hartley**: Used in **AM radio receivers** (local oscillator) and **RF signal generators**.
> - **Colpitts**: Used in **FM transmitters**, **RF power amplifiers**, and **VHF/UHF oscillators** (more stable than Hartley).
> - **Crystal**: Used in **microcontroller clock circuits**, **real-time clocks (RTC)**, **RF transmitters**, **digital watches**, and **quartz frequency standards** — anywhere a **precise, stable reference frequency** is required.

---

## 2.7 Why We Need a Starting Mechanism

Even though sustained oscillations need $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$, the **build-up** needs $\vert A\beta\vert > 1$ initially. This is because:
- Oscillations start from **thermal noise** at the device input (always present at room temperature).
- The slightly higher gain ensures this noise is **amplified**, growing the amplitude.
- As amplitude grows, **non-linearities** in the active device (saturation/cutoff in BJT, slew rate in op-amp) **reduce the effective gain** until $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ at steady state.
- This is called **amplitude stabilization**.

> [!NOTE]
> In op-amp Wien bridge oscillators, two **back-to-back Zener diodes** (or a thermistor in the negative feedback path) are used to **limit the amplitude** and stabilize it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive derivations** with every algebraic step shown. No shortcuts, no skipped steps — just like a KTU model answer sheet.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation of RC Phase Shift Oscillator (BJT)

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup

Consider a BJT in CE configuration with a **3-section RC ladder** as the feedback network. Each section is identical with $R$ and $C$. Let the amplifier have input resistance $R_i$ (treated as large; ideal approximation $R_i \to \infty$).

**The feedback ladder** has resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and capacitors $C_1, C_2, C_3$. For an **equal-component ladder**, $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.

Let $V_o$ be the output voltage, $V_f$ be the feedback voltage at the base, and $\beta = V_f / V_o$.

### 3.1.2 Mesh Analysis

Define the node voltages at the junctions of the ladder as $V_1, V_2$ and $V_f$ (with $V_f$ applied to the base of the BJT).

**At Node 1 (junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$):**
$$(V_1 - V_o) j\omega C + \frac{V_1 - V_f}{R} + (V_1 - V_f) j\omega C = 0$$

Wait — let me reconsider. The standard configuration uses $C_1, C_2, C_3$ **in series** and $R_1, R_2, R_3$ **shunting each node to ground** (or vice versa). Following the standard textbook configuration (Millman/Jaeger):

Let me adopt the standard **C-R-C ladder** form with output $V_o$ and feedback node $V_f$:

Let the load seen by the ladder at the output end be $R_c$ (the collector resistor) plus the amplifier input impedance — we use the simplified form.

### 3.1.3 Standard Formulation (Simplified)

Using the standard result for an **unloaded** 3-section RC ladder:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}$$

where $\alpha = \omega RC$.

### 3.1.4 Applying the Barkhausen Phase Condition

For oscillations, the **imaginary part of the denominator must be zero** (so that $\beta$ is purely real):

$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0$$

$$\alpha(\alpha^2 - 6) = 0$$

Since $\alpha \neq 0$ (else $f_o = 0$):

$$\alpha^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

Substituting back $\alpha = \omega RC$:

$$\omega_o RC = \sqrt{6}$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC}$$

### 3.1.5 Frequency of Oscillation

$$f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2\pi RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$

### 3.1.6 Applying the Magnitude Condition (Minimum Gain)

With the imaginary part zero, $\beta$ becomes purely real:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\sqrt{6})^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 5(6)} = \frac{1}{1 - 30} = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

So $\vert\beta\vert = 1/29$.

For the loop gain condition $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$:

$$\vert A\vert \cdot \frac{1}{29} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\vert A\vert_{\min} = 29}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** of $\beta$ confirms the 180° phase shift introduced by the ladder (the CE amplifier adds another 180°, totaling 360°).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation of Wien Bridge Oscillator

### 3.2.1 Circuit Setup

The Wien bridge network consists of:
- **Series arm**: $R_1$ in series with $C_1$. Impedance:
$$Z_1 = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}$$
- **Parallel arm**: $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$. Impedance:
$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}} = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$

For symmetric Wien network: $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

So:
$$Z_1 = R - \frac{j}{\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### 3.2.2 Feedback Factor

The feedback network forms a **voltage divider**:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC) \cdot \dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + j\omega RC}$$

Let me simplify step by step. Write:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}, \quad Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

$$Z_1 + Z_2 = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Common denominator $= j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$Z_1 + Z_2 = \frac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC \cdot R}{j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)} = \frac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega R^2 C}{j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)}$$

Now:

$$\beta = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega R^2 C}{j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)}}$$

$$= \frac{R \cdot j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)}{(1 + j\omega RC)\left[(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega R^2 C\right]}$$

$$= \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega R^2 C}$$

Expanding $(1 + j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 = 1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2j\omega RC$:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2j\omega RC + j\omega R^2 C}$$

Wait, this is incorrect. Let me redo:

The term $j\omega R^2 C$ in the denominator doesn't match dimensions. Let me re-check.

Going back: $Z_1 + Z_2 = \dfrac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + (j\omega RC)^2}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}$ — that's also wrong.

Let me be more careful:

$Z_1 \cdot j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC) = (1 + j\omega RC)^2$

$Z_2 \cdot j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC) = R \cdot j\omega C = j\omega RC$

So:

$$Z_1 + Z_2 = \frac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}$$

Now expand $(1 + j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2$:

$$Z_1 + Z_2 = \frac{1 + 2j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)} = \frac{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(3\omega RC)}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(3\omega RC)}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}} = \frac{R \cdot j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}{(1 + j\omega RC)\left[(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(3\omega RC)\right]}$$

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(3\omega RC)}$$

### 3.2.3 Applying the Phase Condition

For zero phase shift, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) = 0$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_o RC = 1$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

### 3.2.4 Frequency of Oscillation

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### 3.2.5 Applying the Magnitude Condition

At $\omega = \omega_o = 1/RC$, the denominator becomes purely imaginary: $j(3\omega RC) = j(3 \cdot 1) = j3$.

So:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega_o RC}{j(3\omega_o RC)} = \frac{j \cdot 1}{j \cdot 3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore $\vert\beta\vert = 1/3$, and for $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$:

$$\vert A\vert \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\vert A\vert_{\min} = 3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a non-inverting op-amp, $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$, where $R_f$ is the feedback resistor and $R_1$ is the resistor to ground. For $\vert A\vert = 3$, we need $R_f = 2 R_1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation of Hartley Oscillator

### 3.3.1 Circuit Setup

A BJT in CE configuration. The tank circuit consists of $L_1$ and $L_2$ in series (with mutual inductance $M$) and capacitor $C$ in parallel. The feedback is taken from the junction of $L_1$ and $L_2$ back to the base.

### 3.3.2 Tank Circuit Analysis

The total inductance of the tapped inductor:

$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$

The parallel tank circuit's resonant frequency (ignoring the small resistance of the inductor):

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$$

### 3.3.3 Condition for Sustained Oscillation

The feedback factor for Hartley (from the inductive divider):

$$\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$$

The CE amplifier inverts, so the loop gain condition:

$$\vert A\beta\vert \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A\vert \geq \frac{L_1 + M}{L_2 + M}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If mutual inductance $M$ is **negligible** (no coupling between $L_1$ and $L_2$):
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2) C}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation of Colpitts Oscillator

### 3.4.1 Circuit Setup

A BJT in CE configuration. The tank circuit consists of a single inductor $L$ in parallel with two series capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$. The feedback is taken from the junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$ back to the base.

### 3.4.2 Tank Circuit Analysis

The two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in **series** give the equivalent tank capacitance:

$$C_T = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

The resonant frequency of the parallel LC tank:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_T}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L \cdot C_1 \cdot C_2}}}$$

### 3.4.3 Condition for Sustained Oscillation

The feedback factor for Colpitts (from the capacitive divider):

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$

For sustained oscillation:

$$\vert A\beta\vert \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A\vert \geq \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $C_1 < C_2$ (typically), $\vert A\vert > 1$ is needed. This is easily achieved by a CE BJT amplifier with high intrinsic gain.

---

## 3.5 Crystal Oscillator — Equivalent Circuit and Resonance Frequencies

### 3.5.1 Equivalent Circuit

The electrical equivalent of a quartz crystal is:
- A series RLC branch: $R$, $L$, $C$ (representing the mechanical resonance).
- A shunt capacitance $C_p$ (the "holder" or "mounting" capacitance), typically 2–5 pF.

### 3.5.2 Series Resonant Frequency

The series branch $L$-$C$ resonates when the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel:

$$X_L = X_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_s L = \frac{1}{\omega_s C}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

$$\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

At $f_s$, the series branch impedance = $R$ (minimum, very small), so the crystal behaves like a **small resistor**.

### 3.5.3 Parallel Resonant Frequency

At frequencies slightly above $f_s$, the series branch becomes **inductive**. The crystal as a whole (series branch + shunt $C_p$) forms a parallel resonant circuit when:

$$\omega_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC_p^{\text{eq}}}}$$

where $C_p^{\text{eq}}$ is the series combination of $C$ and $C_p$:

$$C_p^{\text{eq}} = \frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}$$

$$\boxed{f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}}}}$$

At $f_p$, the crystal impedance is **maximum (very large)**, so the crystal behaves like an **open circuit**.

### 3.5.4 Crystal — Final Resonance Relation

Since $C_p \gg C$ typically, we have $C_p^{\text{eq}} \approx C$, so $f_p \approx f_s$, but **$f_p > f_s$** (always).

$$\boxed{f_s < f_p, \quad f_p \approx f_s \left(1 + \frac{C}{2C_p}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The crystal is operated **between** $f_s$ and $f_p$, where it acts as a **highly stable inductor** (in the equivalent reactance plot). This narrow band is the key to its **exceptional frequency stability**.

### 3.5.5 Crystal Quality Factor

The Q-factor of a quartz crystal is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

Typical values: $Q = 10{,}000$ to $1{,}000{,}000$ — orders of magnitude higher than LC tank circuits ($Q \sim 100$).

---

## 3.6 Comparison: RC vs. LC vs. Crystal Oscillators (Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift | Wien Bridge | LC (Hartley/Colpitts) | Crystal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | 1 Hz – 1 MHz | 100 kHz – 500 MHz | 1 kHz – 100 MHz+ |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Q-Factor** | < 1 | < 1 | ~ 100 | 10,000 – 1,000,000 |
| **Tuning** | Difficult (all R's or all C's must change together) | Easy (ganged R or C) | Moderate | Not tunable (fixed) |
| **Amplifier Used** | BJT (CE) | Op-amp (non-inv.) | BJT/FET (CE) | BJT/FET/Op-amp |
| **Output Sine Purity** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Application** | Tone generators | Audio frequency standards | RF signal sources | Clocks, communication |
| **Approximate Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium–High |

        RS --> C1[CAPACITOR C<br/>CRYSTAL STIFFNESS]

## 4.7 Reactance vs. Frequency Characteristic of Quartz Crystal

```mermaid
graph LR
    faxis[Frequency axis] --> f1[fs: Series Resonance]
    f1 --> f2[fo: Operating Frequency]
    f2 --> f3[fp: Parallel Resonance]
    faxis --> react1[Reactance]
    react1 --> r1[fs: X equals 0<br/>CRYSTAL ACTS LIKE R]
    react1 --> r2[between fs and fp: INDUCTIVE REGION]
    react1 --> r3[above fp: CAPACITIVE REGION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart:** The crystal's reactance is **zero** at $f_s$ (looks like a small resistance), **inductive** in the narrow band between $f_s$ and $f_p$ (used for stable oscillation), and **capacitive** above $f_p$. The crystal oscillator is always designed to operate **between** $f_s$ and $f_p$.

---

## 4.8 Crystal Operating Modes — Series vs. Parallel

```mermaid
graph TB
    startX[CRYSTAL APPLICATION] --> seriesX[SERIES MODE<br/>Operate at fs]
    startX --> parallelX[PARALLEL MODE<br/>Operate between fs and fp]
    seriesX --> useSer[Used in Pierce<br/>low impedance load]
    parallelX --> usePar[Used in Miller<br/>Pierce Oscillator]
```
