<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Why does a phase shift oscillator require three RC sections instead of two?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations states that:

1. The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$
2. The total phase shift around the loop must be zero or an integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians: $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

A common-emitter BJT amplifier inherently introduces a **180°** phase shift between its input and output. To satisfy the loop phase condition, the feedback network must provide an additional **180°**. A single RC section can yield a maximum of 90° of phase shift, and two sections can at best approach 180° only at infinite frequency (impractical). Three identical RC sections, each contributing a stable **60°**, provide exactly **180°** with finite, realizable component values. Thus three sections are the minimum required for a practical phase shift oscillator.

**[Total: 3 marks]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Write the expression for the frequency of oscillation of an RC phase shift oscillator and the minimum voltage gain required. Mention the role of each RC section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$$

**Minimum Voltage Gain:** $\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$

**Role of RC Sections:** The three identical RC sections together form the feedback network. Each section is a high-pass (lead) network that contributes approximately **60°** of phase lead at the oscillation frequency. The cumulative phase shift of **180°** from the three sections, combined with the **180°** from the inverting CE amplifier, yields a total loop phase of **360° (≡ 0°)**, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition. The attenuation of the ladder at $f_o$ is $|\beta| = 1/29$, requiring the amplifier to compensate with a minimum gain of 29.

**[Total: 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT in CE configuration.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
The feedback network consists of three identical RC sections cascaded between the amplifier output and the input. Each section has series capacitance $C$ and shunt resistance $R$. Let the node voltages at the three junctions be $V_1, V_2$ and $V_3 = V_o$.

**Step 2: KCL at the Three Nodes**
Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law and introducing the dimensionless parameter $\alpha = \omega RC$, the three nodal equations reduce to a linear system. Solving this system using Cramer's rule or successive substitution gives the feedback factor:

$$\beta = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}$$

**Step 3: Applying the Phase Condition**
For sustained oscillation, the network must contribute exactly 180° of phase shift. Hence the imaginary part of the denominator must equal zero:

$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha(\alpha^2 - 6) = 0$$

The non-trivial root is:

$$\alpha^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_o RC = \sqrt{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_o = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC}$$

**Step 4: Frequency of Oscillation**

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \approx \frac{0.065}{RC}}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude of $\beta$ at $f_o$**
Substituting $\alpha^2 = 6$ in the real part:

$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = -29$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{29} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

**Step 6: Minimum Loop Gain**
From $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$\boxed{\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29}$$

**[7 marks: 1 for circuit description, 2 for $\beta$ derivation, 2 for frequency, 2 for minimum gain]**

---

#### (b) **Design an RC phase shift oscillator for $f_o = 2$ kHz using a BJT. Choose $C = 4.7$ nF. Calculate R, minimum $h_{fe}$, and verify Barkhausen criterion.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Solve for R**
From the frequency formula:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.449}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{(6.2832 \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.449)}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.4456 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.92 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Standard value: $R \approx 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ (or use 6.91 kΩ).

**Step 2: Minimum $h_{fe}$**
For BJT CE amplifier with bypassed emitter:

$$h_{fe,min} = 29$$

Choose $h_{fe} = 35$ for safe startup margin.

**Step 3: Verify Magnitude Condition**
- $|A_v| \cdot |\beta| = 29 \times \frac{1}{29} = 1$ ✓

**Step 4: Verify Phase Condition**
- Amplifier phase = $180°$ (CE inverts)
- RC ladder phase = $180°$ (3 × 60°)
- Total loop phase = $180° + 180° = 360° \equiv 0°$ ✓

**Both Barkhausen conditions are satisfied. The design is valid.**

**[7 marks: 2 for R, 1 for $h_{fe}$, 2 for magnitude verification, 2 for phase verification]**

---

### **Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) **With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an RC phase shift oscillator. Compare it with the Wien Bridge oscillator.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of RC Phase Shift Oscillator:**

```
   ┌──────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────────────┐
   │  CE Amplifier    │      │  RC Ladder (3 sections)    │
   │  Gain: |A| ≥ 29  │─────▶│  Phase: 180° @ f_o         │
   │  Phase: 180°     │      │  |β| = 1/29 @ f_o          │
   └────────▲─────────┘      └──────────────┬─────────────┘
            │                               │
            │           Feedback            │
            └───────────────────────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The CE BJT amplifier provides voltage gain $|A_v|$ and a phase inversion of **180°**.
2. The output is applied to a three-section RC ladder network. At a particular frequency $f_o$, each RC section contributes a phase lead of **60°**, totaling **180°** for the network.
3. The attenuated, phase-shifted output of the ladder is fed back to the amplifier input. The total loop phase is $180° + 180° = 360°$, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.
4. The ladder attenuates the signal by a factor of 29 at $f_o$. The amplifier compensates with a gain of 29, making the loop gain unity. Sustained oscillations result.

**Comparison with Wien Bridge Oscillator:**

| Parameter | Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback network | 3 RC sections (ladder) | 1 series RC + 1 parallel RC |
| Phase shift per network | 180° | 0° (positive feedback) |
| Amplifier type | Inverting (BJT CE / FET CS) | Non-inverting (Op-amp) |
| Frequency formula | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Minimum gain | 29 | 3 |
| Output purity | Moderate THD (~3–5%) | Low THD (<1%) |
| Frequency tuning | Difficult (change 3 R or 3 C together) | Easy (ganged R or C) |
| Frequency range | 1 Hz – 1 MHz | 1 Hz – 1 MHz |
| Stability | Moderate | High (with AGC) |

**[7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for working, 2 for comparison]**

---

#### (b) **For an RC phase shift oscillator with $R = 12$ kΩ and $C = 0.01$ μF, calculate the frequency of oscillation, the minimum loop gain, and the feedback factor. Verify that the Barkhausen criterion is satisfied when the amplifier provides a gain of 30.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $R = 12 \text{ k}\Omega = 12 \times 10^3 \,\Omega$
- $C = 0.01 \,\mu F = 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \,F = 10^{-8} \,F$
- Amplifier gain $|A_v| = 30$

**Step 1: Calculate $f_o$**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 12 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.449}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{1.846 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{f_o \approx 541.6 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 2: Feedback Factor**

$$|\beta| = \frac{1}{29} \approx 0.0345$$

**Step 3: Minimum Loop Gain**

$$|A_v|_{min} = 29$$

**Step 4: Verify Magnitude Condition**

$$|A_v \beta| = 30 \times \frac{1}{29} = 1.034$$

Since $|A_v \beta| = 1.034 > 1$, the magnitude condition is **satisfied** (gain is slightly above unity, ensuring startup and automatic stabilization).

**Step 5: Verify Phase Condition**
- Amplifier phase: $180°$ (CE inverting)
- RC ladder phase: $180°$ (three 60° sections)
- Total: $180° + 180° = 360° \equiv 0°$ ✓

**Barkhausen Criterion is satisfied. Oscillations will be sustained at approximately 541.6 Hz.**

**Additional Note:** The fact that $|A_v \beta| = 1.034 > 1$ means the oscillator will start up with growing amplitude. As the amplitude increases, the BJT enters non-linear regions (saturation/cutoff), which effectively reduces the gain back to 29, stabilizing the amplitude.

**[7 marks: 2 for $f_o$, 1 for $\beta$, 1 for minimum gain, 3 for verification]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor:** Writing $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ — this is the Wien Bridge formula and **will be marked wrong** (-3 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up gain directions:** Stating the minimum gain as 3 (Wien Bridge value) instead of 29 — common confusion, **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the Barkhausen verification:** Just writing the formula is not enough. KTU examiners **expect explicit verification** of both phase and magnitude conditions (-2 to -3 marks if missing).
> 4. **Ignoring the assumption $R_1 = R_2 = R_3$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3$:** If the question does not specify identical components, you must state this assumption clearly, otherwise the formula does not apply.
> 5. **Sign error in phase:** The total loop phase is $360°$, **not** $180°$. Writing "180°" is a common mistake that loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Units not converted:** Failing to convert kΩ to Ω and µF/nF to F in numerical problems — automatic 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist** — Tick off each point before entering the exam hall!

- [x] **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$
- [x] **Frequency Formula:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ (memorize the $\sqrt{6}$!)
- [x] **Minimum Gain:** $\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$
- [x] **Feedback Factor:** $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ at $f_o$
- [x] **Phase Distribution:** 180° (CE amp) + 180° (RC ladder, 3×60°) = 360° ≡ 0°
- [x] **Why 3 sections?** Each RC section gives at most 60° practically; 3×60° = 180° is the minimum required.
- [x] **BJT condition:** $h_{fe} \geq 29$ (choose 30–35 for safe startup)
- [x] **FET condition:** $g_m R_D \geq 29$
- [x] **Design Rule:** When designing, calculate $R$ from $f_o$ and chosen $C$ (or vice versa) using the formula.
- [x] **Numerical values:** $\sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$, $2\pi\sqrt{6} \approx 15.39$
- [x] **Frequency range:** Practical use from **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio to low RF).
- [x] **Output waveform:** Pure sine wave (when $|A_v|$ is exactly 29); distorted if gain is much larger.
- [x] **Comparison with Wien Bridge:** Phase shift needs inverting amp; Wien Bridge needs non-inverting amp. Phase shift has minimum gain 29; Wien Bridge has minimum gain 3.
- [x] **Where used:** Audio oscillators, function generators, tone generators, low-frequency instrumentation.
- [x] **Limitation:** Frequency cannot be easily varied (requires changing 3 resistors or 3 capacitors simultaneously).

---

## 5.4 Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Aspect | Phase Shift Oscillator |
|---|---|
| **Type** | RC Sinusoidal Oscillator |
| **Active device** | BJT (CE) or FET (CS) |
| **Feedback network** | 3-stage RC ladder |
| **$f_o$ formula** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ |
| **$\vert \beta \vert$ at $f_o$** | $1/29$ |
| **Min $\vert A_v \vert$** | $29$ |
| **Loop phase @ $f_o$** | $360°$ |
| **Typical use** | Audio frequency generation |
| **KTU weightage** | 14 marks (full Module 2 question) |
| **Key derivation** | Solving 3-node KCL → imaginary part of $\beta$ = 0 → $\alpha = \sqrt{6}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "Barkhausen first, formula second, numbers last." Always begin any oscillator problem by stating the **Barkhausen Criterion** explicitly, then derive or apply the frequency formula, then verify both magnitude and phase conditions numerically. This sequence fetches **full marks** consistently in KTU valuation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wien Bridge Oscillator (WBO)** is a *low-frequency sine wave generator* (typically **1 Hz to 1 MHz**) that uses an **RC (Resistance-Capacitance) frequency-selective feedback network** arranged in the form of a classical **Wheatstone bridge**. The active element is an **op-amp in a non-inverting configuration** that provides the requisite gain of exactly **A = 3** to satisfy the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained, stable oscillations.

The frequency-determining element is the **Wien network** — a series RC combination ($Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$) feeding into a parallel RC combination ($Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C}$). It is the **workhorse of audio-frequency signal generation** in laboratory function generators, audio test equipment, and instrumentation tone generators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tuned Radio Resonator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of it like a perfectly tuned guitar string:**
> Imagine plucking a guitar string — it vibrates at one dominant natural frequency determined by its tension and mass. The **Wien Bridge Oscillator is the electronic equivalent of this acoustic resonator**. The "tension" is set by the resistor value, and the "mass" is set by the capacitor value. The op-amp acts like the musician's hand continuously feeding back just the right amount of energy to keep the string vibrating *forever* without fading out (damping) or distorting (over-driving). The feedback network "listens" to the output and only amplifies a *single chosen frequency* while rejecting all others — this is **frequency selectivity**.

## 1.3 Classification & Salient Features

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Type** | RC-feedback sine wave oscillator |
| **Frequency Range** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** (audio + low RF) |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Required Gain (A)** | Exactly **3** (ideally) |
| **Phase Shift at $f_0$** | **0°** (network) + **0°** (non-inverting amp) = **0°** total |
| **Feedback Factor $\beta$ at $f_0$** | $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$ |
| **Stability Network** | Typically uses **thermistor** or **lamp (FET/Zener)** for amplitude stabilization |
| **Distortion** | Low (< 1% with stabilization) |
| **Applications** | Audio oscillators, function generators, test benches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Wien Bridge Oscillator is a *guaranteed high-weightage topic* in Module 2 of Analog Electronics. Students must memorize the frequency formula, derive the feedback factor, and explain the role of amplitude stabilization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Barkhausen Criterion — The "Birth Certificate" of Every Oscillator

For any sinusoidal oscillator to produce **sustained, self-sustaining oscillations**, two simultaneous mathematical conditions (collectively called the **Barkhausen Criterion**) must be satisfied at the loop level:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the *loop gain* $|A\beta|$ must equal exactly **1** (unity). This ensures the signal neither decays (|Aβ| < 1) nor grows unbounded into distortion (|Aβ| > 1).
> 2. **Phase Condition:** The *total phase shift* around the feedback loop must equal an integer multiple of **360°** (or 0°). This ensures **positive (regenerative) feedback**.

Mathematically stated:

$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \text{(Magnitude Condition)}$$

$$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ or } 2n\pi \quad \text{(Phase Condition)}$$

where $A$ is the amplifier's voltage gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network's transfer function.

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Wien Bridge Oscillator — Component-by-Component

The circuit consists of two functional blocks connected in a *closed regenerative loop*:

### Block A — The Frequency-Selective Wien Network (Feedback Element $\beta$)

- **Series Arm ($Z_1$):** Resistor $R$ in **series** with Capacitor $C$
$$Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$
- **Parallel Arm ($Z_2$):** Resistor $R$ in **parallel** with Capacitor $C$
$$Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Block B — The Non-Inverting Op-Amp Amplifier (Active Element $A$)

- Provides a stable gain of $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- Phase shift of **0°** (non-inverting topology)
- For oscillation, the gain *must* be set to **3**, which means $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 2$, so typically $R_f = 2R_1$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Step Logic of Wien Bridge Operation:**
> 1. At power-on, random thermal noise (containing all frequencies) appears at the op-amp input.
> 2. The op-amp amplifies this noise with a gain of **A**.
> 3. The Wien network samples the output and feeds back *only* the frequency component for which $\beta$ is maximum (i.e., $f = f_0$).
> 4. At $f_0$, the phase through the network is **0°** and $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$. The op-amp is also 0° phase shift, satisfying Barkhausen.
> 5. The amplitude stabilizes when the *effective* loop gain settles to **exactly 1** — achieved practically using a nonlinear amplitude stabilization element (thermistor/incandescent lamp/FET).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet for Wien Bridge Oscillator**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Frequency of oscillation** (the golden equation — memorize it) |
| 2 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | Angular frequency |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Feedback factor (voltage divider rule) |
| 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$ | Frequency-dependent feedback factor |
| 5 | $\beta_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{3}$ at $\omega = \omega_0$ | Maximum feedback at resonance |
| 6 | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain |
| 7 | $A = 3$ | Required gain for oscillation |
| 8 | $R_f = 2 R_1$ | Resistor ratio for $A=3$ |
| 9 | $\|A\beta\| = 1$ | Barkhausen magnitude condition |
| 10 | $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ | Barkhausen phase condition |
| 11 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of the Wien network |

> **Note on notation:** In the formulas above, vertical bars (e.g., in $\vert A\beta \vert$) are deliberately written using LaTeX `\vert` to avoid any table-breaking issues.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Wien Bridge is found inside virtually every **laboratory function/arbitrary waveform generator** (Keysight, Tektronix, Rigol). It is also the heart of **audio test tone generators** (e.g., 1 kHz reference tone), **burglar alarm sirens**, and **VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator)** front-ends in synthesizers. Engineers use it because the **RC components are cheap, stable, and the output is a low-distortion pure sine wave** — qualities that LC oscillators cannot match at audio frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frequency of Oscillation ($f_0$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that the frequency at which the Wien network's feedback factor is maximum (and phase = 0°) is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.

**Step 1:** Write the impedances of the two arms of the Wien network.

$$Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 2:** Write the impedance of the parallel arm $Z_2$.

$$Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the voltage-divider rule to get the feedback factor $\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o}$ where $V_f$ is the voltage fed back to the non-inverting input.

$$\beta = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify the expression.

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$ to clear the inner fraction.

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC \cdot R}{j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + R \cdot j\omega C}$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $j\omega C$ from the denominator.

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{j\omega C \left[ (1 + j\omega RC)^2 / j\omega C \cdot (1/j\omega C) \text{...} \right]} \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** After careful algebraic manipulation (multiplying out and grouping real/imaginary parts), the transfer function reduces to a beautifully symmetric form with real part 3 and imaginary part $\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$.

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}}$$

**Step 7:** Identify two critical sub-conditions:

- **Magnitude of $\beta$:** $\quad |\beta| = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)^2}}$
- **Phase of $\beta$:** $\quad \phi = -\tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}}{3}\right]$

**Step 8:** $\beta$ is **maximum** (and purely real) when the imaginary part of the denominator is zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

**Step 9:** Solve for the angular frequency $\omega_0$.

$$(\omega_0 RC)^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 10:** Convert angular frequency to standard frequency in Hz.

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

At this frequency, $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$ and the phase shift is exactly **0°**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Amplifier Gain ($A$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that for sustained oscillations, the op-amp gain must be exactly 3.

**Step 1:** Apply the Barkhausen magnitude condition at $f = f_0$:

$$|A \beta| = 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the maximum value of $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$:

$$A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $A$:

$$\boxed{A = 3}$$

**Step 4:** For a non-inverting op-amp configuration, the gain is:

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2$$

Therefore, the feedback resistor must be exactly **twice** the input resistor: $R_f = 2 R_1$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Phase Response of the Wien Network

The phase angle of the feedback network as a function of frequency is:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}}{3}\right]$$

**Phase Behavior Table:**

| Frequency Condition | Phase $\phi$ | Nature of $\beta$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega \to 0$ | $+90°$ | $\beta$ acts like a differentiator (low frequency) |
| $\omega = \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$ | $\mathbf{0°}$ | $\beta$ is purely real & maximum |
| $\omega \to \infty$ | $-90°$ | $\beta$ acts like an integrator (high frequency) |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Why Non-Inverting Topology?

| Topology | Gain | Phase Shift | Suitable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Amplifier | $-R_f/R_1$ | 180° | ❌ No (phase ≠ 0°) |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | $1 + R_f/R_1$ | 0° | ✅ **Yes — chosen for WBO** |
| Voltage Follower | 1 | 0° | ❌ No (gain too low) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If the amplifier contributed 180° phase shift (inverting), the total loop phase would be $180° + 0° = 180°$, which violates the Barkhausen phase condition. The non-inverting op-amp is **mandatory**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem (KTU-style):** *A Wien Bridge Oscillator uses $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation and the required feedback resistor $R_f$ if $R_1 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the time constant $\tau$.

$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3) \times (0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the frequency formula.

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the gain requirement $A = 3$.

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{5\text{k}} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}, \quad R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply Rail] --> B[Op-Amp in Non-Inverting Configuration]
    B --> C[Output Node Vout - Sine Wave]
    C --> D[Wien Network Series Arm R plus C]
    D --> E[Wien Network Parallel Arm R parallel C]
    E --> F[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    F --> B
    C --> G[Amplitude Stabilizer Thermistor or Lamp]
    G --> H[Gain Control Resistor Rf]
    H --> B
    C --> I[Load and Frequency Output Terminal]
```

## 4.2 Complete Wien Bridge Oscillator Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerRails [DC Power Supply]
        Vplus[Positive Rail plus Vcc]
        Vminus[Negative Rail minus Vcc]
    end
    subgraph OpAmpStage [Non Inverting Op Amp Active Stage]
        Op1[Op Amp Triangle Symbol]
        InPos[Non Inverting Input plus]
        InNeg[Inverting Input minus]
    end
    subgraph WienNetwork [Frequency Selective Wien Network]
        Rs[R Series Resistor]
        Cs[C Series Capacitor]
        Rp[R Parallel Resistor]
        Cp[C Parallel Capacitor]
    end
    subgraph GainControl [Feedback Resistor Divider]
        Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf]
        R1[Ground Resistor R1]
    end
    subgraph Stabilizer [Amplitude Stabilization Network]
        Lamp[Incandescent Lamp or Thermistor]
    end
    Vplus --> Op1
    Vminus --> Op1
    Op1 --> Output[Sine Wave Output Vout at frequency f0]
    Output --> Rs
    Rs --> Cs
    Cs --> NodeMid[Network Junction Node]
    NodeMid --> Rp
    Rp --> Cp
    Cp --> GroundNode[Ground]
    NodeMid --> FeedbackLine[Feedback Line to plus Input]
    FeedbackLine --> InPos
    Output --> Lamp
    Lamp --> Rf
    Rf --> InNeg
    R1 --> InNeg
    R1 --> GroundNode
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow & Barkhausen Loop Visualization

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Noise as Thermal Noise Source
    participant Amp as Non Inverting Op Amp Gain equals 3
    participant FB as Wien Feedback Network Beta equals one third
    participant Out as Output Vout
    Noise->>Amp: Random noise containing all frequencies
    Amp->>Out: Amplified by factor 3
    Out->>FB: Vout sampled and fed back
    FB->>Amp: Only f0 component passes with zero phase shift
    Amp->>Out: Continuous regeneration
    Note over FB,Amp: Barkhausen condition satisfied at f0
    Note over Out: Pure sine wave output at f0 equals one over two pi RC
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LowFreq [Low Frequency Region]
        LF[Phase plus 90 deg, Beta small]
    end
    subgraph ResFreq [Resonant Frequency f0]
        RF[Phase 0 deg, Beta maximum equals 1/3]
    end
    subgraph HighFreq [High Frequency Region]
        HF[Phase minus 90 deg, Beta small]
    end
    LowFreq --> ResFreq
    ResFreq --> HighFreq
    RF --> OutputNode[Selected and Amplified Sine Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Wien network behaves like a **band-pass filter** — it allows only a narrow band of frequencies near $f_0$ to pass through with full feedback ($\beta = 1/3$). All other frequencies are heavily attenuated and effectively ignored by the loop, resulting in a *clean* sinusoidal output at $f_0$ only.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a Wien Bridge Oscillator. State the formula for its frequency of oscillation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a type of sine wave oscillator that uses an **RC (Resistance-Capacitance) feedback network** in the form of a balanced bridge circuit, combined with a **non-inverting op-amp amplifier**, to generate low-distortion sinusoidal signals in the audio frequency range (typically 1 Hz to 1 MHz).

The frequency of oscillation is given by:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

where $R$ and $C$ are the matched resistor and capacitor values in the Wien network (series and parallel arms). The op-amp must provide a gain of **A = 3** for sustained oscillations as dictated by the Barkhausen criterion.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Barkhausen Criterion** specifies the two necessary and sufficient conditions for a feedback amplifier circuit to operate as a self-sustaining sinusoidal oscillator:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity.
$$|A \beta| = 1$$
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed feedback loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$).
$$\angle A \beta = 0° \text{ or } 2n\pi \text{ radians}$$

The first condition ensures that the signal neither decays (gain too low) nor grows into saturation/distortion (gain too high). The second condition ensures **regenerative (positive) feedback** so that the output reinforces the input.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Wien Bridge Oscillator, $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = 0.05 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Calculate: (i) the frequency of oscillation, (ii) the required feedback resistor $R_f$ if the input resistor is $R_1 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and (iii) the feedback factor $\beta$ at resonance. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

The Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of two arms — a **series RC arm** ($Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$) and a **parallel RC arm** ($Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C}$) — connected across the output of a **non-inverting op-amp**. Two additional resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$ form the negative feedback path that sets the amplifier gain.

At power-on, thermal noise containing all frequencies enters the op-amp. The op-amp amplifies the noise (gain = $1 + R_f/R_1$). The output is sampled and fed back to the non-inverting input through the Wien network. The Wien network acts as a **frequency-selective band-pass filter**, allowing only the frequency $f_0$ to pass with maximum feedback ($\beta = 1/3$) and zero phase shift. Thus the loop phase becomes $0°$ (op-amp) + $0°$ (Wien) = $0°$, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition. With $A = 3$, the loop gain becomes $A \beta = 1$, satisfying the magnitude condition, and a **sustained sine wave** is generated at $f_0$.

**Derivation (2 marks — see Section 3.1 above):**

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 0.05 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$, $R_1 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Part (i) — Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (10 \times 10^3) \times (0.05 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{10\pi} = \frac{1000}{\pi} \approx 318.31 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 318.3 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Part (ii) — Required $R_f$:**

For oscillation, $A = 3$, so:

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2 R_1 = 2 \times 5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Part (iii) — Feedback factor $\beta$ at resonance:**

At $f = f_0$, the imaginary part of the Wien network transfer function vanishes, leaving:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{1}{3}}$$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a Wien Bridge Oscillator and explain the function of each block. Why is a non-inverting amplifier used? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the feedback factor $\beta$ of the Wien network. Show that the phase shift is zero only at $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Why is amplitude stabilization necessary in practical oscillators? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the architecture in Section 4.2. The circuit contains:
- A **non-inverting op-amp** (powered by ±Vcc)
- A **Wien network** in the positive feedback path (series R-C and parallel R-C)
- A **resistor divider** ($R_f$ and $R_1$) in the negative feedback path
- An **amplitude stabilization element** (thermistor/incandescent lamp)

**Block Functions (3 marks):**
1. **Op-amp:** Provides voltage gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$ and acts as the active element of the oscillator.
2. **Wien Network:** Acts as a frequency-selective band-pass filter that determines the frequency of oscillation and provides the correct phase (0°) at $f_0$.
3. **Resistor Divider ($R_f$, $R_1$):** Sets the closed-loop gain of the amplifier to exactly 3.

**Why Non-Inverting?** (1 mark): A non-inverting amplifier contributes **0° phase shift**, while the Wien network also contributes **0°** at $f_0$. This gives a total loop phase of $0°$, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition. An inverting amplifier would add 180°, violating the criterion.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of $\beta$ (4 marks — see Section 3.1, Steps 1–6):**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Zero Phase Condition (2 marks):**

The phase of $\beta$ is:
$$\phi = -\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}}{3}\right]$$

For $\phi = 0°$, the argument of the $\tan^{-1}$ must be zero:
$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Amplitude Stabilization (1 mark):**

In practice, the loop gain $A\beta$ is kept slightly *greater* than 1 (e.g., 1.01) to ensure oscillations build up from noise. However, this would otherwise cause the output to grow unboundedly and clip into distortion. A **nonlinear element** (thermistor with negative temperature coefficient, or an incandescent lamp whose resistance increases with temperature) automatically reduces the gain back to exactly 3 once the desired amplitude is reached, yielding a stable low-distortion sine wave.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set A = 3:** Many students derive $f_0$ correctly but forget to state that the amplifier gain must be exactly 3 (and consequently $R_f = 2R_1$). The examiner will deduct **at least 2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Phase error:** Students often write that the Wien network produces 180° phase shift — this is WRONG. It produces 0° at $f_0$, not 180°. Memorize: **Wien network = 0° at $f_0$**.
> 3. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** Always convert $C$ to **Farads** and $R$ to **Ohms** before substituting into the formula. A common error is using $C$ in $\mu$F directly, which produces an answer off by a factor of $10^6$.
> 4. **Confusion with RC phase-shift oscillator:** The RC phase-shift oscillator uses *three* RC sections producing 180° total phase shift and requires an inverting amplifier. Do not confuse it with the Wien Bridge.
> 5. **Skipping the stabilization network:** Examiners expect a mention of amplitude stabilization. If absent, expect a **1-mark penalty**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick-Recall Summary:**

- ✅ **Full name:** Wien Bridge Sine Wave Oscillator (WBO).
- ✅ **Type:** RC feedback oscillator using a balanced bridge topology.
- ✅ **Active element:** Op-amp in **non-inverting** configuration.
- ✅ **Frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ *(memorize verbatim)*.
- ✅ **Angular frequency:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$.
- ✅ **Feedback factor at resonance:** $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ (purely real).
- ✅ **Phase of $\beta$ at $f_0$:** $0°$ (this is the *zero-crossing* of the phase response).
- ✅ **Required amplifier gain:** $A = 3$ exactly.
- ✅ **Resistor ratio:** $R_f = 2 R_1$.
- ✅ **Phase behavior at extremes:** $+90°$ as $\omega \to 0$; $-90°$ as $\omega \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Barkhausen conditions:** $|A\beta| = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$.
- ✅ **Amplitude stabilization:** Use a thermistor (NTC) or incandescent lamp in the feedback path to prevent output clipping/distortion.
- ✅ **Frequency range:** **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio + low RF). For higher frequencies, use **LC oscillators** like Colpitts or Hartley.
- ✅ **Frequency-selective element:** The Wien network acts as a **band-pass filter** with $Q \approx \frac{1}{3}$ (low-Q).
- ✅ **Typical distortion:** $< 1\%$ with amplitude stabilization; $< 0.1\%$ with high-quality components.
- ✅ **Comparison with other oscillators:**
   - *Wien Bridge:* Op-amp based, audio frequencies, 0° phase network.
   - *RC Phase Shift:* Op-amp based, 180° phase network (inverting amp).
   - *Colpitts / Hartley:* Transistor-based, RF frequencies, LC tank circuit.
- ✅ **The classic numerical KTU question:** Given $R$ and $C$, find $f_0$. Then given $R_1$, find $R_f$. Then find $\beta$.
- ✅ **Real-world application:** Inside every commercial **function/arbitrary waveform generator** (Keysight, Tektronix, etc.) and **audio test bench** equipment.
- ✅ **Two golden equations to remember:** $\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$ and $\boxed{A = 3}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuned Oscillator Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tuned Oscillator Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuned Oscillator Circuit:** A **tuned (or sinusoidal) oscillator** is an electronic circuit that generates a sustained, periodic, sinusoidal waveform at a specific desired frequency by employing a **frequency-selective resonant network** (typically an **LC tank circuit**, **RC phase-shift network**, or **piezoelectric quartz crystal**) as its load. The circuit is said to be "tuned" because the active device (BJT/FET) is biased at its **operating point (Q-point)** and the network resonates at one specific *natural frequency* $f_0$, filtering out all other harmonics.

The mathematical condition that must be satisfied for sustained oscillation is given by the **Barkhausen Criterion**:

$$|A \beta| = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ \text{)}$$

where $A$ is the forward amplifier gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network transfer function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Tuning Fork in an Orchestra**
> Imagine a tuning fork struck in a quiet room. It vibrates at *one pure, sharp, fixed frequency* (e.g., 440 Hz – the musical note "A"). The surrounding air molecules at other frequencies are simply ignored. A **tuned oscillator** behaves identically. Inside a radio receiver, when you turn the dial, you are *tuning* an LC circuit so that it "listens" to one specific radio station while ignoring all others. The capacitor stores energy as an electric field, the inductor stores it as a magnetic field, and they keep *trading* this energy back and forth, like a swing being pushed at exactly the right moment.

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Piezoelectric Strain Constant (Quartz):** Approximately $\mathbf{2.3 \text{ pC/N}}$
- **Standard Q-point (VCE):** Midpoint of load line, typically $\mathbf{V_{CC}/2}$
- **Quality Factor (Q):** Ratio of stored energy to dissipated energy per cycle; for tuned circuits $\mathbf{Q > 10}$ for stable oscillations.

## 1.4 Classification of Tuned Oscillators (Syllabus Map)

| Family | Sub-Type | Typical Frequency Range | Key Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LC Oscillators** | Hartley | **1 kHz – 30 MHz** | Tapped Inductor |
| **LC Oscillators** | Colpitts | **1 kHz – 500 MHz** | Capacitive Divider |
| **Crystal Oscillators** | Pierce / Miller | **1 MHz – 200 MHz** | Quartz Crystal |
| **RC Oscillators** | Phase-Shift / Wien | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** | Resistor & Capacitor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heart of a Tuned Oscillator: The LC Tank Circuit

The **tank circuit** is a parallel combination of an inductor $L$ and a capacitor $C$. It acts as a **band-pass filter** that resonates at one precise frequency.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Energy Storage Phase (Capacitor):** Initially, the capacitor $C$ is fully charged to a voltage $V_c$. The entire energy is stored in the electric field: $E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$.
2. **Energy Transfer Phase (Discharge through L):** Capacitor discharges through the inductor, current $i_L$ rises. Energy transfers to the magnetic field: $E_L = \frac{1}{2} L i_L^2$.
3. **Magnetic Collapse Phase:** When capacitor voltage reaches zero, the inductor's collapsing magnetic field forces current to keep flowing, recharging the capacitor with *opposite* polarity.
4. **Oscillation Cycle:** This to-and-fro energy exchange produces a sinusoidal waveform. The frequency is governed by the values of $L$ and $C$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** LC tank circuits form the foundation of **RF receivers, AM/FM transmitters, TV tuners, radar systems, and SDR (Software Defined Radio) front-ends**. The ability to "tune" a circuit to a specific frequency allows multiple stations to coexist on different carrier frequencies without mutual interference (a technique called **Frequency Division Multiplexing – FDM**).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU University Exams for Tuned Oscillators. Memorize the resonant frequency expressions and the Barkhausen condition.

| Oscillator Type | Frequency of Oscillation ($f_0$) | Effective Component | Feedback Fraction ($\beta$) | Marking Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **General LC Tank** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $L$ and $C$ in parallel | N/A (generic) | **High** |
| **Hartley Oscillator** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ | $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | $\beta = \dfrac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + L_2 + 2M}$ | **Very High** |
| **Colpitts Oscillator** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_T}}$ | $C_T = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$ | **Very High** |
| **Crystal Oscillator (Series)** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$ | $L_s$, $C_s$ (motional) | Pierce configuration | **High** |
| **RC Phase-Shift** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | 3 cascaded RC sections | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | **Moderate** |
| **Wien Bridge** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Series & parallel RC | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | **Moderate** |
| **Barkhausen (Gain)** | $A_v \ge \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ (typically $A_v \ge 29$) | Amplifier must compensate loss | Magnitude condition | **Critical** |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
- $M$ = Mutual Inductance between $L_1$ and $L_2$ (Hartley)
- All resistances are assumed **ideal** ($Q \to \infty$)
- $f_s < f_p$ (series resonant frequency is always less than parallel resonant frequency in a crystal)

## 2.3 The Two Barkhausen Conditions Explained

**Why is the condition $A\beta = 1$?**
- If $|A\beta| < 1$: Oscillations **decay and die out** (like a swing being pushed too gently).
- If $|A\beta| > 1$: Oscillations **grow without bound** until clipping/distortion occurs.
- If $|A\beta| = 1$: **Steady-state sinusoidal oscillations** are sustained forever (theoretical ideal).

**Why is the phase shift condition $0^\circ$?**
- The amplifier inverts the signal by $180^\circ$ (Common-Emitter configuration).
- The feedback network must therefore introduce an *additional* $180^\circ$ phase shift so the *total* loop phase is $360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ$, which is the **condition for positive feedback**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resonant Frequency of a Parallel LC Tank

**Problem:** Derive the resonant frequency $f_0$ of a lossless parallel LC circuit.

**Given:** A capacitor $C$ in parallel with an inductor $L$, both ideal.

**Step 1 — Express the impedance of each element.**
The capacitor has impedance $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ and the inductor has impedance $Z_L = j\omega L$.

**Step 2 — Write the total admittance.**
The total admittance $Y_{total}$ is the sum of individual admittances:

$$Y_{total} = Y_C + Y_L = j\omega C + \frac{1}{j\omega L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $1/j = -j$.**

$$Y_{total} = j\omega C - \frac{j}{\omega L} = j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the resonance condition.**
At resonance, the circuit behaves as a **pure resistor** (impedance is real), so the imaginary part of $Y_{total}$ must equal zero:

$$\omega_0 C - \frac{1}{\omega_0 L} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega_0$.**

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert angular frequency to Hz.**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

> This is the **fundamental tuned-oscillator equation** that governs Hartley, Colpitts, and Crystal oscillators alike.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency of Hartley Oscillator

**Hartley Oscillator Circuit:** A common-emitter BJT amplifier with a tapped inductor ($L_1$, $L_2$) and a single capacitor $C$ forming the tank. The mutual inductance between the two inductor sections is $M$.

**Step 1 — Identify the total inductance.**
The two coils $L_1$ and $L_2$ are wound on a common core. The total inductance for aiding (additive) coupling is:

$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$

where $M$ is the **mutual inductance** (positive for series-aiding connection, negative for series-opposing).

**Step 2 — Substitute into the generic tank formula.**
The capacitor in the Hartley oscillator is $C$, and the equivalent inductance is $L_T$:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T \, C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \, C}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the feedback fraction $\beta$.**
The Hartley oscillator is an inductive voltage divider. The feedback voltage is taken from $L_2$:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{j\omega(L_2 + M)}{j\omega(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)} = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + L_2 + 2M}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Barkhausen magnitude condition.**
The amplifier must provide gain:

$$A_v \ge \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{L_1 + L_2 + 2M}{L_2 + M}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frequency of Colpitts Oscillator

**Colpitts Oscillator Circuit:** A capacitive voltage divider ($C_1$, $C_2$) is used in place of the inductive divider. The single inductor is $L$.

**Step 1 — Compute the equivalent tank capacitance.**
$C_1$ and $C_2$ are in series as far as the tank is concerned (only the series combination appears across the inductor):

$$C_T = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the generic tank formula.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_T}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L \, C_1 \, C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the feedback fraction $\beta$.**
This is a capacitive voltage divider. Voltage across $C_2$ provides feedback:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Insight:** In a Colpitts oscillator, the feedback fraction $\beta$ depends on the *inverse* ratio of capacitors. To *increase* the feedback, you must *decrease* $C_1$. This is the **exact opposite** of the Hartley oscillator, where increasing $L_2$ increases $\beta$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Crystal Oscillator Equivalent Model & Frequencies

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalent circuit.**
A quartz crystal can be modeled as a series RLC network ($L_s$, $C_s$, $R_s$) shunted by the *electrode capacitance* $C_p$ (also called *holder capacitance*).

**Step 2 — Series resonant frequency.**
When the motional inductance $L_s$ and motional capacitance $C_s$ resonate in series, the impedance is at a **minimum** (theoretically zero):

$$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$$

**Step 3 — Parallel resonant frequency.**
The crystal exhibits parallel resonance when the net reactance is zero considering the shunt $C_p$:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s \left(\dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}\right)}}$$

**Step 4 — Relationship between $f_s$ and $f_p$.**

$$f_p = f_s \sqrt{1 + \frac{C_s}{C_p}}$$

Since $C_p \gg C_s$ in practice, $f_p$ is typically only **0.1% to 1% higher** than $f_s$. This narrow "active region" is what gives crystal oscillators their **exceptional frequency stability**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Colpitts oscillator uses $C_1 = 1 \text{ nF}$, $C_2 = 10 \text{ nF}$, and $L = 100 \text{ \mu H}$. Find the frequency of oscillation and the feedback fraction.

**Solution:**

$$C_T = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-9})(10 \times 10^{-9})}{11 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-18}}{11 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.909 \text{ nF}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.909 \times 10^{-9}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{9.09 \times 10^{-14}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.015 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 527.7 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

$$\text{Required gain: } A_v \ge \frac{1}{\beta} = 11$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Tuned Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDC["DC Power Supply VCC"] --> nodeQ["BJT Active Device biased at Q-point"]
    nodeQ --> nodeAmp["Amplifier Stage A"]
    nodeAmp --> nodeTank["Frequency Selective LC Tank Circuit"]
    nodeTank --> nodeFilt["Resonant Frequency f0 Output"]
    nodeTank --> nodeFB["Feedback Network Beta"]
    nodeFB --> nodeQ
    nodeOut["Sinusoidal Output"] --- nodeTank
    style nodeDC fill:#e1f5ff
    style nodeQ fill:#fff4e1
    style nodeTank fill:#ffe1e1
    style nodeFB fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.2 Colpitts Oscillator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVCC["VCC Supply"] --> nodeBJT["BJT Common Emitter Configuration"]
    nodeBJT --> nodeC1["Capacitor C1 in Tank"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2["Capacitor C2 in Tank"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeL["Inductor L Connected to Ground"]
    nodeL --> nodeGND["Reference Node Ground"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeTap["Tap Point between C1 and C2"]
    nodeTap --> nodeFB["Feedback to Base of BJT"]
    nodeFB --> nodeBJT
    nodeBJT --> nodeOut["Sinusoidal Output across L"]
    style nodeBJT fill:#fff4e1
    style nodeC1 fill:#ffe1e1
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** State and explain the Barkhausen Criterion for sustained oscillations. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Barkhausen Criterion specifies two simultaneous conditions for a feedback circuit to produce sustained oscillations:
1. **Magnitude Condition:** The loop gain magnitude must equal unity, $|A\beta| = 1$, where $A$ is the forward gain and $\beta$ is the feedback fraction.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $360^\circ$ (i.e., $0^\circ$), $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

If $|A\beta| < 1$, oscillations decay; if $|A\beta| > 1$, oscillations grow unbounded; only at exactly $|A\beta| = 1$ are oscillations sustained with constant amplitude.

---

### **Question 2:** What is a tank circuit? Why is it called a "tuned" circuit? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **tank circuit** is a parallel combination of an inductor $L$ and a capacitor $C$ that stores and exchanges energy between the magnetic field of $L$ and the electric field of $C$.

It is called a **"tuned"** circuit because it exhibits **resonance** at a specific natural frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, where the impedance becomes purely resistive and maximum. By varying either $L$ or $C$, the resonant frequency can be "tuned" to a desired value, allowing the circuit to *select* one frequency while rejecting others. This property is exploited in radio receivers, where turning the dial effectively changes the capacitance to tune into different broadcasting stations.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Tuned Oscillator Design and Analysis **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Colpitts Oscillator**. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A Colpitts oscillator is designed with $L = 50 \text{ \mu H}$, $C_1 = 1.5 \text{ nF}$, and $C_2 = 4.5 \text{ nF}$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation, the feedback fraction, and the minimum required amplifier gain for sustained oscillation. Also explain the role of mutual inductance if the inductors are wound on a common core.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Working and Derivation [7 Marks]:**

> The Colpitts oscillator uses a **capacitive voltage divider** in the LC tank. The common-emitter BJT inverts the signal by $180^\circ$. The capacitive divider introduces the *additional* $180^\circ$ phase shift required to satisfy the Barkhausen phase condition.

**Step 1 — Identify effective capacitance.**
The two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ appear in series across the inductor $L$. The effective tank capacitance is:

$$C_T = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LC resonance formula.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_T}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L \, C_1 \, C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Feedback fraction.**
Voltage feedback is taken across $C_1$ (or $C_2$ depending on tap):

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 4 — Sustained oscillation condition (Barkhausen).**
The amplifier gain must satisfy:

$$A_v \ge \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1}$$

**Step 5 — Frequency stability.**
Because $C_1$ and $C_2$ can be made very stable (NPO/COG ceramic), the Colpitts oscillator offers excellent frequency stability, often used in **VHF/UHF transmitters**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute effective capacitance.**

$$C_T = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-9})(4.5 \times 10^{-9})}{(1.5 + 4.5) \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{6.75 \times 10^{-18}}{6.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.125 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency of oscillation.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.125 \times 10^{-9}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{5.625 \times 10^{-14}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.372 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 670.8 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute feedback fraction.**

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{1.5}{6.0} = 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Minimum amplifier gain.**

$$A_v \ge \frac{1}{0.25} = 4$$

**Step 5 — Role of mutual inductance.**
In a Colpitts oscillator, the inductor $L$ is a *single* component — there is no mutual inductance term. The role of mutual inductance is only relevant in the **Hartley oscillator**, where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are wound on a common magnetic core. Any leakage flux linking both coils introduces $M$, which **adds** to the effective inductance if the coils are series-aiding, or **subtracts** if series-opposing. Thus $M$ directly influences $f_0$ in the Hartley configuration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Crystal Oscillators and Frequency Stability **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Internal Choice]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the equivalent circuit of a quartz crystal. Derive the expressions for **series** and **parallel** resonant frequencies. Explain why crystal oscillators offer far superior frequency stability compared to LC oscillators.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the performance of **Hartley, Colpitts, and Crystal** oscillators in terms of frequency range, frequency stability, output waveform purity, and typical applications. Justify the use of a crystal in a digital watch timing circuit.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Crystal Equivalent Circuit and Resonances [7 Marks]:**

> A quartz crystal is a **piezoelectric device** that vibrates mechanically at a precise natural frequency when an alternating electric field is applied. The electrical equivalent is a series RLC ($R_s$, $L_s$, $C_s$) shunted by a parallel capacitance $C_p$ (representing the metal electrodes and holder).

**Series resonance $f_s$** occurs when the motional branch impedance $R_s + j\omega L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s}$ becomes purely resistive at its minimum value. This requires the reactive components to cancel:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$$

**Parallel resonance $f_p$** occurs when the net admittance of the entire circuit becomes purely conductive. Setting total susceptance to zero yields:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s \, C_{eq}}} \quad \text{where} \quad C_{eq} = \frac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}$$

Since $C_p \gg C_s$, it follows that $f_p$ is **slightly higher** than $f_s$, and the gap between them is small.

**Why superior stability?**
- The **Quality Factor (Q)** of a quartz crystal is extraordinarily high (often $\mathbf{10^4 \text{ to } 10^6}$), compared to $Q \approx 50$ to $200$ for a typical LC tank.
- The equivalent inductance $L_s$ is very large (Henries) and the capacitance $C_s$ is very small (femtofarads), making the frequency **extremely insensitive** to external component variations.
- The frequency is governed by the **precise mechanical dimensions of the crystal**, which are physically stable and have a very low **temperature coefficient of expansion**.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table and Justification [7 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Hartley | Colpitts | Crystal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | 1 kHz – 30 MHz | 1 kHz – 500 MHz | 1 MHz – 200 MHz |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor to Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent (ppm level)** |
| **Tuning Method** | Variable L (slug tuning) | Variable C (ganged) | **Fixed** (crystal determines $f_0$) |
| **Waveform Purity** | Good (if loaded lightly) | Better than Hartley | **Best (very low distortion)** |
| **Q-Factor** | 50 – 200 | 50 – 200 | **10,000 – 1,000,000** |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | High | Moderate | **Very Low (TCXO cut)** |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | **Moderate to High** |
| **Applications** | Radio receivers, oscillators | VHF transmitters, signal generators | **Wristwatches, microcontrollers, RF synth** |

**Justification for Digital Watch:**
A digital wristwatch must keep time accurately over years of operation with **minimal power consumption**. A 32.768 kHz quartz crystal is used because:
1. It is **$2^{15}$ Hz**, which when divided down by 15 cascaded flip-flops gives exactly **1 Hz** — perfect for second-by-second counting.
2. The **extremely high Q-factor** ensures the watch drifts by less than 1 second per day (accuracy of $\approx \pm 10 \text{ ppm}$).
3. LC oscillators cannot be used at such low frequencies because $L$ and $C$ values would become impractically large, and they would drift with temperature.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mutual inductance $M$ in Hartley formula** — Many students write $L_T = L_1 + L_2$ only, missing $+2M$. This costs 2 full marks.
> 2. **Writing $C_T = C_1 + C_2$ in Colpitts** — The correct expression is series combination, $C_T = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$. Mixing up series and parallel capacitor combinations is the **#1 error** in KTU answer sheets.
> 3. **Skipping the Barkhausen application** — Even in a "derivation" question, the examiner expects you to end with the condition $A_v \ge 1/\beta$. Don't stop at the frequency expression.
> 4. **Drawing circuit with no Q-point bias** — Always show $V_{CC}$, $R_C$, $R_B$ for the BJT. A naked BJT with no biasing gets zero for the "circuit diagram" portion.
> 5. **Unit confusion in numerics** — Always convert $\mu\text{H}$ to H and nF to F before substituting into $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$. The wrong unit is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✔ **Barkhausen Criterion** is the *gatekeeper* of all sinusoidal oscillators: $|A\beta| = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$.
- ✔ The **LC tank** is the universal frequency-determining element; resonant frequency is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
- ✔ **Hartley = Inductive divider** → $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ and $\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_T}$.
- ✔ **Colpitts = Capacitive divider** → $C_T = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ and $\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$.
- ✔ **Crystal = Two resonances** → $f_s$ (series, low impedance) and $f_p$ (parallel, high impedance), with $f_p > f_s$.
- ✔ **Q-factor of crystal is $10^4$–$10^6$**, making it the most stable practical oscillator.
- ✔ **Hartley suffers from stray capacitance effects** at high frequencies; **Colpitts is preferred for VHF/UHF**.
- ✔ **RC Phase-Shift** oscillators give $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ — used in audio range (1 Hz to 1 MHz).
- ✔ **Wien Bridge** uses both series and parallel RC arms and gives $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — used in function generators.
- ✔ For **sustained oscillations**, amplifier gain must always satisfy $A_v \ge \frac{1}{\beta}$; design typically uses $A_v = 1.02 \times \frac{1}{\beta}$ to guarantee startup.
- ✔ **Crystal cannot be easily tuned** — its frequency is fixed by the physical cut. Variable-frequency oscillators must use LC or RC networks.
- ✔ The **common-emitter BJT** provides the necessary $180^\circ$ phase inversion, requiring the feedback network to provide an additional $180^\circ$ for a total of $360^\circ$ loop phase.
- ✔ **Digital watches use 32.768 kHz crystals** because $32768 = 2^{15}$, enabling a 15-stage binary divider to produce an exact 1 Hz "tick."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystal Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crystal Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Crystal Oscillator** is a highly stable electronic oscillator circuit that uses the mechanical resonance of a vibrating piezoelectric crystal (typically **quartz**, SiO₂) as the frequency-selective feedback element. The resonant frequency is determined by the **physical dimensions and elastic properties** of the quartz crystal, not by external passive components. This yields a **Quality Factor (Q)** on the order of **10,000 to 1,000,000**, vastly superior to LC or RC oscillators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A crystal oscillator is treated as the benchmark reference for **frequency stability** in analog electronic systems. The course requires understanding of the **piezoelectric effect**, the **equivalent circuit model**, and the distinction between **series vs. parallel resonance**.

### 1.2 The Piezoelectric Effect

The **piezoelectric effect** is the foundational physical principle behind every crystal oscillator. It is a two-way electromechanical coupling:

- **Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** A mechanical stress applied to the crystal generates a proportional electrical charge across its faces.
- **Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** An applied alternating voltage causes the crystal to physically deform (vibrate) at the same frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Discovery:** Discovered by **Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880**. Quartz is the most commonly used piezoelectric material due to its excellent temperature stability and very high intrinsic Q-factor.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Tuning Fork Analogy:** Imagine striking a tuning fork. It rings at a *fixed, pure musical note* determined only by its shape and metal — not by how hard you struck it or the air around it. A quartz crystal behaves identically: when energized by an AC signal, it "rings" mechanically at a precise natural frequency. The surrounding electronic circuit simply feeds back and amplifies that natural vibration, sustaining a rock-steady oscillation. The crystal's natural mechanical stiffness is like the fork's metal — set by physics, and almost impossible to perturb.

**Why Quartz?** Quartz exhibits *zero net piezoelectric strain* at **0°C and 93°C** (its inversion points), making its temperature coefficient predictable. Its **Young's Modulus (E ≈ 86.8 GPa)** and low internal friction yield extraordinarily sharp resonance.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Constant |
|---|---|
| Piezoelectric Material | **Quartz (SiO₂)** — most common |
| Density of Quartz (ρ) | **2649 kg/m³** |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **86.8 × 10⁹ N/m²** |
| Intrinsic Q-factor | **10,000 – 1,000,000** |
| Frequency Range (practical) | **1 kHz to 500 MHz** (fundamental) |
| Frequency Stability | **0.001 % to 0.000001 %** (over decades) |
| Dielectric Constant (εr) | **3.78 (along optical axis)**, 4.58 (along X-axis) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of a Crystal

A quartz crystal is cut into a thin disc or rectangular plate, with two **metal electrodes** deposited on opposite parallel faces. When an AC voltage is applied, the crystal vibrates in a particular mechanical mode (thickness-shear, thickness-extensional, flexural, etc.) determined by the **cut angle** (AT, BT, CT, DT, GT, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **AT-cut** is the most widely used for 1–10 MHz oscillators because of its near-zero temperature coefficient at room temperature.

### 2.2 The Equivalent Electrical Circuit

The mechanical vibration of the crystal can be mathematically modeled as an electrical resonant circuit:

**Mechanical RLC Equivalent:**

- **L** (motional inductance) — represents the vibrating mass of the crystal.
- **C** (motional capacitance) — represents the mechanical compliance (inverse stiffness).
- **R** (motional resistance) — represents internal friction and acoustic losses.

**Static Capacitance (C₀):** The actual physical capacitance between the two electrodes (housing + lead capacitance included), which exists in *parallel* with the motional branch.

```
        R       L       C
  o----MMM----UUUU----||----o  (motional branch)
  |                           |
  |          C0               |
  |          ||               |
  o---------------------------o
```

### 2.3 Two Resonant Frequencies

Because the model has both a series and a parallel resonance, the crystal exhibits **two distinct resonant frequencies** very close to one another:

- **Series Resonance (fₛ):** When the motional L and C cancel (Xₗ = Xc), impedance is **purely R (minimum)**.
- **Parallel Resonance (fₚ):** When the inductive reactance of the L-C branch resonates with the static capacitance C₀; impedance is **maximum (purely resistive)**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}}$ | Series resonant frequency (motional L and C) |
| 2 | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C'}} \quad \text{where} \quad C' = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot C}{C_0 + C}$ | Parallel resonant frequency (includes C₀) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{f_p - f_s}{f_s} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot\dfrac{C}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{2p}$ | Frequency separation; $p = C_0/C$ is the **capacitance ratio** |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{\omega_s L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_s R C}$ | Quality factor (sharpness of resonance) |
| 5 | $\Delta f_{pull} = \dfrac{f_s \cdot C}{2(C_0 + C_L)}$ | Frequency pulling due to load capacitance $C_L$ |
| 6 | $X_{eff} = \dfrac{\omega^2 L C - 1}{\omega \left[C_0 + C - \omega^2 L C C_0\right]}$ | Effective reactance of the crystal |
| 7 | $Z_{eff} = R\left[1 + jQ\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s} - \dfrac{\omega_s}{\omega}\right)\right]$ | Effective impedance near series resonance |
| 8 | $\omega_p^2 = \omega_s^2\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{p}\right)$ | Relation between parallel & series angular frequencies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The capacitance ratio $p = C_0 / C$ is typically **100 to 1000** for AT-cut crystals, which means $f_p$ and $f_s$ differ by only **0.05% to 0.5%**. This very narrow "active band" is what gives crystal oscillators their extreme frequency stability.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crystal oscillators are the **heartbeat of the digital world**:

- **Microcontrollers & CPUs** – every clock pin requires a crystal.
- **Telecommunications** – cellular base stations use OCXO (Oven-Controlled XO) for ±1 ppb stability.
- **GPS Satellites** – atomic clocks use quartz oscillators as low-noise reference.
- **Radar & Radio** – for frequency synthesizers and PLLs.
- **Digital watches, USB devices, WiFi modules.**

> [!NOTE]
> Without crystal oscillators, there would be **no digital synchronization** — every wireless protocol, processor, and data link depends on this single invention.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Series Resonant Frequency (fₛ)

**Starting Point:** Consider the motional branch (R, L, C) in series. The total impedance of the motional branch is:

$$Z_m = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

At **series resonance**, the imaginary part of Zₘ vanishes (purely resistive), so the inductive and capacitive reactances are equal in magnitude:

$$\omega_s L = \frac{1}{\omega_s C}$$

Solving for the resonant angular frequency:

$$\omega_s^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Converting angular frequency to ordinary frequency (ω = 2πf):

$$\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** At fₛ, the crystal's mechanical reactance is zero. It behaves like a short circuit — the oscillator can pull it into oscillation with minimum impedance, hence maximum loop gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Parallel Resonant Frequency (fₚ)

**Starting Point:** At parallel resonance, the **total admittance of the entire equivalent network is purely real** (i.e., the susceptance is zero). The full circuit admittance is:

$$Y = j\omega C_0 + \frac{1}{R + j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the motional branch admittance:**

$$Y_m = \frac{1}{R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)}$$

Rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate:

$$Y_m = \frac{R - j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)}{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the total admittance:**

$$Y = j\omega C_0 + \frac{R}{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2} - j\frac{\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)}{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Set the imaginary (susceptive) part to zero:**

$$\omega_p C_0 - \frac{\left(\omega_p L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_p C}\right)}{R^2 + \left(\omega_p L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_p C}\right)^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Neglect R (since R is very small for high-Q crystals, R² ≈ 0):**

$$\omega_p C_0 = \frac{\omega_p L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_p C}}{\left(\omega_p L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_p C}\right)^2}$$

$$\omega_p C_0 = \frac{1}{\omega_p L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_p C}}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-multiply and simplify:**

$$\omega_p^2 C_0 L C - C_0 = 1$$

$$\omega_p^2 L C = 1 + \frac{C}{C_0}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute ωₛ² = 1/(LC) and rearrange:**

$$\frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega_s^2} = 1 + \frac{C}{C_0} = 1 + \frac{1}{p}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_p = \omega_s \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{p}} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_p = f_s \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{p}}}$$

Since $C_0 \gg C$ (i.e., $p \gg 1$), we can use a binomial approximation $\sqrt{1 + x} \approx 1 + x/2$:

$$f_p \approx f_s\left(1 + \frac{1}{2p}\right)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Frequency Separation (Δf = fₚ − fₛ)

Using the approximation above:

$$f_p - f_s = f_s\left[\left(1 + \frac{1}{2p}\right) - 1\right] = \frac{f_s}{2p}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{f_p - f_s}{f_s} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{1}{2p}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** This relationship shows the spacing between the two resonances is governed by the **inverse of the capacitance ratio**. Practical AT-cut crystals have $p \approx 250$, so $f_p - f_s \approx 0.2\%$ of $f_s$. This is the **narrow "active band"** of the crystal.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Quality Factor (Q)

**Definition:** Q is the ratio of stored energy to dissipated energy per cycle, multiplied by 2π:

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{E_{stored}}{E_{dissipated\, per\, cycle}}$$

**For the series RLC motional branch**, the energy stored oscillates between the inductor (½LI²) and the capacitor (½CV²), while the resistor dissipates I²R per cycle.

At series resonance ωₛ, the peak current is $I_{peak} = V_{applied}/R$, and the angular frequency is $\omega_s = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. Substituting into the energy expressions:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_s L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_s R C}$$

All three forms are equivalent. The canonical form used in crystal oscillator analysis is:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\omega_s L}{R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Frequency Pulling Due to Load Capacitance

In **parallel-mode (Pierce/Miller) oscillator** circuits, an external **load capacitor C_L** is added in parallel with the crystal. The effective parallel capacitance becomes:

$$C_{eq} = C_0 + C_L$$

The operating frequency $f_{op}$ shifts slightly above $f_s$:

$$f_{op} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}}\sqrt{1 + \frac{C}{C_0 + C_L}}$$

For $C_L \gg C$, the binomial approximation yields the **pulling figure** (frequency deviation from $f_s$):

$$\Delta f = f_{op} - f_s \approx f_s \cdot \frac{C}{2(C_0 + C_L)}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f_s \cdot C}{2(C_0 + C_L)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students forget that $f_s$ and $f_p$ are *not* design knobs — the crystal manufacturer fixes them. The designer can only fine-tune the operating frequency by **changing C_L**, and only within the narrow active band.

---

### 3.6 Effective Reactance of the Crystal (X_eff)

Combining the motional branch in parallel with C₀, the effective impedance is:

$$Z_{eff} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_0} \parallel \left(R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right)$$

After algebraic manipulation (see KTU Reference: Sedra/Smith 8th Ed., Ch. 13):

$$Z_{eff} = \frac{R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)}{1 - \omega^2 L C_0 + \dfrac{C_0}{C} + j\omega R C_0}$$

**Effective Reactance (Imaginary Part of Z_eff for low R):**

$$\boxed{X_{eff} = \frac{\omega^2 L C - 1}{\omega\left[C_0 + C - \omega^2 L C C_0\right]}}$$

The sign of $X_{eff}$ changes from **capacitive** (at low f) to **inductive** (between $f_s$ and $f_p$) to **capacitive** (at high f). This **inductive region** is where the crystal acts as a stable inductor in oscillator circuits like the **Pierce oscillator**.

---

### 3.7 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Resonance Mode | Impedance | Reactance | Operating Behavior |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| $f < f_s$ | High | Capacitive | Crystal = Capacitor |
| $f = f_s$ | **Minimum (R only)** | Zero (Resistive) | **Series Resonance** |
| $f_s < f < f_p$ | Medium | Inductive | Crystal = Inductor (oscillators operate here) |
| $f = f_p$ | **Maximum (resistive)** | Zero (Resistive) | **Parallel Resonance** |
| $f > f_p$ | High | Capacitive | Crystal = Capacitor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equivalent Circuit Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture of a crystal's equivalent circuit** and its role in the feedback loop of an oscillator.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[AC Input Source] --> IM[Input Metal Electrode]
    IM --> MECH[Quartz Crystal Disc]
    MECH --> OM[Output Metal Electrode]
    OM --> LOUT[To Amplifier Input]

    subgraph MOTIONAL_BRANCH[Mechanical RLC Equivalence]
        RBLK[Motional Resistance R - Internal Friction]
        LBLK[Motional Inductance L - Equivalent Mass]
        CBLK[Motional Capacitance C - Mechanical Compliance]
        RBLK --- LBLK --- CBLK
    end

    subgraph STATIC_BRANCH[Static Holder Capacitance]
        C0BLK[Static Capacitance C0 - Electrode Geometry]
    end

    MOTIONAL_BRANCH --- STATIC_BRANCH

    subgraph RESONANCE_BEHAVIOR[Two Resonant Frequencies]
        FSBLK[Series Resonance fs - Impedance Minimum]
        FPBLK[Parallel Resonance fp - Impedance Maximum]
        FSBLK --> FPBLK
    end

    LBLK -.-> FSBLK
    C0BLK -.-> FPBLK

    subgraph OSC_USAGE[Usage in Oscillator Circuits]
        PIERCE[Parallel Mode - Pierce Oscillator]
        SERIES[Series Mode - Series Resonant Oscillator]
    end

    MOTIONAL_BRANCH --> PIERCE
    MOTIONAL_BRANCH --> SERIES
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The motional branch (R-L-C series combination) models the *vibration* of the crystal. The static branch (C₀) models the *electrode geometry*. The interaction of the two creates the dual resonance behavior exploited in oscillators.

---

### 4.2 Pierce Oscillator Block-Level Architecture

The **Pierce oscillator** is the most common crystal oscillator topology used in KTU 2024 syllabus problems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC[DC Supply VCC] --> RD[Resistor RD - Bias to Base]
    VCC --> RC[Resistor RC - Bias to Collector]
    RD --> AMP[Inverting Amplifier - BJT or Inverter Gate]
    RC --> AMP
    AMP --> OUT[Oscillating Output]
    OUT --> CB[DC Blocking Capacitor CB]
    CB --> XTAL[Crystal X1 - Operates in Inductive Region]
    XTAL --> CG[Capacitor CG - Ground Reference]
    CG --> GND1[Ground]
    AMP --> CA[Capacitor CA - Feedback to Input]
    CA --> AMP
```
