# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*Define Project-Based Learning (PBL) as applicable to the Analog Electronics course PBEET304. List any **two** Module 4 sub-topics that a typical PBL project should integrate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Project-Based Learning (PBL) in Analog Electronics is a pedagogical methodology under the KTU 2024 NEP 2020 framework in which students, working in teams, identify a real-world problem, design and simulate an analog circuit solution, build a hardware prototype, and demonstrate the working system. The project is evaluated as a continuous assessment component (typically 20 marks split across mid-term and final review).
>
> **Two Module 4 sub-topics (1 mark):** Any two from — *Power Amplifiers (Class A/B/AB), Feedback Amplifiers, RC/LC Oscillators (Wien Bridge, RC Phase Shift, Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal), Multivibrators (Astable/Monostable/Bistable), 555 Timer Waveform Generators, IC Voltage Regulators (78xx, LM317, LM723).*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*State the **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained oscillation. For a Wien Bridge oscillator with $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\ \mu$F, calculate the frequency of oscillation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Criterion (2 marks):** The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained oscillation, the loop gain magnitude must equal unity and the loop phase shift must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (i.e., $0°$). Formally, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = n \cdot 360°$, where $A$ is the amplifier gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network transfer function.
>
> **Calculation (1 mark):**
>
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$
>
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.55\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.59\ \text{kHz}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*As part of the PBL project in Analog Electronics, your team has been assigned to **design a 1 kHz Wien Bridge sine wave oscillator with amplitude stabilization using a JFET**. Answer the following:*

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a Wien Bridge oscillator using an op-amp and JFET amplitude stabilization. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and calculate the values of $R$ and $C$ for $f_o = 1$ kHz. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of the JFET in stabilizing the amplitude. What happens if the gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ is set exactly equal to 3? (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Circuit & Frequency Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Draw the standard Wien Bridge oscillator with a TL082 op-amp. The non-inverting input receives the Wien network feedback. The inverting input has $R_i$ to ground and $R_f$ in the feedback path, where $R_f$ is replaced by a **JFET in the triode/ohmic region** acting as a voltage-controlled resistor (VCR) in series with a fixed resistor.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Derive the frequency. As derived in Section 3.1 above, the Wien network transfer function $H(j\omega)$ has zero phase and magnitude $1/3$ when $\omega RC = 1$. Hence:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Design for $f_o = 1$ kHz. Choosing a standard capacitor value $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F} = 10$ nF:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R = 15915.5\ \Omega \approx 15.9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Use a **15 k$\Omega$ fixed resistor in series with a 2 k$\Omega$ potentiometer** for fine adjustment.

#### **Part (b) — JFET Role and Gain Considerations (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** The JFET in the feedback path acts as a **voltage-dependent resistor (VCR)**. As the output amplitude grows, the rectified DC across the JFET increases, driving the JFET deeper into the **pinch-off region** (for an n-channel JFET, the gate becomes more negative). This increases the drain-source resistance $r_{ds}$, which increases the effective $R_f$, which **reduces the closed-loop gain** $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$. The system reaches a stable equilibrium when $A = 3$ exactly, and amplitude ceases to grow.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** If $A$ is set *exactly* equal to 3 (i.e., $R_f = 2 R_i$), the circuit is on the knife-edge of the Barkhausen magnitude condition. Any small perturbation — temperature drift, supply variation, noise — will push $A\beta$ either above 1 (amplitude runaway → clipping) or below 1 (oscillation dies). The JFET prevents this by providing **automatic gain control (AGC)** that keeps $A\beta$ locked at unity.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** A practical JFET choice is the **BF245C** or **2N3819** with the source tied to the op-amp inverting input, drain to $R_f$, and a small RC low-pass filter (10 k$\Omega$ + 100 $\mu$F electrolytic) on the gate to extract the rectified amplitude control voltage.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*For your PBL project, you have chosen to design a **5 W Class AB audio amplifier using a complementary push-pull output stage with TIP31 and TIP32 transistors**. Answer the following:*

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a Class AB push-pull amplifier with proper biasing to eliminate crossover distortion. Derive the expression for the maximum theoretical efficiency and state its numerical value. (7 marks)**

**(b) Design a heat sink for the output transistors assuming each transistor dissipates $P_D = 2.5$ W maximum, ambient temperature $T_A = 40$°C, and maximum junction temperature $T_{J,max} = 150$°C. Given $\theta_{JC} = 1.5$ °C/W and $\theta_{CS} = 0.5$ °C/W, calculate the required thermal resistance of the heat sink. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Circuit & Efficiency (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Draw the Class AB circuit with:
- A driver transistor $Q_1$ (BC547, NPN) at the input, with its base receiving the audio signal through a coupling capacitor.
- A complementary pair $Q_2$ (TIP31, NPN) and $Q_3$ (TIP32, PNP) in push-pull at the output.
- **Two series silicon diodes** (1N4148) between the bases of $Q_2$ and $Q_3$, forward-biased by a small current through $R_{bias}$ from the driver collector. This creates a voltage drop of $\approx 1.2$ V, which is exactly $2 V_{BE}$, keeping the output transistors *just barely* ON at quiescence — this is the **Class AB bias trick** that eliminates crossover distortion.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Derive maximum efficiency. As shown in Section 3.2:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi I_{peak} R_L}{4 V_{CC}}$$

Maximum efficiency occurs when $V_{peak} = V_{CC}$ (full output swing):

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\%$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The numerical answer is **78.5%**. Note that this is the theoretical maximum for *ideal* Class B; practical Class AB with the bias network loaded by $R_{bias}$ typically achieves 60–70% in real hardware.

#### **Part (b) — Heat Sink Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall the thermal resistance chain (analogous to Ohm's law for heat):

$$T_J = T_A + P_D (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Rearranging for the required heat-sink thermal resistance $\theta_{SA}$:

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Substitute the given values:

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{150 - 40}{2.5} - 1.5 - 0.5 = \frac{110}{2.5} - 2.0$$

$$\theta_{SA} = 44 - 2.0 = 42\ \text{°C/W}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** A standard extruded aluminum heat sink rated at $\theta_{SA} \leq 42$ °C/W must be selected. A typical **TO-220 clip-on heat sink** (e.g., Fischer Elektronik SK 75/37.5) has $\theta_{SA} \approx 21$ °C/W naturally, which gives ample thermal margin and keeps the junction temperature around **$T_J = 40 + 2.5(1.5 + 0.5 + 21) = 40 + 57.5 = 97.5$ °C** — well below the 150 °C limit with a **52.5 °C safety margin**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Heat Sink Calculation:**
> - **Common error 1 (–2 marks):** Students forget to subtract $\theta_{JC}$ and $\theta_{CS}$, and they quote the *total* thermal resistance from junction to ambient as 44 °C/W. This is wrong — the heat sink only handles the *junction-to-sink-to-ambient* portion.
> - **Common error 2 (–1 mark):** Confusing units. Thermal resistance is always in **°C/W**, never in W/°C.
> - **Common error 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the mica insulator or thermal compound between transistor and heat sink. The $\theta_{CS}$ value of 0.5 °C/W assumes a thermal pad is used; without it, this can balloon to 5 °C/W and cause thermal runaway.
> - **Common error 4 (–2 marks):** Using $T_J = 125$ °C (some older datasheets) instead of 150 °C. The KTU exam typically assumes the **150 °C** modern limit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Stick on Your Wall.**

- ✅ **PBL Definition:** A 20-mark continuous assessment in PBEET304 Module 4 involving a working analog circuit prototype.
- ✅ **Four Pillars of PBL:** Driving Question, Inquiry, Authentic Artifact, Public Demonstration.
- ✅ **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$. Both must be satisfied.
- ✅ **Wien Bridge Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ with required gain $A = 3$.
- ✅ **RC Phase Shift Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ with three RC sections.
- ✅ **Hartley Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ where $L_T = L_1 + L_2$.
- ✅ **Colpitts Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.
- ✅ **Class A Max Efficiency:** **25%** (transformer-coupled).
- ✅ **Class B Max Efficiency:** **78.5%** ($\pi/4$).
- ✅ **Class AB:** Solves crossover distortion by biasing transistors just above cutoff.
- ✅ **Crossover distortion dead-zone:** $\approx 2 V_{BE} \approx 1.2$ V.
- ✅ **555 Astable Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$.
- ✅ **555 Monostable Pulse Width:** $T = 1.1 R_A C$.
- ✅ **LM317 Output:** $V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + R_2/R_1\right)$ (ignore $I_{adj}$).
- ✅ **Heat sink formula:** $\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$.
- ✅ **Slew rate limit:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_{peak}}$ — beyond this, triangle distortion.
- ✅ **THD formula:** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \ldots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **JFET as VCR:** Used for amplitude stabilization in Wien Bridge oscillators.
- ✅ **Indian mains:** $V_{RMS} = 230$ V at $f = 50$ Hz.
- ✅ **Boltzmann constant $k_B$:** $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage $V_T$ at 300 K:** $25.85$ mV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Advice for the PBL Viva:** The examiner will almost certainly ask one of the following — be prepared with crisp 30-second answers:
> 1. *"Why this topology and not another?"* — Have a **trade-off table** memorized.
> 2. *"What is your THD, and how did you measure it?"* — Know the FFT menu on your oscilloscope.
> 3. *"How will you improve your design?"* — Mention Class D switching efficiency, or active load regulation.
> 4. *"What is the cost of your BOM?"* — Always tabulate Bill of Materials with prices.
> 5. *"Show me the simulated vs. measured waveform."* — Have a **side-by-side screenshot** in your report.
